topics.title: Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET502 - PROCESS DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Process Dynamics and Control**, a **Resistance (R)** is a system element that offers opposition to the flow of a process variable (such as mass, energy, or momentum) between two points. It is formally defined as the ratio of the change in **driving force (potential)** across the element to the resulting change in the **flow rate (throughput)** through it.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$R = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta F}$$

Where:
- $\Delta P$ = Change in potential (driving force) — e.g., pressure drop ($\Delta p$), temperature difference ($\Delta T$), voltage difference ($\Delta V$)
- $\Delta F$ = Change in flow rate — e.g., mass flow rate ($\Delta m$), volumetric flow rate ($\Delta Q$), current ($\Delta I$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1, the concept of Resistance is introduced as one of the three fundamental elements of any physical process — **Resistance (R), Capacitance (C), and Inductance (L)** — alongside the concepts of **process variables, dynamic, steady state, and self-regulation.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Resistance in KTU 2024 Scheme:** Resistance is a *static* element. It does not store energy or material. It only dissipates or transforms the driving potential into flow. There is no accumulation of mass or energy *within* a pure resistance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are watering your garden using a **tap connected to a pipe**. When you open the tap slightly, a small amount of water flows. To get *more* water flow, you must open the tap further — that is, you must apply a **greater pressure difference** (driving force) across the valve.

- If the valve is **partially closed (high resistance)** → you need a *large* pressure difference to push even a small amount of water.
- If the valve is **fully open (low resistance)** → a small pressure difference can push a *large* flow.

The valve behaves exactly like an **electrical resistor**: it is a passive element that opposes flow and obeys a linear (or non-linear) relationship between pressure drop and flow rate — analogous to **Ohm's Law** ($V = IR$).

In the same way, a **finned heat exchanger wall** resists heat flow, a **long narrow pipe** resists fluid flow, and a **control valve** is the most important *adjustable resistance* in any chemical plant.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hack for KTU Exam:** Think of Resistance as the **"Friction of the process world"** — it always tries to *dissipate* the driving force, never store it.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **SI Unit of Resistance (Fluid Flow):** $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s/m}^3$ (or $\text{J·s/kg}$)
- **SI Unit of Resistance (Thermal):** $\text{K/W}$ (Kelvin per Watt)
- **SI Unit of Resistance (Electrical):** $\Omega$ (Ohm)
- **Inherent Property:** Resistance depends on the geometry and physical nature of the element, *not* on the amount of material stored inside it.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

A **Resistance element** in a process has the following defining characteristics:

1. **Opposition to Flow:** It restricts the movement of mass, energy, or momentum from one point to another.
2. **Energy Dissipation:** The driving potential is irreversibly converted into heat (friction) or other forms — no accumulation occurs.
3. **Static Relationship:** The relation between potential and flow is *instantaneous* — there is **no time delay** between applying the potential and the flow response. (This is critical for distinguishing Resistance from Capacitance/Inductance.)
4. **Single-Point (Lumped) Element:** Resistance is always treated as a *lumped* or *point* element in process modeling — it has a single value of potential and flow at any given instant.

### 2.2 Generalized Mathematical Form of Resistance

The generalized resistance relation is given by:

$$R = \frac{dP}{dF} = \frac{\text{change in driving potential}}{\text{change in flow rate}}$$

For a **linear (ohmic) resistance**:

$$\Delta P = R \cdot \Delta F$$

For a **non-linear resistance** (the more general, real-world case), the resistance may be a function of flow itself:

$$R(F) = \frac{dP}{dF}\bigg\vert_{F_0}$$

This is the **local slope** of the P–F curve at the operating point $F_0$, which is a key concept for **linearization** of non-linear process elements.

### 2.3 Classification of Resistance

Resistances are broadly classified based on the process variable they affect:

| **Domain** | **Driving Potential (P)** | **Flow (F)** | **Resistance Definition** | **Common Engineering Example** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fluid Flow** | Pressure difference ($\Delta p$) | Volumetric flow rate ($Q$) | $R = \Delta p / Q$ | Control valve, pipe, orifice |
| **Mass Transfer** | Concentration difference ($\Delta C$) | Mass transfer rate ($\dot{m}$) | $R_m = \Delta C / \dot{m}$ | Membrane, diffusion barrier |
| **Heat Transfer** | Temperature difference ($\Delta T$) | Heat transfer rate ($q$) | $R_{th} = \Delta T / q$ | Heat exchanger wall, insulation |
| **Electrical** | Voltage difference ($\Delta V$) | Current ($I$) | $R_e = \Delta V / I$ | Resistor, heater coil |

### 2.4 Linear vs. Non-Linear Resistance

#### (a) Linear Resistance

In a **linear resistance**, the relationship between potential and flow is a straight line through the origin:

$$F = \frac{P}{R} \quad \text{or} \quad P = R \cdot F$$

**Examples:** Flow through a Venturi meter in laminar regime, electrical resistor, thermal conduction through a thin wall.

#### (b) Non-Linear Resistance

In practice, most process resistances are **non-linear**. For turbulent fluid flow through a control valve, for instance:

$$Q = C_v \sqrt{\frac{\Delta p}{\rho}}$$

Where:
- $C_v$ = Valve flow coefficient
- $\rho$ = Fluid density

Rearranging:

$$\Delta p = \frac{\rho}{C_v^2} \cdot Q^2$$

This is a **quadratic** relationship. The *local* resistance at operating point $Q_0$ is:

$$R(Q_0) = \frac{d(\Delta p)}{dQ}\bigg\vert_{Q_0} = \frac{2\rho Q_0}{C_v^2}$$

This linearization technique is heavily tested in KTU Module 1 problems.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for quick revision before the KTU University Exam.**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Domain** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \Delta P / \Delta F$ | Generalized resistance definition | All | Domain-specific |
| 2 | $\Delta P = R \cdot F$ | Linear resistance equation | All | — |
| 3 | $R(F_0) = dP/dF \vert_{F_0}$ | Local (linearized) resistance | Non-linear | — |
| 4 | $Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta p / \rho}$ | Valve flow equation | Fluid | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 5 | $\Delta p = R_h \cdot Q^2$ | Non-linear flow resistance | Fluid | Pa |
| 6 | $R_h = 2\rho Q_0 / C_v^2$ | Hydraulic resistance linearized | Fluid | $\text{Pa·s/m}^3$ |
| 7 | $q = UA \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat transfer (conduction) | Thermal | W |
| 8 | $R_{th} = 1 / (UA)$ | Thermal resistance | Thermal | K/W |
| 9 | $V = I R$ | Ohm's law analogy | Electrical | V |
| 10 | $\tau = RC$ | Time constant of R-C system | Combined | s |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Resistance elements are foundational in:

- **Chemical Process Plants:** Control valves are the most manipulated resistance in any feedback control loop (e.g., flow control, level control, pressure control).
- **HVAC Systems:** Duct resistances determine fan power requirements.
- **Instrumentation:** Orifice plates and Venturi meters act as calibrated flow resistances.
- **Heat Exchangers:** Thermal resistance determines the heat duty required for a given temperature change.
- **Bioprocess & Pharma:** Membrane resistances govern filtration flux rates.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In a feedback control loop, the **control valve** is almost always treated as a *linearized* resistance around the operating point. Marks are awarded for correctly identifying the operating point and computing the local slope.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linearization of a Non-Linear Valve Resistance

**Problem Setup:**
A control valve has the following non-linear flow-pressure relationship:

$$Q = K \sqrt{\Delta p}$$

Where $K$ is a constant having units of $\text{m}^3 / (\text{s} \cdot \text{Pa}^{1/2})$.
Derive an expression for the **linearized hydraulic resistance** $R_h$ at an operating point $(\Delta p_0, Q_0)$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Start with the given non-linear equation relating flow to pressure drop.

$$Q = K \sqrt{\Delta p}$$

**Step 2:** The non-linear resistance function is defined as the ratio of pressure drop to flow rate at any point.

$$R_h(\Delta p) = \frac{\Delta p}{Q} = \frac{\Delta p}{K \sqrt{\Delta p}} = \frac{\sqrt{\Delta p}}{K}$$

**Step 3:** For a *linear* process model, we need the **local slope** of the $\Delta p$ versus $Q$ curve. This is the linearized resistance.

$$R_h = \frac{d(\Delta p)}{dQ}$$

**Step 4:** To find $d(\Delta p)/dQ$, we can either differentiate directly or invert the relation. Let's square the original equation and solve for $\Delta p$.

$$Q^2 = K^2 \Delta p$$

$$\Delta p = \frac{Q^2}{K^2}$$

**Step 5:** Now differentiate $\Delta p$ with respect to $Q$ to find the local linearized resistance.

$$\frac{d(\Delta p)}{dQ} = \frac{d}{dQ} \left( \frac{Q^2}{K^2} \right) = \frac{2Q}{K^2}$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate this derivative at the steady-state operating point $Q = Q_0$.

$$R_h = \frac{2Q_0}{K^2}$$

**Step 7:** Express the linearized incremental model about the operating point $(\Delta p_0, Q_0)$.

$$\Delta p - \Delta p_0 = R_h (Q - Q_0)$$

$$\Delta p = R_h \cdot Q + (\Delta p_0 - R_h Q_0)$$

**Step 8:** The constant offset term $(\Delta p_0 - R_h Q_0)$ is typically absorbed into a new constant $C$, giving the final linearized model.

$$\Delta p = R_h \cdot Q + C$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The *incremental* form is what's tested in university exams. Always write the model in **deviation variables** ($p' = p - p_0$, $Q' = Q - Q_0$) when the question asks for a "linearized dynamic model."

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Series and Parallel Combination of Resistances

**Problem Setup:**
Three hydraulic resistances $R_1$, $R_2$, and $R_3$ are arranged (a) in series and (b) in parallel. The total flow is $Q$ and the total pressure drop is $\Delta p$. Derive the **equivalent resistance** for both cases.

#### (a) Series Combination

**Step 1:** In series, the **same flow $Q$** passes through each resistance, and the total pressure drop is the **sum** of individual drops.

$$\Delta p_{total} = \Delta p_1 + \Delta p_2 + \Delta p_3$$

**Step 2:** Apply the linear resistance relation $\Delta p_i = R_i Q$ to each element.

$$\Delta p_{total} = R_1 Q + R_2 Q + R_3 Q$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $Q$.

$$\Delta p_{total} = (R_1 + R_2 + R_3) \cdot Q$$

**Step 4:** The equivalent series resistance is therefore:

$$R_{eq,series} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3$$

$$\boxed{R_{eq,series} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i}$$

#### (b) Parallel Combination

**Step 1:** In parallel, the **same pressure drop $\Delta p$** is experienced across each branch, and the total flow is the **sum** of individual branch flows.

$$Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3$$

**Step 2:** Apply the linear resistance relation $Q_i = \Delta p / R_i$ to each element.

$$Q_{total} = \frac{\Delta p}{R_1} + \frac{\Delta p}{R_2} + \frac{\Delta p}{R_3}$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $\Delta p$.

$$Q_{total} = \Delta p \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3} \right)$$

**Step 4:** The equivalent parallel resistance is therefore:

$$\frac{1}{R_{eq,parallel}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{R_{eq,parallel}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{R_i}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hack:** Resistances combine like *electrical resistors* — series adds directly, parallel adds reciprocally. This is because the linear resistance relation $\Delta p = R F$ is mathematically identical to Ohm's law $V = IR$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resistances in a Liquid Level System

**Problem Setup:**
Consider a tank with a constant density liquid being drained through a linear resistance $R$ (a valve) at the bottom. The inlet flow rate is $F_i$ and outlet flow rate is $F_o$. The level is $h$ and the cross-sectional area is $A$. Show how the resistance $R$ enters the dynamic model of the tank.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The pressure at the bottom of the tank (the driving force for outflow) is hydrostatic.

$$p = \rho g h$$

**Step 2:** The outflow through the linear resistance is given by:

$$F_o = \frac{\Delta p}{R} = \frac{\rho g h}{R}$$

**Step 3:** The mass balance on the tank (capacitance) gives:

$$A \frac{dh}{dt} = F_i - F_o = F_i - \frac{\rho g h}{R}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and rearrange to put the equation in standard first-order form.

$$A \frac{dh}{dt} + \frac{\rho g}{R} h = F_i$$

**Step 5:** The time constant of the system is:

$$\tau = \frac{AR}{\rho g}$$

**Step 6:** The steady-state gain is:

$$K_p = \frac{R}{\rho g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Notice that the resistance $R$ appears in *both* the time constant $\tau$ and the process gain $K_p$ of a non-interacting level system. Increasing $R$ (closing the valve) makes the system *slower* and *more sensitive* — a key intuitive result.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Aspect** | **Resistance (R)** | **Capacitance (C)** | **Inductance (L)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Physical Role** | Opposes flow | Stores mass/energy | Stores momentum |
| **Mathematical Form** | $\Delta P = R \cdot F$ | $\Delta V = C \cdot \Delta P$ | $\Delta P = L \cdot dF/dt$ |
| **Has Time Dependence?** | No (static) | Yes (dynamic) | Yes (dynamic) |
| **Energy Storage?** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Analogy (Electrical)** | Resistor | Capacitor | Inductor |
| **Example** | Control valve | Tank | Fluid inertia in pipe |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Resistance Element in a Process Loop

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a Resistance element is represented in a standard process control block diagram, showing its role as the *plant* or *manipulated element* in a feedback loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ctrl["Controller Output"] --> cv["Control Valve"]
    cv -- R = dP/dF --> process["Process / Plant"]
    process --> sensor["Measurement Sensor"]
    sensor --> fb["Feedback Signal"]
    fb --> ctrl
    style ctrl fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style cv fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style process fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style sensor fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style fb fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Controller Output** is a signal (e.g., 4–20 mA) sent to the **Control Valve**, which *is* the variable resistance in the process.
- The valve imposes a **pressure-flow relationship** characterized by the resistance $R$.
- The process responds, is measured by the sensor, and fed back to the controller.

---

### 4.2 Schematic — Resistance in a Tank-With-Valve System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TankSystem["Liquid Storage Tank"]
        inflow["Inlet Flow F_i"] --> tank["Tank: Capacitance C = A"]
        tank --> valve["Outlet Valve: Resistance R"]
    end
    valve -- F_o = h/R_h --> out["Drain"]
    h["Liquid Level h"] -.drives.-> valve
    style TankSystem fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style tank fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style valve fill:#FF8A65,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style inflow fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style out fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style h fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **tank** is the *capacitance* (stores liquid).
- The **valve** is the *resistance* (controls outflow based on hydrostatic head).
- The liquid level $h$ acts as the *driving potential* (analogous to voltage in Ohm's law).
- This is the classic **non-interacting level system** that KTU tests frequently.

---

### 4.3 Functional Flow — How Resistance Acts in Series & Parallel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Series["SERIES COMBINATION"]
        sP["Total Pressure Drop ΔP"] --> sR1["Resistance R1"]
        sR1 --> sR2["Resistance R2"]
        sR2 --> sR3["Resistance R3"]
        sR3 --> sQ["Common Flow Q (same through all)"]
    end
    subgraph Parallel["PARALLEL COMBINATION"]
        pP["Common Pressure Drop ΔP"] --> pR1["Branch 1: R1"]
        pP --> pR2["Branch 2: R2"]
        pP --> pR3["Branch 3: R3"]
        pR1 --> pQ["Total Flow Q = Q1+Q2+Q3"]
        pR2 --> pQ
        pR3 --> pQ
    end
    style sP fill:#FFB74D,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style sQ fill:#FFB74D,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style sR1 fill:#FFF176,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style sR2 fill:#FFF176,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style sR3 fill:#FFF176,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style pP fill:#81D4FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style pQ fill:#81D4FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style pR1 fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style pR2 fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style pR3 fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Series:** Same flow passes through all resistances → individual pressure drops add up.
- **Parallel:** Same pressure drop across all resistances → individual branch flows add up.

---

### 4.4 Mapping Resistance to Electrical Analogy (Ohm's Law)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fluid["ΔP (Pressure Drop)"] -->|"F = ΔP / R"| valve["R (Fluid Resistance)"]
    valve --> flow["F (Flow Rate)"]
    volt["V (Voltage)"] -->|"I = V / R"| res["R (Electrical Resistance)"]
    res --> curr["I (Current)"]
    style fluid fill:#FFCC80,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style valve fill:#FF8A65,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style flow fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style volt fill:#FFCC80,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style res fill:#FF8A65,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style curr fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

This is the **hydraulic-electrical analogy** that KTU examiners love to test. The math is identical; only the variable names differ.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

---

**Q1.** Define the term **"Resistance"** as applied to a process. Give **two** physical examples where resistance plays a key role, and state the generalized mathematical definition.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Resistance is a process element that offers opposition to the flow of mass, energy, or momentum between two points. It is the ratio of the change in driving potential to the change in flow rate.

$$R = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta F}$$

**Examples (any two):**
- A control valve resisting fluid flow in a chemical process line.
- A pipe wall resisting heat transfer in a furnace.
- A membrane resisting mass transfer in a separation process.
- An electrical heating coil resisting current.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1M) + Formula (1M) + Examples (1M).

---

**Q2.** A tank is filled with a liquid of density $\rho$ and is drained through a valve at the bottom. The hydrostatic head provides the driving pressure. If the valve has a linear flow characteristic given by $F_o = \frac{\rho g h}{R}$, where $R$ is the hydraulic resistance, derive the **time constant** of the level system in terms of $R$, tank area $A$, $\rho$, and $g$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Mass balance on the tank:

$$A \frac{dh}{dt} = F_i - F_o = F_i - \frac{\rho g h}{R}$$

Rearranging into standard first-order form $R C \frac{dh}{dt} + h = K F_i$:

$$\underbrace{\left(\frac{AR}{\rho g}\right)}_{\tau} \frac{dh}{dt} + h = \underbrace{\left(\frac{R}{\rho g}\right)}_{K} F_i$$

Therefore, the time constant is:

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{AR}{\rho g}}$$

**Mark Distribution:** Mass balance (1M) + Rearrangement (1M) + Final time constant (1M).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style | End Semester Evaluation]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the **linearized resistance** for a control valve whose non-linear characteristic is given by:

$$Q = K \sqrt{\Delta p}$$

where $Q$ is volumetric flow, $\Delta p$ is the pressure drop across the valve, and $K$ is a constant. **Express the result in terms of deviation variables.** [7 Marks]

**(b)** A process has three hydraulic resistances $R_1 = 2$, $R_2 = 4$, and $R_3 = 6$ (all in consistent units) connected in **series-parallel** as follows: $R_1$ is in series with the parallel combination of $R_2$ and $R_3$. Calculate the **equivalent resistance**. If a total pressure drop of $\Delta p = 48$ Pa is applied, calculate the **flow through each resistance**. [7 Marks]

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Square the original non-linear equation to obtain $\Delta p$ explicitly as a function of $Q$.

$$Q = K \sqrt{\Delta p} \implies Q^2 = K^2 \Delta p$$

$$\Delta p = \frac{Q^2}{K^2}$$

**Step 2:** The linearized resistance is the local slope of the $\Delta p$–$Q$ curve at the steady-state operating point $Q_0$.

$$R_{linearized} = \frac{d(\Delta p)}{dQ} \bigg\vert_{Q=Q_0} = \frac{d}{dQ}\left(\frac{Q^2}{K^2}\right)\bigg\vert_{Q_0} = \frac{2Q_0}{K^2}$$

**Step 3:** Define the deviation variables from the operating point $(\Delta p_0, Q_0)$.

$$Q' = Q - Q_0, \quad \Delta p' = \Delta p - \Delta p_0$$

**Step 4:** Apply first-order Taylor expansion (linearization) about the operating point.

$$\Delta p \approx \Delta p_0 + \left.\frac{d(\Delta p)}{dQ}\right\vert_{Q_0} \cdot (Q - Q_0)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the expression for the slope.

$$\Delta p = \Delta p_0 + \frac{2Q_0}{K^2} (Q - Q_0)$$

**Step 6:** In deviation variable form (subtracting the steady state $\Delta p_0 = Q_0^2 / K^2$):

$$\Delta p' = \frac{2Q_0}{K^2} \cdot Q'$$

**Step 7:** Final linearized resistance:

$$\boxed{R_{linearized} = \frac{2Q_0}{K^2} = \frac{2\Delta p_0}{Q_0}}$$

[7 Marks: 2M for derivation of slope, 2M for linearization expansion, 2M for deviation variable form, 1M for final boxed result]

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the parallel combination of $R_2$ and $R_3$.

$$\frac{1}{R_{23}} = \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{3 + 2}{12} = \frac{5}{12}$$

$$R_{23} = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4$$

**Step 2:** Compute the series combination of $R_1$ and $R_{23}$.

$$R_{eq} = R_1 + R_{23} = 2 + 2.4 = 4.4$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the total flow $Q_{total}$ using the applied pressure drop.

$$Q_{total} = \frac{\Delta p}{R_{eq}} = \frac{48}{4.4} = 10.909 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4:** In series, the same flow passes through $R_1$, so:

$$Q_1 = Q_{total} = 10.909 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Pressure drop across $R_1$:

$$\Delta p_1 = R_1 \cdot Q_1 = 2 \times 10.909 = 21.818 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 5:** In parallel, $R_2$ and $R_3$ experience the same pressure drop:

$$\Delta p_2 = \Delta p_3 = \Delta p - \Delta p_1 = 48 - 21.818 = 26.182 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 6:** Flow through each parallel branch:

$$Q_2 = \frac{\Delta p_2}{R_2} = \frac{26.182}{4} = 6.545 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_3 = \frac{\Delta p_3}{R_3} = \frac{26.182}{6} = 4.364 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 7:** Verification: $Q_2 + Q_3 = 6.545 + 4.364 = 10.909 = Q_{total}$ ✓

[7 Marks: 2M for parallel reduction, 1M for series equivalent, 1M for total flow, 2M for individual branch flows, 1M for verification]

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat schematic**, explain the role of a control valve as a variable resistance in a feedback control loop. Show how it enters the **block diagram** of the loop and identify the manipulated variable (MV), controlled variable (CV), and disturbance variable (DV). [7 Marks]

**(b)** For a non-interacting tank system, the dynamic equation is:

$$A \frac{dh}{dt} + \frac{\rho g}{R} h = F_i$$

where $h$ is the liquid level, $A$ is the tank cross-sectional area, $\rho$ is density, $g$ is gravity, $R$ is the outlet resistance, and $F_i$ is the inlet flow. **Find:**
- (i) The time constant $\tau$ and steady-state gain $K_p$ [3 Marks]
- (ii) The transfer function $H(s)/F_i(s)$ in deviation variables [2 Marks]
- (iii) The new time constant if the resistance is **doubled** [2 Marks]

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) Solution:**

A control valve is a **variable resistance** whose opening is adjusted by the controller output signal. When the valve stem moves, the flow area changes, which directly alters the resistance $R$ seen by the flowing fluid. The relationship between the pressure drop $\Delta p$ and flow $Q$ through the valve is given by:

$$Q = f(\Delta p, u)$$

where $u$ is the controller signal. For a linearized valve:

$$Q' = K_v u' - \frac{1}{R_v} \Delta p'$$

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP["Setpoint r"] --> sumJ["Summing Junction"]
    sumJ --> C["Controller C(s)"]
    C -->|"MV u (controller signal)"| V["Control Valve = Variable R"]
    V -->|"Flow Q"| P["Process Gp(s)"]
    P -->|"CV h"| H["Sensor H(s)"]
    H --> sumJ
    style SP fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style sumJ fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#81D4FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style V fill:#FF8A65,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Identification:**
- **MV (Manipulated Variable):** Valve stem position / controller signal $u$
- **CV (Controlled Variable):** Process output $h$ (e.g., level, temperature, pressure)
- **DV (Disturbance Variable):** Inlet flow / load changes $F_i$

[7 Marks: 2M for explanation, 2M for block diagram, 3M for MV/CV/DV identification]

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step (i):** Comparing the given equation $A \frac{dh}{dt} + \frac{\rho g}{R} h = F_i$ to the standard first-order form:

$$\tau \frac{dh}{dt} + h = K_p F_i$$

Dividing throughout by $\frac{\rho g}{R}$:

$$\frac{AR}{\rho g} \frac{dh}{dt} + h = \frac{R}{\rho g} F_i$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{AR}{\rho g} \quad \text{and} \quad K_p = \frac{R}{\rho g}}$$

[3 Marks: 2M for time constant, 1M for steady-state gain]

---

**Step (ii):** Taking the Laplace transform in deviation variables (zero initial conditions):

$$A s H(s) + \frac{\rho g}{R} H(s) = F_i(s)$$

$$\left(A s + \frac{\rho g}{R}\right) H(s) = F_i(s)$$

$$\frac{H(s)}{F_i(s)} = \frac{1}{A s + \frac{\rho g}{R}} = \frac{R/\rho g}{\frac{AR}{\rho g} s + 1}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{H(s)}{F_i(s)} = \frac{K_p}{\tau s + 1} = \frac{R/\rho g}{(AR/\rho g) s + 1}}$$

[2 Marks: 1M for Laplace transform, 1M for final transfer function]

---

**Step (iii):** If the resistance is doubled, $R_{new} = 2R$:

$$\tau_{new} = \frac{A (2R)}{\rho g} = 2\tau$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{new} = 2\tau = \frac{2AR}{\rho g}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Doubling the resistance (closing the valve halfway) makes the system **twice as slow** to reach steady state. This is because the tank takes longer to drain.

[2 Marks: 1M for calculation, 1M for physical interpretation]

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Resistance with Capacitance:** A resistance is *static* (no time dependence). If you see $d/dt$ in the relation, it's NOT a pure resistance — it's either an inductance or relates to a capacitance.
> 2. **Forgetting to Linearize Non-Linear Resistances:** The valve equation $Q = K\sqrt{\Delta p}$ is non-linear. A common error is to assume $R = \Delta p / Q = \sqrt{\Delta p}/K$ (which is the *chord slope*). The KTU-correct approach is the **local tangent slope** $R = 2Q_0/K^2$.
> 3. **Mixing Up Series and Parallel Formulas:** Students often write $R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3$ for parallel. The correct formula for parallel is $\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \sum \frac{1}{R_i}$.
> 4. **Not Using Deviation Variables:** In linearization problems, KTU examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks if the final answer is not expressed in deviation variables $X' = X - X_0$.
> 5. **Ignoring Units:** Always write the units of resistance. For fluid systems, $R$ is in $\text{Pa·s/m}^3$; for thermal systems, $R$ is in $\text{K/W}$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This Before Your Exam!**

✅ **Definition:** Resistance $R$ is the ratio of change in driving potential to change in flow rate: $R = \Delta P / \Delta F$.

✅ **Static Element:** A pure resistance has *no* energy/mass storage. It dissipates potential.

✅ **Generalized Form:** $R$ can be defined for fluid flow, heat transfer, mass transfer, and electrical domains.

✅ **Linear Resistance:** $F = P / R$ (straight-line P–F relation).

✅ **Non-Linear Resistance (e.g., Valve):** $Q = K\sqrt{\Delta p}$ — must be **linearized** using the tangent slope at the operating point.

✅ **Linearized Resistance Formula:** $R_{lin} = dP/dF \vert_{F_0} = 2Q_0 / K^2 = 2\Delta p_0 / Q_0$.

✅ **Series Combination:** $R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_n$ (same flow).

✅ **Parallel Combination:** $\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{R_n}$ (same pressure drop).

✅ **Role in Dynamic Models:** Resistance $R$ appears in the *time constant* $\tau = RC$ of first-order systems.

✅ **Analogy to Electrical:** $R$ in fluid = Resistor in electrical, $\Delta p$ = Voltage, $Q$ = Current.

✅ **KTU Hot Spot:** Linearization of a control valve's $Q$–$\Delta p$ characteristic is a guaranteed exam question.

✅ **Key Insight:** Increasing $R$ makes a non-interacting level system *slower* and *more sensitive* (higher gain).

---

**End of Module 1 — Resistance Notes**
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, PCAET502 — Process Dynamics and Control*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacitance and Combination of Both
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET502 - PROCESS DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Capacitance and Combination of Both

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Process Capacitance

In the rigorous framework of **Process Dynamics and Control**, **Capacitance** is defined as the quantitative measure of the inherent ability of a physical system to **store energy** (in the form of heat, electrical charge, mechanical energy) or **store material** (mass, volume of fluid).

Mathematically, the capacitance $C$ of a process element is the proportionality constant that relates the **quantity of material or energy stored** ($M$) to the **potential variable** (intensive property, $X$) at which the storage occurs:

$$C = \frac{dM}{dX}$$

Where:
- $M$ = Total mass, energy, or quantity stored in the system
- $X$ = The intensive variable (Temperature, Pressure, Voltage, Concentration, etc.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Capacitance is a **lumped parameter** that captures storage. Without capacitance, a process would respond instantaneously (algebraic relation). The presence of capacitance introduces a **memory effect** leading to **differential equations** that govern dynamic behavior.

## 1.2 Types of Process Capacitances (KTU 2024 Classification)

| Capacitance Type | Storage Quantity ($M$) | Potential ($X$) | Defining Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermal Capacitance** | Enthalpy ($H$) | Temperature ($T$) | $C = MC_p$ |
| **Electrical Capacitance** | Charge ($Q$) | Voltage ($V$) | $C = Q/V$ |
| **Gas/Fluid Capacitance** | Mass of fluid ($m$) | Pressure ($P$) | $C = V/R_g T$ |
| **Liquid Capacitance** | Volume of liquid ($V$) | Liquid Head ($h$) | $C = A$ (Area) |
| **Mechanical Capacitance** | Momentum | Velocity | $C = M$ (Mass) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Notice that thermal capacitance involves the product of mass ($M$) and specific heat ($C_p$). This is because temperature changes depend on how much "stuff" is present and how much heat it takes to warm it up by 1 K.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Water Bucket" Intuition

Imagine you have a **bucket with a small hole at the bottom** that lets water drain out, and you're pouring water in at the top at a variable rate.

- The **Capacitance** is the **cross-sectional area of the bucket** ($C = A$). A wider bucket holds more water per unit height, so it has more capacitance.
- The **Resistance** is the **restriction to flow** through the hole. A smaller hole = higher resistance.
- The **Combination of Both (R and C)** describes the dynamic behavior: as water flows in and out, the height in the bucket doesn't change instantly — it builds up or falls gradually based on the difference between inflow and outflow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering example:** A large industrial **thermal storage tank** used in solar power plants. Its massive thermal capacitance means even if heat input drops suddenly, the temperature of the working fluid doesn't crash — it falls slowly. This is **thermal inertia** at work.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Specific Heat of Water:** $C_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_g = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}$
- **Standard Temperature (for Gas Capacitance):** $T = 273.15 \text{ K}$ (often used at STP)
- **Units of Capacitance** are system-dependent: $\text{kJ/K}$ (thermal), $\text{C/V} = \text{F}$ (Farad, electrical), $\text{kg/Pa}$ or $\text{m}^3/\text{Pa}$ (gas/fluid)

## 1.5 The "Combination of Both" — Resistance and Capacitance Together

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, **"Combination of Both"** explicitly refers to the **simultaneous presence of Resistance ($R$) and Capacitance ($C$)** in a single physical system. This combination is the fundamental building block of:

1. **First-Order Lags (First-Order Systems)** — the most common dynamic element in process control
2. **Non-Interacting (Non-Mixing) Systems** — where multiple R-C stages are isolated
3. **Interacting (Mixing) Systems** — where R-C stages share common capacitances

The combination of $R$ and $C$ introduces a **time constant** $\tau = RC$, which dictates how fast the system responds to changes in input.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Capacitive Behavior

A capacitive element in a process follows these logical steps:

- **Step 1: Identify the storage quantity** — Determine what the system is storing: energy, mass, charge, momentum, etc.
- **Step 2: Identify the potential variable** — Find the intensive property whose change will alter the stored quantity (e.g., temperature, pressure, voltage).
- **Step 3: Apply the defining relation** — Use $C = dM/dX$. For linear systems, this becomes a simple constant.
- **Step 4: Write the conservation law** — Rate of accumulation = Rate of input − Rate of output. The accumulation is $C \cdot (dX/dt)$.
- **Step 5: Combine with resistance law** — Flow rate is proportional to the driving force: $q = \Delta X / R$. Substitute into the conservation law.

**Why Capacitance Matters (The "Why" Behind It):**
- Pure resistance = instantaneous response (no memory).
- Pure capacitance = storage without dissipation (potential rises indefinitely).
- **Together** = physically realistic dynamic response that smooths disturbances and introduces time lag — exactly what we see in real industrial processes.

## 2.2 Thermal Capacitance — Detailed Derivation Setup

For a solid body of mass $M$ and specific heat $C_p$:

The enthalpy stored is:

$$H = M \cdot C_p \cdot T$$

Differentiating with respect to temperature:

$$C_{th} = \frac{dH}{dT} = M \cdot C_p$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Result:** Thermal capacitance is **constant** (linear system) for a body of fixed mass and constant specific heat. If the process is non-isothermal, $C_p$ varies with $T$, and the system becomes **nonlinear**.

## 2.3 Gas Capacitance — Detailed Derivation Setup

For an ideal gas in a rigid vessel of volume $V$ at temperature $T$:

Using ideal gas law: $PV = n R_g T$

Solving for moles: $n = \frac{PV}{R_g T}$

Mass stored: $M = n \cdot MW = \frac{PV \cdot MW}{R_g T}$

Differentiating with respect to pressure (at constant $V$ and $T$):

$$C_{gas} = \frac{dM}{dP} = \frac{V \cdot MW}{R_g T} = \frac{V}{R_g T / MW}$$

For SI units, if $R_g$ is in J/(kmol·K):

$$C_{gas} = \frac{V \cdot MW}{R_g \cdot T} \quad [\text{kg/Pa}]$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = dM/dX$ | Generic definition of capacitance | Universal storage law |
| 2 | $C_{th} = M \cdot C_p$ | Thermal capacitance | Heat exchangers, reactors |
| 3 | $C_{elec} = Q/V$ | Electrical capacitance | Control circuits, sensors |
| 4 | $C_{liq} = A$ (area) | Liquid level capacitance | Tanks, reservoirs |
| 5 | $C_{gas} = V/(R_g T/MW)$ | Gas capacitance | Pressurized vessels |
| 6 | $R = \Delta X / q$ | Resistance (flow restriction) | Valves, orifices |
| 7 | $C \frac{dX_o}{dt} = q_i - q_o$ | Material balance (lumped) | Mass conservation |
| 8 | $RC \frac{dX_o}{dt} + X_o = X_i$ | First-order ODE | R-C combined dynamics |
| 9 | $\tau = RC$ | Time constant | Speed of response |
| 10 | $K = R$ (or 1, depending on convention) | Steady-state gain | DC amplification |
| 11 | $G(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ | First-order transfer function | Frequency response |
| 12 | $X_o(t) = K(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Step response (charging) | Time-domain analysis |
| 13 | $X_o(t) = K e^{-t/\tau}$ | Step response (discharging) | Decay analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The student should use LaTeX vertical bars as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. For example, write $\vert G(s) \vert$ instead of $\vert G(s) \vert$ when typing in a table.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distillation Column Reflux Drum:** The liquid capacitance determines the time it takes for the level to respond to changes in feed — critical for control loop tuning.
- **Thermal Storage in Power Plants:** Capacitance of the molten salt tank determines how long the plant can generate electricity without sunlight.
- **Pneumatic Actuators:** Gas capacitance combined with valve resistance gives the time lag that controls actuator response.
- **Chemical Reactors:** Thermal and material capacitances together govern the runaway behavior of exothermic reactions.

## 2.6 The "Combination of Both" — Resistance and Capacitance in Series

Consider a process where input $X_i$ flows through a resistance $R$ to charge a capacitance $C$, which then outputs a level $X_o$.

The flow rate is driven by the pressure difference:

$$q = \frac{X_i - X_o}{R}$$

The accumulation in the capacitance:

$$C \frac{dX_o}{dt} = q = \frac{X_i - X_o}{R}$$

Rearranging:

$$RC \frac{dX_o}{dt} + X_o = X_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical first-order differential equation** of a non-interacting R-C system. The student must memorize this form as it is the basis for nearly every process control problem in the KTU exam.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: First-Order R-C System Response to a Step Input

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A tank with capacitance $C$ is charged by an inlet stream at flow rate $q_i$ (kg/s), and the outlet passes through a resistance $R$ (s/m²). The input variable $X_i$ is the inlet concentration, and the output variable $X_o$ is the tank concentration.

The flow rate through the resistance:

$$q = \frac{X_i - X_o}{R}$$

### 3.1.2 Material Balance (Conservation Law)

Rate of accumulation of mass in tank:

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output}$$

$$C \frac{dX_o}{dt} = q - q_o$$

For the simplified case where outflow rate equals inflow rate (constant holdup):

$$C \frac{dX_o}{dt} = \frac{X_i - X_o}{R}$$

### 3.1.3 Algebraic Manipulation

Multiplying both sides by $R$:

$$RC \frac{dX_o}{dt} = X_i - X_o$$

Rearranging to standard form (output derivative on left, output on right):

$$RC \frac{dX_o}{dt} + X_o = X_i$$

> This is the **standard first-order ODE**.

### 3.1.4 Solution for Step Input

Assume the input is a step change of magnitude $A$ applied at $t = 0$:

$$X_i(t) = A \cdot u(t)$$

where $u(t)$ is the unit step function.

The ODE becomes:

$$RC \frac{dX_o}{dt} + X_o = A$$

### 3.1.5 Solution Method: Homogeneous + Particular

**Step A: Find the homogeneous solution** (set RHS = 0):

$$RC \frac{dX_o^h}{dt} + X_o^h = 0$$

The characteristic equation is:

$$RC \cdot s + 1 = 0 \implies s = -\frac{1}{RC} = -\frac{1}{\tau}$$

Therefore:

$$X_o^h(t) = K_1 e^{-t/\tau}$$

where $\tau = RC$ is the time constant.

**Step B: Find the particular solution** (assume constant output):

Since the RHS is constant $A$, try $X_o^p = \text{constant}$:

$$0 + X_o^p = A \implies X_o^p = A$$

**Step C: Combine the two solutions:**

$$X_o(t) = K_1 e^{-t/\tau} + A$$

**Step D: Apply initial condition** $X_o(0) = 0$ (tank initially empty/concentration zero):

$$0 = K_1 e^{0} + A \implies K_1 = -A$$

**Step E: Final complete solution:**

$$\boxed{X_o(t) = A \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)}$$

### 3.1.6 Key Observations from the Solution

- At $t = 0$: $X_o(0) = A(1 - 1) = 0$ ✓
- At $t = \tau$: $X_o(\tau) = A(1 - e^{-1}) \approx 0.632 A$ → reaches **63.2% of final value**
- At $t = 2\tau$: $X_o(2\tau) \approx 0.865 A$ → **86.5%**
- At $t = 3\tau$: $X_o(3\tau) \approx 0.950 A$ → **95%**
- At $t = 4\tau$: $X_o(4\tau) \approx 0.982 A$ → **98.2%**
- At $t = 5\tau$: $X_o(5\tau) \approx 0.993 A$ → **99.3%** (engineering "steady state")

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Exams:** A first-order system is considered to reach steady state after **4 time constants**. The student should always quote this in numerical problems.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Non-Interacting vs. Interacting R-C Systems

### 3.2.1 Non-Interacting System (Two R-C Stages in Series, Isolated)

Setup: Two tanks in series, each with its own resistance and capacitance. The output of the first tank is the input to the second tank, and the capacitances do not affect each other.

**Stage 1 (Tank 1):**

$$\tau_1 \frac{dX_1}{dt} + X_1 = X_i \quad \text{where} \quad \tau_1 = R_1 C_1$$

**Stage 2 (Tank 2):**

$$\tau_2 \frac{dX_2}{dt} + X_2 = X_1 \quad \text{where} \quad \tau_2 = R_2 C_2$$

Combined transfer function (using Laplace transforms, with zero initial conditions):

$$G(s) = \frac{X_2(s)}{X_i(s)} = \frac{1}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}$$

### 3.2.2 Interacting System (Two R-C Stages Sharing a Capacitance)

Setup: Two tanks where the connecting line has a resistance, and the levels in the two tanks interact (change in one affects the other).

Let $R_1$ = resistance between tank 1 and tank 2, $R_2$ = outlet resistance of tank 2, $C_1$ and $C_2$ = tank capacitances.

**Material balance on Tank 1:**

$$C_1 \frac{dX_1}{dt} = \frac{X_i - X_1}{R_1} - \frac{X_1 - X_2}{R_2}$$

**Material balance on Tank 2:**

$$C_2 \frac{dX_2}{dt} = \frac{X_1 - X_2}{R_2}$$

> **The difference:** In the interacting case, the flow $q_{12}$ depends on **both** $X_1$ and $X_2$, creating dynamic coupling.

The transfer function in this case has the form:

$$G(s) = \frac{1}{\tau^2 s^2 + 2\zeta \tau s + 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Students often confuse these two cases. The **non-interacting** system is mathematically simpler because the transfer function is a clean product of two first-order terms. The **interacting** system has a characteristic equation that must be derived by eliminating intermediate variables.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Time-Domain Response Comparison

| Time | Non-Interacting (Two R-C) | Interacting (Two R-C) | Single R-C |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $\tau$ | $\approx 0.264 A$ | Lower than non-interacting | $0.632 A$ |
| $2\tau$ | $\approx 0.594 A$ | Lower | $0.865 A$ |
| $3\tau$ | $\approx 0.799 A$ | Lower | $0.950 A$ |
| $4\tau$ | $\approx 0.908 A$ | Lower | $0.982 A$ |
| $\infty$ | $A$ | $A$ | $A$ |

> **Key Insight:** The interacting system responds **slower** than the non-interacting system because the dynamic coupling creates an additional "resistance" to the flow of information from input to output.

## 3.4 Detailed Worked Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem:** A thermal process has a resistance of $R = 0.5 \text{ K/(kJ/min)}$ and a capacitance of $C = 200 \text{ kJ/K}$. If the inlet temperature changes by $\Delta T_i = 10 \text{ K}$ at $t = 0$, find:

(a) The time constant $\tau$.
(b) The temperature response $T_o(t)$.
(c) The time to reach 95% of the final value.

**Solution:**

**(a) Time constant:**

$$\tau = R \times C = 0.5 \times 200 = 100 \text{ minutes}$$

**(b) Temperature response:**

$$T_o(t) = T_o(\infty) \left(1 - e^{-t/100}\right)$$

For a step change of 10 K:

$$T_o(t) = 10 \left(1 - e^{-t/100}\right) \text{ K}$$

**(c) Time to reach 95%:**

Setting $T_o(t) = 0.95 \times 10 = 9.5$ K:

$$9.5 = 10 \left(1 - e^{-t/100}\right)$$

$$0.95 = 1 - e^{-t/100}$$

$$e^{-t/100} = 0.05$$

$$-t/100 = \ln(0.05) = -2.996$$

$$t = 299.6 \text{ minutes} \approx 3\tau = 300 \text{ minutes} \checkmark$$

This confirms the **4-time-constants rule** is a tight engineering approximation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams below follow KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards: alphanumeric node IDs (no reserved keywords), clean uppercase labels without markdown formatting inside node definitions, and no embedded HTML tables.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Single R-C System

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT["INPUT VARIABLE Xi"] --> RESNODE["RESISTANCE R"]
    RESNODE --> CAPNODE["CAPACITANCE C"]
    CAPNODE --> OUTPUT["OUTPUT VARIABLE Xo"]
    
    FLOW1["FLOW RATE q = (Xi - Xo) R"] -.-> RESNODE
    ACCUM["ACCUMULATION C dXo dt"] -.-> CAPNODE
    
    RESNODE -- "Driving Force Delta X" --> CAPNODE
    CAPNODE -- "Potential Variable Xo" --> OUTPUT
    
    style INPUT fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style RESNODE fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style CAPNODE fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style OUTPUT fill:#D0021B,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style FLOW1 fill:#9013FE,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style ACCUM fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** Input $X_i$ experiences a pressure drop across resistance $R$, producing a flow $q$ that accumulates in capacitance $C$, raising its potential to $X_o$.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Non-Interacting Two-Stage R-C System

```mermaid
graph LR
    INP["INPUT Xi"] --> R1["RESISTANCE R1"]
    R1 --> C1["CAPACITANCE C1"]
    C1 --> MID["INTERMEDIATE X1"]
    MID --> R2["RESISTANCE R2"]
    R2 --> C2["CAPACITANCE C2"]
    C2 --> OUTP["OUTPUT Xo"]
    
    EQ1["ODE 1: tau1 dX1 dt + X1 = Xi"] -.-> C1
    EQ2["ODE 2: tau2 dX2 dt + X2 = X1"] -.-> C2
    
    style INP fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R1 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style MID fill:#9013FE,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R2 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style OUTP fill:#D0021B,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style EQ1 fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style EQ2 fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** Two completely independent first-order systems in cascade. The output of stage 1 feeds the input of stage 2 without any feedback. The overall transfer function is the product of the individual transfer functions.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Interacting Two-Stage R-C System

```mermaid
graph LR
    INP2["INPUT Xi"] --> R1B["RESISTANCE R1"]
    R1B --> C1B["CAPACITANCE C1"]
    C1B --> MID2["INTERMEDIATE X1"]
    MID2 --> R2B["RESISTANCE R2"]
    R2B --> C2B["CAPACITANCE C2"]
    C2B --> OUTP2["OUTPUT Xo"]
    
    MID2 -. "FEEDBACK COUPLING" .-> C1B
    C2B -. "AFFECTS FLOW Q12" .-> R1B
    
    style INP2 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R1B fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C1B fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style MID2 fill:#9013FE,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R2B fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C2B fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style OUTP2 fill:#D0021B,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation:** Notice the **dashed feedback arrows** that are absent in the non-interacting case. These represent the dynamic coupling: the level in tank 2 influences the flow rate between tanks 1 and 2, which in turn affects tank 1.

## 4.5 Step Response Comparison Chart (Conceptual Mermaid Timeline)

```mermaid
graph TB
    TIMELINE["TIME AXIS"] --> T0["T EQUALS 0"]
    TIMELINE --> T1["T EQUALS TAU"]
    TIMELINE --> T2["T EQUALS 2 TAU"]
    TIMELINE --> T3["T EQUALS 3 TAU"]
    TIMELINE --> T4["T EQUALS 4 TAU"]
    TIMELINE --> TINF["T EQUALS INFINITY"]
    
    V0["63.2 PERCENT"] --> T1
    V1["86.5 PERCENT"] --> T2
    V2["95.0 PERCENT"] --> T3
    V3["98.2 PERCENT"] --> T4
    V4["100 PERCENT"] --> TINF
    
    T0 --> VAL0["0 PERCENT"]
```
