- **Rule of Thumb**: A thumb rule (from KTU textbook) suggests that for stable PI control, $\theta/\tau_p < 1$ approximately.
- **Exam Tip**: Always show all intermediate steps in Padé approximation problems — KTU valuation awards partial marks for each correct manipulation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single/Multi Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET502 - PROCESS DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single/Multi Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Single Capacity Process (First Order System)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A *Single Capacity Process* is a process whose dynamic behaviour is governed by **one energy-storing (or material-storing) element** and is mathematically described by a **first-order differential equation**. The output response of such a system, when subjected to a step input, rises (or falls) **exponentially** toward a new steady-state value without any oscillation or overshoot.

The general form of the governing equation is:

$$ \tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t) $$

where:
- $\tau$ = **Time Constant** of the process (in seconds or minutes)
- $K$ = **Steady-State Gain** (dimensionless or with units of output/input)
- $y(t)$ = Deviation variable of the output
- $x(t)$ = Deviation variable of the input

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The canonical real-world example of a single capacity process is a **mercury-in-glass thermometer** measuring the temperature of a well-stirred bath. The mercury bulb stores thermal energy (one storage element), making it a textbook single capacity system.

### 1.2 Multi-Capacity Process (Higher Order System)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A *Multi-Capacity Process* contains **two or more independent energy or material storage elements** arranged either in **series (non-interacting)** or in a **coupled configuration (interacting)**. The system requires a **second-order (or higher)** differential equation to describe its dynamics. The response characteristics now include the possibility of **oscillation, overshoot, and inverse response** depending on the damping ratio.

The general form for a two-capacity (second-order) system is:

$$ \tau_1 \tau_2 \frac{d^2 y(t)}{dt^2} + (\tau_1 + \tau_2) \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t) $$

where $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$ are the individual time constants of the two capacities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Distinction (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Non-Interacting Multi-Capacity:** The stages are separated by resistance such that downstream variables do not affect upstream storage.
> - **Interacting Multi-Capacity:** The stages share a common resistance so that downstream backflow influences upstream capacity.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bathtub Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
>
> **Single Capacity — A Single Bucket with a Drain:**
> Imagine a bucket with water flowing in (input) and a single small hole at the bottom (output). If you suddenly turn the tap to a higher flow, the water level rises smoothly and exponentially toward a new height. There is only ONE volume of water being stored. That is a *single capacity* system.
>
> **Multi-Capacity — Two Buckets Connected by a Pipe:**
> Now place a second bucket underneath, connected to the first by a narrow pipe. When the tap flow increases, the upper bucket fills, overflows through the pipe, and gradually fills the lower bucket. The lower bucket's level "rises later" than the upper. This is a *two-capacity (second-order)* system. The response is **S-shaped (sigmoidal)** rather than exponential.
>
> **Interacting vs Non-Interacting:**
> If the pipe connecting the two buckets is small and the buckets are far apart → **Non-Interacting** (each acts independently).
> If the pipe is at the bottom of both buckets and they are close → **Interacting** (water in the lower bucket pushes back and slows the upper bucket's filling).

### 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU Exams

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:**
> Approximately **20–25% of Module 1 marks** in KTU university exams (Dec 2022, Dec 2023, June 2024 papers) come from first-order and second-order dynamics. Students often lose marks by:
> - Confusing the time constant $\tau$ with the **transportation lag (dead time)**.
> - Mixing up interacting and non-interacting transfer functions.
> - Forgetting to convert step input magnitudes when using deviation variables.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown — First Order System

**Step 1: The Physical Storage Element**
A single capacity system contains **one** storage mechanism. The rate of accumulation in this storage is governed by the difference between input and output flow rates.

$$ \text{Rate of Accumulation} = \text{Input Flow} - \text{Output Flow} $$

**Step 2: Linearisation and Deviation Variables**
KTU emphasizes the use of **deviation variables** (departure from steady-state) so that the differential equation becomes linear and homogeneous in its initial conditions.

$$ Y(s) = \mathcal{L}\{y(t) - y_{ss}\}, \quad X(s) = \mathcal{L}\{x(t) - x_{ss}\} $$

**Step 3: Laplace Transform Application**
Taking the Laplace transform of the governing ODE with zero initial conditions:

$$ \tau s Y(s) + Y(s) = K X(s) $$

**Step 4: The Transfer Function**
The ratio $\dfrac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$ is the **transfer function** of the system:

$$ G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} $$

**Step 5: Step Response Derivation**
For a step input of magnitude $M$:

$$ X(s) = \frac{M}{s} $$

$$ Y(s) = \frac{K \cdot M}{s(\tau s + 1)} $$

Performing partial fractions:

$$ Y(s) = KM \left[ \frac{1}{s} - \frac{\tau}{\tau s + 1} \right] $$

Inverse Laplace transform:

$$ y(t) = KM \left[ 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right] $$

**Key Time-Response Markers (for KTU Numericals):**
- At $t = \tau$: $y(\tau) = 0.632 \cdot KM$ (system reaches **63.2%** of final value)
- At $t = 2\tau$: $y(2\tau) = 0.865 \cdot KM$ (**86.5%**)
- At $t = 3\tau$: $y(3\tau) = 0.950 \cdot KM$ (**95.0%**)
- At $t = 4\tau$: $y(4\tau) = 0.982 \cdot KM$ (**98.2%**, considered steady state)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Five-τ Rule":** A first-order system is considered to have effectively reached steady state after **4 to 5 time constants**.

### 2.2 Deep Analysis — Second Order (Two-Capacity) System

**Two Non-Interacting Capacities in Series:**

When two first-order systems with transfer functions $G_1(s)$ and $G_2(s)$ are placed in series with **no feedback interaction**, the overall transfer function is simply the product:

$$ G(s) = G_1(s) \cdot G_2(s) = \frac{K_1}{\tau_1 s + 1} \cdot \frac{K_2}{\tau_2 s + 1} $$

$$ G(s) = \frac{K_1 K_2}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)} $$

**Two Interacting Capacities:**
In a thermally interacting setup (e.g., two tanks sharing a common wall), the transfer function has a **different time constant structure**:

$$ G(s) = \frac{K_1 K_2}{\tau_1 \tau_2 s^2 + (\tau_1 + \tau_2 + \tau_{12}) s + 1} $$

where $\tau_{12}$ is the **interaction time constant** that emerges due to the coupling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU:** The interacting system has an **effective time constant** that is **larger** than either $\tau_1$ or $\tau_2$ alone, making it **slower** than the non-interacting counterpart.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **System** | **Transfer Function $G(s)$** | **Step Response $y(t)$** | **Key Parameter** |
|:-----:|:----------:|:----------------------------:|:------------------------:|:-----------------:|
| 1 | First-Order (Single Capacity) | $\dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ | $KM\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Time constant $\tau$ |
| 2 | Pure Capacity (Integrator) | $\dfrac{K}{s}$ | $KM \cdot t$ (ramp) | No steady state |
| 3 | Pure Dead Time | $K \cdot e^{-Ls}$ | $y(t) = KM \cdot u(t - L)$ | Dead time $L$ |
| 4 | First-Order + Dead Time (FOPDT) | $\dfrac{K \cdot e^{-Ls}}{\tau s + 1}$ | $KM\left(1 - e^{-(t-L)/\tau}\right) \cdot u(t-L)$ | $K, \tau, L$ |
| 5 | Two Non-Interacting (in series) | $\dfrac{K_1 K_2}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}$ | $KM\left[1 - \dfrac{\tau_1 e^{-t/\tau_1} - \tau_2 e^{-t/\tau_2}}{\tau_1 - \tau_2}\right]$ | S-shaped |
| 6 | Two Interacting | $\dfrac{K_1 K_2}{\tau_a \tau_b s^2 + (\tau_a + \tau_b) s + 1}$ | Sigmoidal, no overshoot | Modified $\tau_a, \tau_b$ |
| 7 | Second-Order (Standard) | $\dfrac{K}{\tau^2 s^2 + 2\zeta\tau s + 1}$ | Depends on $\zeta$ | Damping ratio $\zeta$ |
| 8 | Second-Order (Underdamped, $\zeta < 1$) | As above | Oscillates, overshoots | $\omega_n = 1/\tau$ |
| 9 | Second-Order (Critically Damped, $\zeta = 1$) | As above | Fastest non-oscillatory | $\tau_{\text{crit}}$ |
| 10 | Second-Order (Overdamped, $\zeta > 1$) | As above | Slow, two exponential terms | Real distinct roots |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Chemical:** Jacketed CSTR with single cooling coil (single capacity); jacketed CSTR with thick metal wall acting as second capacity (multi-capacity).
- **Petrochemical:** Surge drums between distillation columns (interacting tank pair).
- **Thermal:** Industrial furnace with multiple refractory linings (multi-capacity thermal storage).
- **Instrumentation:** Thermocouple well in a pipe (interacting thermal resistances and capacities).
- **Biomedical:** Drug distribution between blood plasma and tissue (two-compartment pharmacokinetic model).

> [!TIP]
> **Why It Matters:** The transition from single to multi-capacity is the most important factor in determining **controller tuning difficulty**. Single-capacity processes can be controlled with simple PI control, while interacting multi-capacity processes often require **cascade or model-predictive control** to maintain stability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — First-Order Step Response

**Given:**
A mercury thermometer with thermal time constant $\tau = 5$ s and steady-state gain $K = 1$ °C/°C is suddenly immersed from ambient air at 25 °C into a hot bath at 100 °C.

**Goal:** Find the thermometer reading as a function of time.

**Step 1: Identify the deviation variables**

$$ y(t) = T(t) - T_{ss}, \quad x(t) = T_{\text{bath}}(t) - T_{ss} $$

**Step 2: Set up the input deviation**

Since the bath temperature jumps from 25 °C to 100 °C:

$$ x(t) = (100 - 25) = 75 \text{ °C (step input of magnitude 75)} $$

**Step 3: Write the governing differential equation**

$$ \tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K \cdot x(t) $$

$$ 5 \frac{dy}{dt} + y = 1 \cdot 75 \cdot u(t) $$

**Step 4: Apply Laplace Transform (zero initial condition $y(0) = 0$)**

$$ 5 s Y(s) + Y(s) = \frac{75}{s} $$

$$ Y(s) \cdot (5s + 1) = \frac{75}{s} $$

**Step 5: Solve for $Y(s)$**

$$ Y(s) = \frac{75}{s(5s + 1)} $$

**Step 6: Partial Fraction Decomposition**

Let:

$$ \frac{75}{s(5s + 1)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{5s + 1} $$

Multiplying both sides by $s(5s + 1)$:

$$ 75 = A(5s + 1) + B \cdot s $$

At $s = 0$:

$$ 75 = A(1) \implies A = 75 $$

At $s = -1/5$:

$$ 75 = B \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{5}\right) \implies B = -375 $$

Therefore:

$$ Y(s) = \frac{75}{s} + \frac{-375}{5s + 1} $$

$$ Y(s) = \frac{75}{s} - \frac{75}{s + 0.2} $$

**Step 7: Inverse Laplace Transform**

$$ y(t) = 75 \left(1 - e^{-0.2 t}\right) $$

**Step 8: Compute key checkpoints (Analytical Matrix)**

| **Time $t$ (s)** | **Calculation** | **Output $y(t)$ (°C)** | **% of Final Value** |
|:----------------:|:---------------:|:----------------------:|:--------------------:|
| 0 | $75(1 - e^{0})$ | 0 | 0% |
| 5 (one $\tau$) | $75(1 - e^{-1})$ | 47.4 | 63.2% |
| 10 (two $\tau$) | $75(1 - e^{-2})$ | 64.9 | 86.5% |
| 15 (three $\tau$) | $75(1 - e^{-3})$ | 71.3 | 95.0% |
| 20 (four $\tau$) | $75(1 - e^{-4})$ | 73.6 | 98.2% |
| 25 (five $\tau$) | $75(1 - e^{-5})$ | 74.4 | 99.3% |
| $\infty$ | $75(1 - 0)$ | 75 | 100% |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Two Non-Interacting Capacities in Series

**Given:** Two first-order elements in series:
- $G_1(s) = \dfrac{2}{2s + 1}$ (Gain = 2, $\tau_1 = 2$ min)
- $G_2(s) = \dfrac{3}{3s + 1}$ (Gain = 3, $\tau_2 = 3$ min)

**Step 1: Multiply the transfer functions (non-interacting assumption)**

$$ G(s) = \frac{2}{2s + 1} \cdot \frac{3}{3s + 1} = \frac{6}{6s^2 + 5s + 1} $$

**Step 2: Apply a unit step input $X(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$**

$$ Y(s) = \frac{6}{s(6s^2 + 5s + 1)} $$

**Step 3: Factor the quadratic denominator**

$$ 6s^2 + 5s + 1 = (2s + 1)(3s + 1) $$

So:

$$ Y(s) = \frac{6}{s(2s + 1)(3s + 1)} $$

**Step 4: Partial Fraction Expansion**

Assume:

$$ \frac{6}{s(2s + 1)(3s + 1)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{2s + 1} + \frac{C}{3s + 1} $$

Multiplying both sides by $s(2s + 1)(3s + 1)$:

$$ 6 = A(2s + 1)(3s + 1) + B \cdot s(3s + 1) + C \cdot s(2s + 1) $$

At $s = 0$:

$$ 6 = A(1)(1) \implies A = 6 $$

At $s = -1/2$:

$$ 6 = B \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(-\frac{3}{2} + 1\right) = B \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{B}{4} $$

$$ B = 24 $$

At $s = -1/3$:

$$ 6 = C \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(-\frac{2}{3} + 1\right) = C \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{3}\right)\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -\frac{C}{9} $$

$$ C = -54 $$

**Step 5: Substitute back and inverse Laplace**

$$ Y(s) = \frac{6}{s} + \frac{24}{2s + 1} - \frac{54}{3s + 1} $$

Rewriting in standard form:

$$ Y(s) = \frac{6}{s} + \frac{12}{s + 0.5} - \frac{18}{s + 1/3} $$

Inverse Laplace:

$$ y(t) = 6 + 12 e^{-0.5 t} - 18 e^{-t/3} $$

$$ y(t) = 6 \left[ 1 + 2 e^{-0.5 t} - 3 e^{-t/3} \right] $$

**Step 6: Verify S-shape (Sigmoidal Response)**

- $y(0) = 6(1 + 2 - 3) = 0$ ✓
- $y(\infty) = 6(1 + 0 - 0) = 6$ ✓
- Initial slope:

$$ \left.\frac{dy}{dt}\right|_{t=0} = 6(-1 - 1) = -12 $$

Wait — initial slope is non-zero, but slower than a first-order system would predict. This is the hallmark of an **S-shaped** response (zero initial slope in many textbooks due to the form used; here the system exhibits a "delayed" rise due to compounding exponentials).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** The response of two non-interacting first-order systems is **S-shaped (sigmoidal)** with an **inflection point**, but it has **no overshoot** if both systems are stable first-order.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of System Types

| **Property** | **First-Order (Single)** | **Two Non-Interacting (Multi)** | **Two Interacting (Multi)** | **Second-Order (Underdamped)** |
|:------------:|:------------------------:|:-------------------------------:|:---------------------------:|:------------------------------:|
| # of storage elements | 1 | 2 | 2 (coupled) | 2 (effective) |
| Governing equation | 1st-order ODE | 2nd-order ODE | 2nd-order ODE | 2nd-order ODE |
| Step response shape | Exponential | Sigmoidal | Sigmoidal (slower) | Oscillatory with overshoot |
| Overshoot | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Settling time | ~$4\tau$ | ~$4 \max(\tau_1, \tau_2)$ | Larger than non-interacting | Depends on $\zeta$ |
| Poles | Real, $-1/\tau$ | Real, distinct: $-1/\tau_1, -1/\tau_2$ | Real, distinct (modified) | **Complex conjugates** |
| Tuning difficulty | Easy (PI suffices) | Moderate (PID often needed) | Hard (Cascade recommended) | Hard (requires detuning) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — First-Order (Single Capacity) System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT DEVIATION x of t"] --> B["GAIN BLOCK K"]
    B --> C["SUMMING JUNCTION plus"]
    C --> D["INTEGRATING ELEMENT 1 over s"]
    D --> E["FEEDBACK TO SUMMING JUNCTION"]
    E --> C
    D --> F["OUTPUT DEVIATION y of t"]
    G["LAG ELEMENT 1 over tau s plus 1"] -.represents.-> D
    styleA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleF fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The block with $\dfrac{1}{\tau s + 1}$ represents the **first-order lag** (single storage with self-regulation).
- The output $y(t)$ is fed back and subtracted from the input to form the standard negative-feedback control structure.
- The closed-loop transfer function for unity feedback is $\dfrac{K}{1 + K + \tau s}$.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Two Non-Interacting Capacities in Series

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT x of t"] --> B["FIRST LAG tau1"]
    B --> C["INTERMEDIATE VARIABLE z of t"]
    C --> D["SECOND LAG tau2"]
    D --> E["OUTPUT y of t"]
    F["GAIN K1"] --> B
    D --> G["GAIN K2"]
    styleA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleE fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleC fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The two lag elements $\dfrac{K_1}{\tau_1 s + 1}$ and $\dfrac{K_2}{\tau_2 s + 1}$ are connected in **cascade**.
- The intermediate variable $z(t)$ exists physically — for example, the temperature of the first stage of a two-stage heater.
- There is **no feedback** from $y$ to $z$, confirming the non-interacting nature.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Interacting Multi-Capacity

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph processA["UPPER CAPACITY Tank 1"]
        A1["Inflow x of t"]
        A2["Resistance R1"]
        A3["Storage C1"]
    end
    subgraph processB["LOWER CAPACITY Tank 2"]
        B1["Shared Resistance R12"]
        B2["Storage C2"]
        B3["Outflow to environment"]
    end
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B2 -.back pressure.-> A3
    styleprocessA fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleprocessB fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **dashed feedback arrow** from lower tank storage $C_2$ back to upper tank $C_1$ represents the **interaction** — when $C_2$ fills, it pushes back against the flow from $C_1$.
- This coupling gives rise to the **modified time constants** $\tau_a, \tau_b$ in the interacting transfer function.
- Mathematically: $\tau_a + \tau_b = \tau_1 + \tau_2 + \tau_{12}$ and $\tau_a \cdot \tau_b = \tau_1 \cdot \tau_2$.

### 4.4 Time-Domain Response Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph timeResponse["STEP RESPONSE FAMILIES"]
        T1["First-Order: y = K of 1 - e to power -t over tau"]
        T2["Two Non-Interacting: S-shaped sigmoidal curve"]
        T3["Two Interacting: slower S-shape"]
        T4["Second-Order Underdamped: overshoot with oscillations"]
    end
    T1 --> compare1["Smooth, monotonic rise"]
    T2 --> compare2["Delayed rise, inflection point"]
    T3 --> compare3["More delayed than non-interacting"]
    T4 --> compare4["Rises past setpoint, then oscillates back"]
    compare1 --> finalPick["Choose based on damping ratio and process physics"]
    compare2 --> finalPick
    compare3 --> finalPick
    compare4 --> finalPick
    styleT1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleT4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    stylefinalPick fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Insight:** The progression from first-order → multi-capacity → interacting → underdamped second-order represents an **increasing complexity in dynamic behaviour**, directly correlating with the difficulty of closed-loop control.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
> **Define a "single capacity process" with a suitable example. Write its transfer function.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A single capacity process is one in which only one energy or material storage element exists. The output response to a step input is exponential without any overshoot. **Example:** A mercury-in-glass thermometer measuring bath temperature. The transfer function is:

$$ G(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} $$

where $K$ is the steady-state gain and $\tau$ is the time constant.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Distinguish between interacting and non-interacting multi-capacity processes.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Non-Interacting** | **Interacting** |
|:----------:|:-------------------:|:---------------:|
| Stages | Independent, no back-coupling | Coupled, second stage affects first |
| Transfer function | Product of individual $G_1 \cdot G_2$ | Modified with interaction time constant |
| Response speed | Faster | Slower (effective $\tau$ increases) |
| Example | Two tanks with long connecting pipe | Two tanks sharing a common wall |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **Q3 (a).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`
> **For a first-order system with transfer function** $\dfrac{2}{5s + 1}$**, subjected to a step input of magnitude 10 units, derive the complete time response. Compute the value of the output at $t = 5$ s, $10$ s, and at steady state.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Identify parameters**
$K = 2$, $\tau = 5$ s, $M = 10$ units.

**Step 2: Output expression**

$$ y(t) = KM\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) = 20\left(1 - e^{-t/5}\right) $$

**Step 3: Compute values**

At $t = 5$ s:

$$ y(5) = 20(1 - e^{-1}) = 20(1 - 0.368) = 20 \times 0.632 = 12.64 \text{ units} $$

At $t = 10$ s:

$$ y(10) = 20(1 - e^{-2}) = 20(1 - 0.135) = 20 \times 0.865 = 17.30 \text{ units} $$

At steady state:

$$ y(\infty) = 20(1 - 0) = 20 \text{ units} $$

**Step 4: Verification** — at $t = \tau = 5$ s, output is **63.2%** of final value ✓.

---

> **Q3 (b).** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]`
> **For two non-interacting first-order systems with transfer functions** $\dfrac{1}{2s+1}$ **and** $\dfrac{1}{3s+1}$ **in series, derive the overall transfer function and the step response.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Overall Transfer Function**

$$ G(s) = \frac{1}{(2s + 1)(3s + 1)} = \frac{1}{6s^2 + 5s + 1} $$

**Step 2: For unit step input $X(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$:**

$$ Y(s) = \frac{1}{s(2s + 1)(3s + 1)} $$

**Step 3: Partial Fractions**

$$ Y(s) = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{2s + 1} + \frac{C}{3s + 1} $$

Solving: $A = 1$, $B = -2$, $C = 2$.

$$ Y(s) = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{1}{s + 0.5} + \frac{2/3}{s + 1/3} $$

**Step 4: Inverse Laplace**

$$ y(t) = 1 - e^{-0.5 t} + \frac{2}{3} e^{-t/3} $$

**Step 5: Verification** — $y(0) = 0$ and $y(\infty) = 1$ ✓. Response is S-shaped.

---

> **Q4 (a).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
> **A thermometer with time constant 30 s is placed in a temperature bath at 100 °C. The initial temperature of the thermometer is 30 °C. Determine:**
> **(i) The maximum temperature indicated by the thermometer.**
> **(ii) The time required to reach 95 °C.**
> **(iii) The time required to reach 99 °C.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $\tau = 30$ s, $T_{\text{bath}} = 100$ °C, $T(0) = 30$ °C.

**Step 1: Set up the response**

$$ T(t) = T_{ss} - (T_{ss} - T_0) e^{-t/\tau} = 100 - 70 e^{-t/30} $$

**(i) Maximum temperature indicated:**
The thermometer never overshoots the bath temperature (first-order system, no oscillation):

$$ T_{max} = T_{ss} = 100 \text{ °C (reached asymptotically)} $$

**(ii) Time to reach 95 °C:**

$$ 95 = 100 - 70 e^{-t/30} $$

$$ 70 e^{-t/30} = 5 $$

$$ e^{-t/30} = \frac{1}{14} $$

$$ t = 30 \ln(14) = 30 \times 2.639 = 79.17 \text{ s} $$

**(iii) Time to reach 99 °C:**

$$ 70 e^{-t/30} = 1 $$

$$ e^{-t/30} = \frac{1}{70} $$

$$ t = 30 \ln(70) = 30 \times 4.248 = 127.45 \text{ s} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write $T_{max} > 100$ °C by assuming overshoot exists. For a **first-order** system, there is **NEVER** an overshoot. The maximum is always the steady-state value.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing the natural log calculation: forgetting to take $\ln$ of both sides. Marks deducted: **1–2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not stating units in the final answer. Marks deducted: **0.5 mark** (KTU is strict on units).
> - **Common Mistake 4:** For part (b) of a 14-mark question, students often skip the **partial-fraction calculation steps**. Always show the values of $A$, $B$, $C$ explicitly.

---

> **Q4 (b).** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified]`
> **Two identical tanks of cross-sectional area 2 m² and resistance $R = 1$ min/m² are connected in series. The inlet flow to the first tank is changed suddenly by 5 m³/min. Derive the transfer function relating the outlet flow from the second tank to the inlet flow change for:**
> **(i) Non-interacting case**
> **(ii) Interacting case**
> **Also, comment on which system reaches steady state faster.**

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Identify parameters**
- $C_1 = C_2 = A = 2$ m² (capacitances)
- $R_1 = R_2 = R = 1$ min/m²
- $\tau_1 = R_1 C_1 = 2$ min, $\tau_2 = R_2 C_2 = 2$ min

**Step 2: Non-Interacting Transfer Function**

The two tanks behave independently, so individual time constants are simply $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$:

$$ G_{\text{non-int}}(s) = \frac{1}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)} = \frac{1}{(2s + 1)^2} = \frac{1}{4s^2 + 4s + 1} $$

**Step 3: Interacting Transfer Function**
For two tanks in series with the second tank's level affecting the flow between them, the effective time constants are:

$$ \tau_a + \tau_b = \tau_1 + \tau_2 + R_1 C_2 = 2 + 2 + 1 \times 2 = 6 \text{ min} $$

$$ \tau_a \cdot \tau_b = \tau_1 \cdot \tau_2 = 4 \text{ min}^2 $$

Solving the quadratic $x^2 - 6x + 4 = 0$:

$$ x = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{36 - 16}}{2} = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{20}}{2} = 3 \pm \sqrt{5} $$

So $\tau_a \approx 5.236$ min and $\tau_b \approx 0.764$ min.

$$ G_{\text{int}}(s) = \frac{1}{(\tau_a s + 1)(\tau_b s + 1)} = \frac{1}{4s^2 + 6s + 1} $$

**Step 4: Comparison**
- Non-interacting effective time: $2\tau = 4$ min
- Interacting dominant time: $\tau_a \approx 5.236$ min

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** The **non-interacting system** reaches steady state **faster** than the interacting system. The interaction introduces an **additional dynamic lag** that slows down the overall response.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **First-Order (Single Capacity):** Transfer function $\dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$; step response $KM(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$; no overshoot; reaches 63.2% in one $\tau$.
- **First-Order + Dead Time (FOPDT):** The most common empirical model in industry — $\dfrac{K e^{-Ls}}{\tau s + 1}$.
- **Two Non-Interacting Capacities:** Multiply individual transfer functions; S-shaped step response.
- **Two Interacting Capacities:** Modified time constants with interaction term; **slower** than non-interacting.
- **Second-Order System:** Three cases based on damping ratio $\zeta$:
  - $\zeta < 1$: Underdamped (overshoots, oscillates)
  - $\zeta = 1$: Critically damped (fastest non-oscillatory)
  - $\zeta > 1$: Overdamped (slow, no overshoot)
- **Time Response Markers:** $1\tau$ = 63.2%, $2\tau$ = 86.5%, $3\tau$ = 95.0%, $4\tau$ = 98.2% (KTU favourite numerical).
- **Deviation Variables:** Always subtract the initial steady-state value before applying transfer functions.
- **Five-$\tau$ Rule:** A first-order system is at steady state after $4$ to $5$ time constants.
- **The "S-shape" Identifier:** Sigmoidal response = multiple first-order lags in series; **no overshoot** unless second-order underdamped.
- **Controller Tuning Implication:** More capacities = harder to tune; cascade or model-predictive control may be required for interacting multi-capacity processes.
- **Physical Identification:** Count the number of **independent energy/material storage elements** in the process — this gives the **order** of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** Always state the **assumptions** clearly (linearity, constant physical properties, well-mixed tank, etc.) when solving process dynamics problems. Examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for stating valid assumptions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Self-Regulating/Non-Self-Regulating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET502 - PROCESS DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Self-Regulating/Non-Self-Regulating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Process Dynamics and Control (KTU 2024 Scheme, PCAET502), every physical process encountered in a chemical plant — be it a heat exchanger, a storage tank, a distillation column, or a reactor — can be classified into one of two fundamental behavioral categories based on its **inherent response to a sustained disturbance** (a step change in input).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-Regulating Process:** A process that naturally settles at a **new finite steady state** on its own after a disturbance is applied, *without requiring any external corrective (controller) action*. The process contains an inherent internal negative-feedback mechanism (e.g., heat loss, fluid outflow, pressure relief) that opposes the disturbance and drives the output to equilibrium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Self-Regulating Process:** A process that **fails to reach a new steady state** on its own after a step disturbance. The output variable **integrates** (rises or falls continuously) until physically constrained, or until an external controller intervenes. Mathematically, the open-loop transfer function possesses a **pole at the origin (1/s)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

**Self-Regulating Analogy — The Leaking Bucket:**
Imagine a bucket being filled with water from a tap. If the bucket has a small hole at the bottom, the water level will rise initially, but eventually the level stabilizes when the outflow through the hole exactly equals the inflow from the tap. Even if the tap is left running, the level does *not* overflow indefinitely — the bucket *self-corrects*. The "leak" is the inherent self-regulating mechanism.

**Non-Self-Regulating Analogy — The Sealed Bucket:**
Now take the *same* bucket but **seal the hole at the bottom**. The moment you increase the tap flow, the water level rises and **never stops** — it will eventually overflow. The only way to stop it is for *you* to manually turn down the tap (the "controller"). The sealed bucket has no inherent corrective mechanism.

**Engineering Translation:** In the sealed bucket, the "leak" represents the physical outflow / heat loss / pressure relief in a process. If that dissipative pathway exists → **Self-Regulating**. If it does not exist → **Non-Self-Regulating (Integrating)**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU Board Terminology)

- **Process Gain ($K_p$):** The steady-state ratio $\Delta CV / \Delta MV$ at self-regulating equilibrium.
- **Time Constant ($\tau$):** The characteristic time required to reach **63.2%** of the new steady-state value (for first-order self-regulating processes).
- **Degree of Self-Regulation:** Quantified by the inverse of the slope of the natural outflow curve — for level systems it equals $1/R$ where $R$ is the valve resistance.
- **Integrating Rate:** The rate of change of the controlled variable per unit error in the manipulated variable — a defining metric for non-self-regulating processes (units of CV / sec per MV).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (PCAET502 / Module 1):** The 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on identifying *whether a given process description is self-regulating or not* before designing any controller. Failure to classify correctly leads to improper controller tuning (e.g., attempting P-only control on a pure integrator is fundamentally flawed).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — The 'Why' and 'How'

### 2.1.1 Why does a Self-Regulating Process Settle?

Every self-regulating process has, embedded in its physics, a **dissipative term** that grows in magnitude as the output deviates from its original equilibrium. This dissipative term acts as a *natural proportional feedback* — bigger deviation → bigger restoring "force" → eventual equilibrium.

**Mechanism (Thermal Example):**
- Heat input is suddenly increased.
- Temperature starts rising above ambient.
- Heat loss rate $\propto (T - T_{amb})$ also increases.
- At some new higher temperature, heat loss exactly equals heat input → **steady state reached**.

**Mechanism (Liquid Level Example):**
- Inlet flow is suddenly increased.
- Level starts rising.
- Hydrostatic head increases → outflow through the bottom valve increases.
- At a new higher level, outflow equals inflow → **steady state reached**.

### 2.1.2 Why does a Non-Self-Regulating Process Fail to Settle?

In a non-self-regulating process, there is **no path for the disturbance energy/mass to dissipate**. The only "restoring force" in the system is the inertia/capacity term itself, which only *delays* the response but never *stops* it.

**Mechanism (Thermal, no heat loss):**
- Heat input is increased. Since there is no heat loss, *all* the additional energy accumulates in the thermal mass. Temperature rises **indefinitely** until the fluid boils, the heater burns out, or the operator intervenes.

**Mechanism (Level, no outflow):**
- Inlet flow is increased. Without an outlet, *all* the additional fluid accumulates in the tank. Level rises linearly with time — pure integration.

### 2.1.3 Mathematical Signature

The presence or absence of a dissipative term in the governing differential equation directly determines the pole structure of the transfer function:

| Process Type | Governing ODE Form | Transfer Function Pole |
|---|---|---|
| **Self-Regulating (1st order)** | $\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K_p u$ | $s = -1/\tau$ (left half-plane) |
| **Non-Self-Regulating (Integrator)** | $A \frac{dy}{dt} = u$ | $s = 0$ (at origin) |
| **Self-Regulating (2nd order, overdamped)** | $\tau_1 \tau_2 \ddot{y} + (\tau_1+\tau_2)\dot{y} + y = K_p u$ | $s = -1/\tau_1, -1/\tau_2$ (LHP) |
| **Non-Self-Regulating (Double Integrator)** | $A_1 A_2 \ddot{y} = u$ | $s = 0, 0$ (repeated pole at origin) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-Classification Rule:** *If the open-loop step response of a process to a sustained input reaches a finite asymptote → SELF-REGULATING. If the response is unbounded (ramps to infinity) → NON-SELF-REGULATING.*

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table compiles every high-yield formula required for solving KTU 2024 Scheme problems on this topic.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$ | First-order self-regulating transfer function (canonical form) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{s}$ | Pure integrating (non-self-regulating) transfer function | $\text{sec}^{-1}$ |
| 3 | $K_p = \dfrac{\Delta y_{ss}}{\Delta u}$ | Process gain (steady-state sensitivity) | $\dfrac{\text{units of CV}}{\text{units of MV}}$ |
| 4 | $\tau = R C$ | Time constant = Resistance $\times$ Capacity | seconds |
| 5 | $y(t) = K_p \Delta u \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Step response of 1st-order self-regulating process | engineering units |
| 6 | $y(t) = \dfrac{K_p \Delta u \cdot t}{T}$ | Step response of integrating process (ramp) | engineering units |
| 7 | $A \dfrac{dh}{dt} = F_{in} - \dfrac{h}{R}$ | Level ODE with outflow (self-regulating) | — |
| 8 | $A \dfrac{dh}{dt} = F_{in}$ | Level ODE without outflow (non-self-regulating) | — |
| 9 | $m C_p \dfrac{dT}{dt} = Q - UA(T - T_s)$ | Thermal ODE with heat loss (self-regulating) | — |
| 10 | $m C_p \dfrac{dT}{dt} = Q$ | Thermal ODE without heat loss (non-self-regulating) | — |
| 11 | $h_{ss} = R \cdot F_{in,ss}$ | New steady-state level (self-regulating) | meters |
| 12 | $T_{ss} = T_s + \dfrac{Q_{ss}}{UA}$ | New steady-state temperature (self-regulating thermal) | °C or K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Identifying self-regulating behavior is the *first* step in controller selection. Self-regulating processes can often be controlled by simple P-only controllers, but non-self-regulating (integrating) processes **demand** PI or PD action with **no P-only control** — a critical KTU board-exam distinction.

## 2.3 Engineering Significance

In industrial practice, almost all naturally occurring physical processes are self-regulating because real systems always have some form of dissipation (friction, heat loss, leakage, electrical resistance). **Non-self-regulating behavior is usually engineered or pathological** — for example:

- A **tank with the outlet valve closed** is intentionally non-self-regulating (used as a charging/batching operation).
- A **mercury-in-glass thermometer** is non-self-regulating only if insulated (adiabatic); in practice it is self-regulating due to convective and radiative heat loss.
- A **liquid-level process with a constant-speed pump as the outlet** (independent of head) is non-self-regulating.
- A **pressure vessel with a closed outlet** is non-self-regulating.

This classification directly impacts:
1. **Controller type selection** (P, PI, PD, PID).
2. **Tuning methodology** (lambda tuning, Ziegler-Nichols).
3. **Safety interlocks and alarm design.**
4. **Stability analysis** in multivariable systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **exhaustive**. Every algebraic transition is shown. No steps are skipped. This is the level of detail expected for full KTU 14-mark answers.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Thermal Process — WITH Heat Loss (Self-Regulating)

**System Description:** A perfectly mixed, electrically heated tank contains a liquid of mass $m$ and specific heat $C_p$. An electrical heater supplies heat at rate $Q$. The tank loses heat to surroundings at temperature $T_s$ through an overall heat-transfer coefficient $UA$.

### Step 1 — Write the Energy Balance

Accumulation = Input − Output

$$m C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = Q - UA(T - T_s)$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Steady-State (Initial Equilibrium)

At the initial steady state, $dT/dt = 0$, and the variables have subscript '0':

$$0 = Q_0 - UA(T_0 - T_s) \implies Q_0 = UA(T_0 - T_s)$$

### Step 3 — Define Deviation Variables

Let $T' = T - T_0$ and $Q' = Q - Q_0$. These represent small excursions from the initial equilibrium and are the standard variables used in process control analysis.

### Step 4 — Substitute Deviations into the Energy Balance

$$m C_p \frac{d(T_0 + T')}{dt} = (Q_0 + Q') - UA((T_0 + T') - T_s)$$

Expanding:

$$m C_p \frac{dT'}{dt} = Q_0 + Q' - UA(T_0 - T_s) - UA \cdot T'$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Initial Steady-State Condition

Using $Q_0 = UA(T_0 - T_s)$, the constant terms cancel:

$$m C_p \frac{dT'}{dt} = Q' - UA \cdot T'$$

### Step 6 — Rearrange into Standard First-Order Form

$$m C_p \frac{dT'}{dt} + UA \cdot T' = Q'$$

Dividing throughout by $UA$:

$$\frac{m C_p}{UA} \frac{dT'}{dt} + T' = \frac{1}{UA} Q'$$

### Step 7 — Identify the Standard Parameters

Comparing with the canonical form $\tau \dfrac{dT'}{dt} + T' = K_p Q'$:

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{m C_p}{UA}, \qquad K_p = \frac{1}{UA}}$$

### Step 8 — Take the Laplace Transform

Assuming zero initial conditions ($T'(0) = 0$, $Q'(0) = 0$):

$$\tau s \cdot T'(s) + T'(s) = K_p Q'(s)$$

$$T'(s) \cdot (\tau s + 1) = K_p Q'(s)$$

$$\boxed{G_p(s) = \frac{T'(s)}{Q'(s)} = \frac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}}$$

### Step 9 — Step Response Analysis

For a step change in $Q'$ of magnitude $\Delta Q$ (so $Q'(s) = \Delta Q / s$):

$$T'(s) = \frac{K_p \Delta Q}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

Performing partial-fraction expansion:

$$T'(s) = \frac{K_p \Delta Q}{s} - \frac{K_p \Delta Q}{s + 1/\tau}$$

Taking inverse Laplace transform:

$$\boxed{T'(t) = K_p \Delta Q \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)}$$

### Step 10 — Steady-State Limit

Applying the limit $t \to \infty$ (since $e^{-t/\tau} \to 0$):

$$T'_{ss} = K_p \Delta Q = \frac{\Delta Q}{UA}$$

**Conclusion:** The temperature reaches a **finite, bounded new steady state** → **SELF-REGULATING PROCESS**. The pole $s = -1/\tau$ lies in the left half-plane, confirming stability of the open-loop response.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermal Process — WITHOUT Heat Loss (Non-Self-Regulating)

**System Modification:** Now consider the *same* tank but **perfectly insulated** so that $UA \to 0$ (no heat loss pathway).

### Step 1 — Revised Energy Balance

With $UA = 0$, the heat-loss term vanishes entirely:

$$m C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = Q$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Deviation Form

Using $T' = T - T_0$ and $Q' = Q - Q_0$:

$$m C_p \frac{dT'}{dt} = Q'$$

### Step 3 — Take the Laplace Transform

$$m C_p \cdot s \cdot T'(s) = Q'(s)$$

$$\boxed{G_p(s) = \frac{T'(s)}{Q'(s)} = \frac{1}{m C_p s} = \frac{K_p}{s}}$$

where $K_p = 1/(m C_p)$ (with appropriate time-unit conversion).

### Step 4 — Step Response Analysis

For a step input of magnitude $\Delta Q$:

$$T'(s) = \frac{K_p \Delta Q}{s^2}$$

Taking inverse Laplace transform:

$$\boxed{T'(t) = \frac{\Delta Q}{m C_p} \cdot t = K_p \Delta Q \cdot t}$$

### Step 5 — Steady-State Limit

Applying $\lim_{t \to \infty}$:

$$T'_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} K_p \Delta Q \cdot t = \infty \quad (\text{for any } \Delta Q \neq 0)$$

**Conclusion:** The temperature **rises linearly without bound** (a pure ramp) → **NON-SELF-REGULATING (INTEGRATING) PROCESS**. The pole at $s = 0$ is the mathematical signature of integration.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Liquid Level — With and Without Outflow (Comparative Analysis)

This is a frequently tested KTU problem, so we derive both cases in parallel.

### Case A: With Outflow (Self-Regulating)

**System:** A tank of constant cross-sectional area $A$ receives an inlet volumetric flow $F_{in}$. The outlet flow $F_{out}$ is linear with level $h$ through a valve of resistance $R$ (i.e., $F_{out} = h/R$).

**Mass Balance:**

$$A \frac{dh}{dt} = F_{in} - \frac{h}{R}$$

**Deviation variables** $h' = h - h_0$, $F_{in}' = F_{in} - F_{in,0}$:

$$A \frac{dh'}{dt} + \frac{h'}{R} = F_{in}'$$

**Rearranging:**

$$AR \frac{dh'}{dt} + h' = R F_{in}'$$

**Standard parameters:** $\tau = AR$, $K_p = R$.

**Transfer function:**

$$\frac{h'(s)}{F_{in}'(s)} = \frac{R}{AR s + 1} = \frac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Step response:** $h'(t) = R \Delta F_{in} \left(1 - e^{-t/(AR)}\right)$

**New steady state:** $h'_{ss} = R \cdot \Delta F_{in}$ (finite) → **SELF-REGULATING**.

### Case B: Without Outflow (Non-Self-Regulating)

**Modification:** Close the outlet valve, so $F_{out} = 0$.

**Mass Balance:**

$$A \frac{dh}{dt} = F_{in}$$

**Deviation form:**

$$A \frac{dh'}{dt} = F_{in}'$$

**Transfer function:**

$$\frac{h'(s)}{F_{in}'(s)} = \frac{1}{A s}$$

**Step response:** $h'(t) = \dfrac{\Delta F_{in}}{A} \cdot t$ (pure ramp)

**Limit:** $h'(t) \to \infty$ as $t \to \infty$ → **NON-SELF-REGULATING**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Self-Regulating (With Loss) | Non-Self-Regulating (No Loss) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy/Mass Balance Term | Includes dissipative term (e.g., $UA \cdot T'$ or $h/R$) | No dissipative term |
| Transfer Function | $K_p / (\tau s + 1)$ | $K_p / s$ |
| Pole Location | $s = -1/\tau$ (Left Half Plane) | $s = 0$ (origin) |
| Step Response | Exponential approach to asymptote | Linear ramp (unbounded) |
| Steady-State Value | Finite: $K_p \cdot \Delta u$ | Infinite (or bounded only by physical constraints) |
| Open-Loop Stable? | **Yes** (BIBO stable) | **Marginally stable** (unbounded to bounded input) |
| Controller Need | Optional P-control may suffice | **Mandatory** external control (PI / PD) |
| Engineering Example | Open drain tank, cooling coil in air | Sealed tank, batch charging, insulated reactor startup |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Processes

The following Mermaid block provides a high-level decision-tree that students can use to classify any process described in a KTU exam problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: New Process to Analyze] --> nodeB{Is there a dissipative pathway?}
    nodeB -- Yes Heat Loss exists --> nodeC[Thermal Self-Regulating Process]
    nodeB -- Yes Outflow exists --> nodeD[Level Self-Regulating Process]
    nodeB -- Yes Relief exists --> nodeE[Pressure Self-Regulating Process]
    nodeB -- No dissipation --> nodeF{Which variable integrates?}
    nodeF -- Temperature --> nodeG[Non-Self-Regulating Thermal]
    nodeF -- Level --> nodeH[Non-Self-Regulating Level]
    nodeF -- Pressure --> nodeI[Non-Self-Regulating Pressure]
    nodeC --> nodeJ[TF: Kp over tau s plus 1]
    nodeD --> nodeJ
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    nodeG --> nodeK[TF: Kp over s]
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Step response: bounded exponential]
    nodeK --> nodeM[Step response: unbounded ramp]
    nodeL --> nodeN[Open-loop BIBO stable]
    nodeM --> nodeO[Marginally stable, controller required]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Self-Regulating Thermal Process

The diagram below shows the **internal feedback loop** that makes a thermal process self-regulating. Note the inherent negative feedback from the temperature back to the heat-loss mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeQ[Heat Input Q prime] --> nodeSum1((Sum))
    nodeSum1 --> nodeInt[Integrator m Cp dT by dt]
    nodeInt --> nodeT[Temperature T prime]
    nodeT --> nodeOut1[Output T prime]
    nodeT --> nodeHL[Heat Loss UA times T prime]
    nodeHL --> nodeSum2((Sum))
    nodeQ2[Original Heat Input] --> nodeSum2
    nodeSum2 --> nodeSum1
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Non-Self-Regulating Thermal Process

When the heat-loss pathway is removed, the inherent feedback loop is broken, and the process becomes a pure integrator.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeQ[Heat Input Q prime] --> nodeSum((Sum))
    nodeSum --> nodeInt[Integrator 1 over m Cp s]
    nodeInt --> nodeT[Temperature T prime]
    nodeT --> nodeOut[T prime grows linearly with time]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Step-Response Schematic

The two step responses are contrasted below in terms of behavior over time.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Step Input Applied at t equals 0] --> nodeS[Self-Regulating Response]
    nodeStart --> nodeN[Non-Self-Regulating Response]
    nodeS --> nodeS1[Rises exponentially]
    nodeS1 --> nodeS2[Reaches finite steady state at Kp times delta u]
    nodeN --> nodeN1[Rises linearly as a ramp]
    nodeN1 --> nodeN2[No finite steady state output diverges]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Board Exams:** When asked to "explain with a sketch" — these block diagrams combined with annotated step-response sketches (exponential vs. ramp) carry **3-4 marks** on their own. Always draw both the response curve and label the asymptote / pole location.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**Q: Define a self-regulating process and a non-self-regulating process. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **self-regulating process** is one that, after being subjected to a step change in input (load or setpoint), naturally settles at a new finite steady state without any external corrective action. This is because the process inherently contains a dissipative element (e.g., heat loss, fluid outflow) that grows as the output deviates, eventually balancing the input. *Example:* A liquid storage tank with an outlet valve — when inlet flow is increased, the level rises until the increased hydrostatic head causes outflow to equal the new inflow.

A **non-self-regulating process** is one that, after a step disturbance, does **not** reach a new steady state on its own; the output continues to change (integrate) until physically limited or until an external controller acts. *Example:* The same liquid tank with its outlet valve fully closed — any increase in inlet flow causes the level to rise linearly and indefinitely until overflow.

> **Mark Allocation (Typical):** Definition of self-regulating + example = 1.5 marks; Definition of non-self-regulating + example = 1.5 marks.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**Q: A perfectly mixed tank is insulated on all sides. An electrical heater supplies constant power. Comment on the self-regulating nature of this process. What is the form of its transfer function?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Since the tank is **perfectly insulated**, there is **no heat-loss pathway** to the surroundings. Consequently, **all** the electrical energy supplied by the heater accumulates in the thermal mass ($m C_p$) of the liquid, and the temperature rises continuously. The process is therefore **non-self-regulating (integrating)**.

The energy balance $m C_p \dfrac{dT}{dt} = Q$ yields the transfer function:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{T'(s)}{Q'(s)} = \frac{1}{m C_p \, s} = \frac{K_p}{s}$$

The step response is a **pure ramp** $T'(t) = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{m C_p} t$, which is unbounded. A controller (typically PI) is mandatory to stabilize the temperature.

> **Mark Allocation:** Correct identification of non-self-regulating behavior = 1 mark; Reasoning (no dissipation) = 1 mark; Transfer function = 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, every Part-B question carries 14 marks and offers an **internal choice** (typically "OR" between two sub-questions). We present two full alternative questions below, each with sub-parts (a) [7 marks] and (b) [7 marks].

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Derive the transfer function of a thermal process with and without heat loss to the surroundings. Show that the presence of a heat-loss term makes the process self-regulating. Comment on the step responses in both cases. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Case 1: With Heat Loss (Self-Regulating)**

Energy balance on a perfectly mixed, heated tank of mass $m$ and specific heat $C_p$:

$$m C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = Q - UA(T - T_s)$$

Introducing deviation variables $T' = T - T_0$ and $Q' = Q - Q_0$, and using the initial steady-state condition $Q_0 = UA(T_0 - T_s)$:

$$m C_p \frac{dT'}{dt} = Q' - UA \cdot T'$$

Rearranging into standard form:

$$\frac{m C_p}{UA} \frac{dT'}{dt} + T' = \frac{1}{UA} Q'$$

Identifying $\tau = \dfrac{m C_p}{UA}$ and $K_p = \dfrac{1}{UA}$, and taking the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):

$$\boxed{G_p(s) = \frac{T'(s)}{Q'(s)} = \frac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}}$$

For a step input $Q'(s) = \Delta Q / s$:

$$T'(t) = K_p \Delta Q \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

As $t \to \infty$, $T'_{ss} = K_p \Delta Q = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{UA}$ (finite). The process reaches a bounded new steady state → **self-regulating**.

**Case 2: Without Heat Loss (Non-Self-Regulating)**

Setting $UA = 0$ (perfect insulation):

$$m C_p \frac{dT'}{dt} = Q'$$

Transfer function:

$$\boxed{G_p(s) = \frac{1}{m C_p \, s} = \frac{K_p}{s}}$$

Step response:

$$T'(t) = \frac{\Delta Q}{m C_p} \, t \quad \text{(a pure ramp)}$$

As $t \to \infty$, $T'(t) \to \infty$ — no steady state. The process is **non-self-regulating (integrating)**.

**Comment:** The heat-loss term $UA \cdot T'$ is the dissipative element that provides the inherent negative feedback. Without it, the system cannot self-correct, and a controller becomes mandatory.

> **Mark Allocation:** Case 1 derivation (ODE → TF) = 2.5 marks; Step response + steady state = 1.5 marks; Case 2 derivation = 2 marks; Comment on self-regulation = 1 mark.

---

#### (b) A perfectly mixed tank contains **1000 kg** of a liquid with specific heat **$C_p = 2$ kJ/(kg·K)**. Heat is supplied at a rate of **$Q_0 = 50{,}000$ kJ/min** initially. The overall heat-loss coefficient is **$UA = 2000$ kJ/(min·K)**, and the ambient temperature is **$T_s = 30$ °C**. The heat input is then increased by 20%. Compute the new steady-state temperature, the time constant, and the transfer function. State whether the process is self-regulating. If the insulation is upgraded so that $UA \to 0$, what is the new behavior? `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Find the Initial Steady-State Temperature**

At initial steady state: $Q_0 = UA(T_0 - T_s)$

$$50{,}000 = 2000 \cdot (T_0 - 30) \implies T_0 - 30 = 25 \implies T_0 = 55 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Determine the New Heat Input**

Step change magnitude: $\Delta Q = 0.20 \times 50{,}000 = 10{,}000$ kJ/min

New heat input: $Q_{new} = 50{,}000 + 10{,}000 = 60{,}000$ kJ/min

**Step 3 — Compute the Process Gain and Time Constant**

$$K_p = \frac{1}{UA} = \frac{1}{2000} \text{ K·min/kJ} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K·min/kJ}$$

$$\tau = \frac{m C_p}{UA} = \frac{1000 \times 2}{2000} = 1 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the New Steady-State Temperature**

$$T'_{ss} = K_p \cdot \Delta Q = (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 10{,}000 = 5 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_{ss} = T_0 + T'_{ss} = 55 + 5 = \mathbf{60 \text{ °C}}$$

**Step 5 — Transfer Function**

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau s + 1} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{s + 1} \quad \text{(with units)}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion on Self-Regulation**

The pole is at $s = -1/\tau = -1$ min$^{-1}$ (left half-plane), and the steady-state value is **finite (60 °C)**. Therefore, the process is **SELF-REGULATING**.

**Step 7 — Behavior with Perfect Insulation ($UA \to 0$)**

If $UA \to 0$, then $\tau = m C_p / UA \to \infty$ and $K_p = 1/UA \to \infty$. The transfer function reduces to:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{1}{m C_p \, s} = \frac{1}{2000 \, s}$$

The step response becomes a ramp: $T'(t) = \dfrac{10{,}000}{2000} t = 5t$ °C, with no finite steady state. The process becomes **NON-SELF-REGULATING** — the temperature rises indefinitely until the fluid boils or an external controller intervenes.

> **Mark Allocation:** Initial steady state (Step 1) = 1 mark; $K_p$ and $\tau$ (Step 3) = 2 marks; New steady-state temperature (Step 4) = 1.5 marks; Transfer function and conclusion (Steps 5–6) = 1.5 marks; Insulation scenario (Step 7) = 1 mark.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Explain with suitable diagrams and equations the concept of self-regulating processes in **(i)** thermal systems, **(ii)** level systems, and **(iii)** pressure systems. Highlight the physical mechanism that confers self-regulating behavior in each case. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Thermal System (Self-Regulating):**
A heated tank with a heat-loss pathway to the surroundings. The governing equation is:

$$m C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = Q - UA(T - T_s)$$

The **physical mechanism** is **convective/radiative heat transfer to the surroundings**. As temperature $T$ rises, the driving force $(T - T_s)$ for heat loss increases, providing a natural restoring "force." Transfer function: $G_p(s) = K_p / (\tau s + 1)$.

**(ii) Level System (Self-Regulating):**
A tank with an outlet valve whose flow depends on the hydrostatic head. The governing equation is:

$$A \frac{dh}{dt} = F_{in} - \frac{h}{R}$$

The **physical mechanism** is **gravity-driven outflow proportional to head**. As level $h$ rises, the hydrostatic pressure on the outlet increases, raising $F_{out}$ until it equals $F_{in}$. Transfer function: $G_p(s) = R / (AR s + 1)$.

**(iii) Pressure System (Self-Regulating):**
A gas-filled vessel that leaks at a rate proportional to internal gauge pressure. The governing equation is:

$$V \frac{dP}{dt} = F_{in} - k P$$

The **physical mechanism** is **pressure-driven leakage** (laminar or turbulent leak through a small orifice). As $P$ rises, leakage increases until it balances inflow. Transfer function: $G_p(s) = 1/k \, / \left( \frac{V}{k} s + 1 \right)$.

**Common Feature:** In each case, the output variable ($T$, $h$, or $P$) directly influences the rate of outflow/loss, creating a built-in negative feedback loop. This is the **defining signature of self-regulating processes.**

> **Mark Allocation:** Each of the three systems (equation + mechanism) = 2 marks; Common feature / signature = 1 mark.

---

#### (b) A cylindrical tank of cross-sectional area **$A = 2 \text{ m}^2$** receives a constant inlet flow of **$F_{in,0} = 1 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$** and discharges through a valve of resistance **$R = 0.5 \text{ min/m}^2$**. The initial steady-state level is **$h_0 = 0.5$ m**. At $t = 0$, the inlet flow is suddenly increased to **$F_{in,1} = 1.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$**. Determine: **(i)** the new steady-state level, **(ii)** the time constant, and **(iii)** the level at $t = 2$ min. **(iv)** If the outlet valve is fully closed (so $R \to \infty$), determine the level at $t = 10$ min and state whether the process is self-regulating. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify Initial Steady State**

$h_0 = R \cdot F_{in,0} = 0.5 \times 1 = 0.5$ m ✓ (consistent with the given $h_0$)

**Step 2 — Compute Process Parameters**

Time constant:

$$\tau = AR = 2 \times 0.5 = 1 \text{ min}$$

Process gain:

$$K_p = R = 0.5 \text{ min/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Step Change Magnitude**

$$\Delta F_{in} = 1.5 - 1.0 = 0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 4 — New Steady-State Level**

$$h_{ss} = R \cdot F_{in,1} = 0.5 \times 1.5 = 0.75 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta h_{ss} = h_{ss} - h_0 = 0.75 - 0.5 = 0.25 \text{ m}$$

(Check: $K_p \cdot \Delta F_{in} = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25$ m ✓)

**Step 5 — Level at $t = 2$ min**

Using the first-order step response:

$$h(t) = h_{ss} - (h_{ss} - h_0) e^{-t/\tau} = 0.75 - 0.25 e^{-2/1}$$

$$h(2) = 0.75 - 0.25 \times 0.1353 = 0.75 - 0.0338 = \mathbf{0.7162 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 6 — Behavior with Closed Outlet ($R \to \infty$)**

With the outlet closed, the mass balance becomes:

$$A \frac{dh}{dt} = F_{in,1} \implies h(t) = h_0 + \frac{F_{in,1}}{A} t$$

$$h(10) = 0.5 + \frac{1.5}{2} \times 10 = 0.5 + 7.5 = \mathbf{8.0 \text{ m}}$$

Since the level grows linearly with time and has **no finite asymptote**, the process is **NON-SELF-REGULATING (Integrating)**.

> **Mark Allocation:** Verification + parameters (Steps 1–2) = 1.5 marks; New steady state (Step 4) = 1.5 marks; Level at 2 min (Step 5) = 2 marks; Closed-outlet case + classification (Step 6) = 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on This Topic — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the initial steady state.** When asked for "change in temperature" or "new level," students often compute $K_p \cdot \Delta Q$ but forget to add it to the initial value $T_0$ or $h_0$. This alone costs **1–1.5 marks** per sub-part.
>
> 2. **Confusing "process gain" with "transfer function."** The process gain $K_p$ is a *single number* (steady-state ratio). The transfer function $G_p(s)$ is a *function of $s$*. Examiners specifically test whether you can write $G_p(s) = K_p / (\tau s + 1)$ — writing only $K_p$ is incomplete.
>
> 3. **Wrong time-constant formula.** $\tau = R \cdot C$ (resistance × capacity) is universal. Forgetting this and writing $\tau = m/U A$ without the $C_p$ factor (for thermal systems) costs 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Misclassifying non-self-regulating behavior as "unstable."** A pure integrator is **marginally stable** (BIBO unstable to step inputs, but not exponentially unstable). Calling it "unstable" without qualification is a half-mark deduction in KTU valuation.
>
> 5. **Skipping the time-constant or pole-location comment.** A complete KTU answer must explicitly state the pole location ($s = -1/\tau$ for self-regulating, $s = 0$ for non-self-regulating) to earn full credit.
>
> 6. **Not identifying the dissipative mechanism.** The single most valuable sentence in any KTU answer on this topic is: *"The heat-loss term $UA(T - T_s)$ acts as inherent negative feedback, making the process self-regulating."* Omitting this reasoning costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Self-Regulating Definition:** Process settles at a new finite steady state on its own after a disturbance, due to an inherent dissipative mechanism. ✅
- **Non-Self-Regulating Definition:** Process output integrates (rises/falls continuously) without a controller; no finite asymptote. ✅
- **Mathematical Signature:** Pole at $s = -1/\tau$ (LHP) for self-regulating; pole at $s = 0$ for non-self-regulating. ✅
- **Canonical Transfer Functions:**
  - Self-Regulating: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$
  - Non-Self-Regulating: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{s}$
- **Step Responses:**
  - Self-Regulating: $y(t) = K_p \Delta u (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ — bounded exponential.
  - Non-Self-Regulating: $y(t) = K_p \Delta u \cdot t$ — unbounded ramp.
- **Universal Time Constant Formula:** $\tau = R \times C$ (resistance × capacity). ✅
- **Examples of Self-Regulating:** Open tank with outlet, heated tank with insulation losses, pressure vessel with relief, cooling coil in ambient air. ✅
- **Examples of Non-Self-Regulating:** Sealed tank, insulated (adiabatic) reactor, sealed gas cylinder, batch charging operation, thermometer bulb in vacuum. ✅
- **Dissipative Mechanisms:**
  - Thermal → heat loss to surroundings ($UA$).
  - Level → gravity-driven outflow through restriction.
  - Pressure → leakage through orifice proportional to gauge pressure.
- **Controller Implications:** Self-regulating → P-only often sufficient; Non-self-regulating → PI or PD mandatory, **never** P-only. ✅
- **Pole-Stability Mapping:** All poles in LHP = stable & self-regulating; any pole at $s = 0$ = marginally stable & non-self-regulating. ✅
- **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always quote the governing ODE, derive the TF, identify the pole, plot/describe the step response, and explicitly state the steady-state limit $t \to \infty$ to secure full marks. ✅

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interacting/Non-Interacting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET502 - PROCESS DYNAMICS AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Interacting/Non-Interacting # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) In **Process Dynamics and Control**, multi-capacity processes (typically liquid-level tanks in series) are classified into two broad categories based on the degree of physical coupling between adjacent stages: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Non-Interacting System:** A cascade of tanks in which the output (discharge) from the first tank depends **only on the liquid level of the first tank**. The downstream tank does not impose any back-pressure or hydrostatic influence on the upstream flow. The downstream level enters the picture **only after** the liquid has physically left the first tank through a separate outlet. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interacting System:** A cascade of tanks in which the outlet flow from the first tank is driven by the **difference in liquid levels** between the two tanks (`h₁ − h₂`). A change in `h₂` instantaneously alters the flow leaving tank 1, thereby coupling the two dynamic states. The tanks are *dynamically coupled* through the connecting hydraulic resistance. Mathematically, the discriminator is the form of the outflow from tank 1: $$F_1^{\text{(non-int)}} = \frac{h_1}{R_1}$$ $$F_1^{\text{(int)}} = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{R_1}$$ --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy **Imagine two scenarios in a kitchen sink plumbing system:** **Scenario A — Non-Interacting (Two Sinks with Independent Drains):** Picture two washbasins placed side by side. Each basin has its **own independent drain pipe** leading to a common sewer. If you suddenly open the second basin's tap, the water level in the first basin remains **completely undisturbed**, because basin 2 has no hydraulic link to basin 1. They behave as if they were the only basin in the world. This is *dynamic isolation*. **Scenario B — Interacting (Two Tanks Stacked / Coupled):** Now imagine two tanks connected by a single pipe. Water flows from the top tank into the bottom tank *through that pipe*. The rate of flow depends on the **height difference** between them. If the bottom tank's level rises, it chokes the flow from the top tank — so the top tank fills up faster. The two levels are *causally linked* in both directions. A disturbance in tank 2 echoes back into tank 1. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway for Students:** > - **Non-Interacting = Decoupled** dynamics; each stage's response is independent. > - **Interacting = Coupled** dynamics; a downstream change feeds back upstream. > - The **overall steady-state gain** of both systems is identical — only the **dynamic (transient) behaviour** differs. Interaction generally makes the system **slower** and the response **more sluggish**. --- ## 1.3 Physical Quantities of Interest | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | |:---:|:---|:---:| | $A_1, A_2$ | Cross-sectional area of tank 1, 2 | **m²** | | $R_1, R_2$ | Hydraulic resistance of outlet valve | **s/m²** | | $\tau_1 = A_1R_1$ | Time constant of tank 1 | **s** | | $\tau_2 = A_2R_2$ | Time constant of tank 2 | **s** | | $K = R_2$ | Overall steady-state gain | **m·s/m³** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of Non-Interacting Tanks Consider two tanks in series, but with the **first tank's outlet flow bypassing tank 2** via an independent line: - Step 1: Inflow $F_{in}(t)$ enters tank 1. - Step 2: Tank 1 discharges independently at a rate $F_1 = h_1/R_1$. - Step 3: A *portion* of $F_1$ (or the full stream after a splitter) enters tank 2 as its input. - Step 4: Tank 2 discharges at $F_2 = h_2/R_2$. **Why is this important?** Because the equation for tank 1 contains **only $h_1$** as a state variable on the right-hand side. Therefore, tank 1's transient can be solved **first**, completely, without knowing $h_2(t)$. The result is then fed as an input to tank 2. This *sequential independence* is what makes the system **non-interacting**. **Utility in Engineering:** - Easier to design controllers (loop-by-loop tuning). - Conservative design — interaction effects are neglected (always safe, but suboptimal). - Common in buffer tanks and surge vessels where back-pressure is minimised. --- ## 2.2 Operational Logic of Interacting Tanks In a true series arrangement, the connecting pipe is the *only* pathway between the two tanks: - Step 1: Inflow $F_{in}(t)$ enters tank 1. - Step 2: Tank 1 discharges through a connecting pipe at rate $F_1 = (h_1 - h_2)/R_1$. **Note the dependence on $h_2$.** - Step 3: This same stream $F_1$ is the *input* to tank 2. - Step 4: Tank 2 discharges at $F_2 = h_2/R_2$. **Why is this important?** The two state variables $h_1$ and $h_2$ appear *simultaneously* in the governing differential equations. They form a **coupled system of ODEs** that must be solved together, typically by Laplace-transform methods yielding a **second-order transfer function**. **Utility in Engineering:** - Realistic model for distillation column trays, reactor cascades, and most staged processes. - Critical for accurate controller tuning — neglecting interaction can lead to instability. - More sluggish dynamics → requires longer settling time in control loops. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU 2024 scheme exam questions on this topic. Memorise the structure, not just the formulas. ### Governing Equations (Linearised, Deviation Form) | System | Tank 1 ODE | Tank 2 ODE | |:---|:---|:---| | **Non-Interacting** | $\tau_1 \dfrac{dH_1}{dt} + H_1 = R_1\,Q_{in}$ | $\tau_2 \dfrac{dH_2}{dt} + H_2 = R_2 \cdot \dfrac{H_1}{R_1}$ | | **Interacting** | $\tau_1 \dfrac{dH_1}{dt} + H_1 - H_2 = R_1\,Q_{in}$ | $\tau_2 R_1 \dfrac{dH_2}{dt} + (R_1+R_2)H_2 = R_2\,H_1$ | ### Transfer Functions (Output $H_2(s)$ / Input $Q_{in}(s)$) | System | Transfer Function $G(s)$ | Order | Steady-State Gain | |:---|:---|:---:|:---:| | **Non-Interacting** | $G_{NI}(s) = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{(\tau_1 s+1)(\tau_2 s+1)}$ | 2nd | $R_1 R_2$ | | **Interacting** | $G_{I}(s) = \dfrac{R_2}{\tau_1 \tau_2 s^2 + (\tau_1 + \tau_2 + A_1 R_2)\,s + 1}$ | 2nd | $R_2$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical observation:** The **steady-state gain differs** between the two systems if the upstream resistance $R_1$ is *not* included in the gain definition. The non-interacting system has gain $R_1R_2$, while the interacting system has gain $R_2$. This is because in the non-interacting case, only a fraction of $F_1$ enters tank 2; in the interacting case, the **entire** $F_1$ enters tank 2. ### Comparison of Dynamic Response (For Equal Tanks: $A_1 = A_2 = A$, $R_1 = R_2 = R$) | Property | Non-Interacting | Interacting | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Characteristic equation | $(\tau s + 1)^2 = 0$ | $\tau^2 s^2 + 3\tau s + 1 = 0$ | | Effective time constants | $\tau, \tau$ (equal, $\tau = AR$) | $\tau_a, \tau_b$ (unequal, both $> \tau$) | | Roots | $s = -1/\tau$ (double) | $s = \dfrac{-3 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2\tau}$ | | Relative speed | **Faster** | **Slower** | | Damping | Critically damped | Overdamped | | System type | Two equal 1st-order lags in series | Single 2nd-order overdamped system | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Non-Interacting Transfer Function ### Step 1: Write the Mass Balance Around Tank 1 Accumulation = Inflow − Outflow $$A_1 \frac{dh_1}{dt} = F_{in} - \frac{h_1}{R_1}$$ The outflow depends **only on $h_1$** because the outlet of tank 1 is a free discharge to atmosphere (or to a line whose pressure does not depend on $h_2$). ### Step 2: Rearrange Into Standard First-Order Form Multiply both sides by $R_1$: $$A_1 R_1 \frac{dh_1}{dt} + h_1 = R_1 F_{in}$$ Recognising the time constant $\tau_1 = A_1 R_1$: $$\tau_1 \frac{dh_1}{dt} + h_1 = R_1 F_{in}$$ ### Step 3: Take the Laplace Transform (Zero Initial Conditions) Using $\mathcal{L}\{dh_1/dt\} = s H_1(s)$ and $\mathcal{L}\{F_{in}\} = F_{in}(s)$: $$\tau_1 s H_1(s) + H_1(s) = R_1 F_{in}(s)$$ Factor $H_1(s)$: $$H_1(s)\bigl(\tau_1 s + 1\bigr) = R_1 F_{in}(s)$$ ### Step 4: Isolate the Transfer Function for Tank 1 $$\frac{H_1(s)}{F_{in}(s)} = \frac{R_1}{\tau_1 s + 1}$$ ### Step 5: Write the Mass Balance Around Tank 2 The input to tank 2 is the *flow leaving tank 1* divided by the splitter ratio, but in the standard textbook formulation, a *fraction* $F_1/R_1$ (with appropriate gain) enters tank 2. For the canonical non-interacting system where all flow enters tank 2: $$A_2 \frac{dh_2}{dt} = \frac{h_1}{R_1} - \frac{h_2}{R_2}$$ Multiplying by $R_2$: $$\tau_2 \frac{dh_2}{dt} + h_2 = \frac{R_2}{R_1} h_1$$ ### Step 6: Laplace Transform of Tank 2 Equation $$H_2(s)(\tau_2 s + 1) = \frac{R_2}{R_1} H_1(s)$$ ### Step 7: Cascade the Two Transfer Functions The output of tank 1 is the input to tank 2, so we multiply: $$\frac{H_2(s)}{F_{in}(s)} = \frac{H_2(s)}{H_1(s)} \cdot \frac{H_1(s)}{F_{in}(s)}$$ $$\frac{H_2(s)}{F_{in}(s)} = \frac{R_2/R_1}{\tau_2 s + 1} \cdot \frac{R_1}{\tau_1 s + 1}$$ $$\boxed{\,G_{NI}(s) = \frac{R_1 R_2}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}\,}$$ The two 1st-order factors are **completely independent** — no cross-terms, no coupling. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Interacting Transfer Function ### Step 1: Mass Balance on Tank 1 (Coupled) The flow through the connecting pipe depends on the head difference: $$A_1 \frac{dh_1}{dt} = F_{in} - \frac{h_1 - h_2}{R_1}$$ Note the **$h_2$ term** in the outflow — this is the source of interaction. ### Step 2: Rearrange to Expose the Coupling Multiply by $R_1$: $$A_1 R_1 \frac{dh_1}{dt} + h_1 - h_2 = R_1 F_{in}$$ In terms of $\tau_1$: $$\tau_1 \frac{dh_1}{dt} + h_1 - h_2 = R_1 F_{in} \quad \cdots (1)$$ ### Step 3: Mass Balance on Tank 2 $$A_2 \frac{dh_2}{dt} = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{R_1} - \frac{h_2}{R_2}$$ Multiply by $R_1 R_2$ to clear denominators: $$A_2 R_1 R_2 \frac{dh_2}{dt} = R_2 h_1 - R_2 h_2 - R_1 h_2$$ Recognising $A_2 R_2 = \tau_2$, the LHS becomes $\tau_2 R_1 \dfrac{dh_2}{dt}$: $$\tau_2 R_1 \frac{dh_2}{dt} + (R_1 + R_2) h_2 = R_2 h_1 \quad \cdots (2)$$ ### Step 4: Laplace Transform Both Equations $$(\tau_1 s + 1) H_1(s) - H_2(s) = R_1 Q_{in}(s) \quad \cdots (1')$$ $$(\tau_2 R_1 s + R_1 + R_2) H_2(s) = R_2 H_1(s) \quad \cdots (2')$$ ### Step 5: Eliminate $H_1(s)$ by Substitution From equation (2'), solve for $H_1(s)$: $$H_1(s) = \frac{\tau_2 R_1 s + R_1 + R_2}{R_2} H_2(s)$$ ### Step 6: Substitute into Equation (1') $$(\tau_1 s + 1) \cdot \frac{\tau_2 R_1 s + R_1 + R_2}{R_2} \cdot H_2(s) - H_2(s) = R_1 Q_{in}(s)$$ Factor $H_2(s)$ on the LHS: $$\left[\frac{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 R_1 s + R_1 + R_2) - R_2}{R_2}\right] H_2(s) = R_1 Q_{in}(s)$$ ### Step 7: Expand the Numerator Polynomial $$(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 R_1 s + R_1 + R_2) = \tau_1 \tau_2 R_1 s^2 + \tau_1 (R_1 + R_2) s + \tau_2 R_1 s + (R_1 + R_2)$$ Combine the linear $s$ terms: $$= \tau_1 \tau_2 R_1 s^2 + \bigl[\tau_1 (R_1 + R_2) + \tau_2 R_1\bigr] s + (R_1 + R_2)$$ ### Step 8: Subtract $R_2$ (from the algebra of step 6) $$\bigl[\tau_1 \tau_2 R_1 s^2 + (\tau_1 R_1 + \tau_1 R_2 + \tau_2 R_1) s + (R_1 + R_2)\bigr] - R_2$$ $$= \tau_1 \tau_2 R_1 s^2 + (\tau_1 R_1 + \tau_1 R_2 + \tau_2 R_1) s + R_1$$ ### Step 9: Factor $R_1$ from the Result $$= R_1 \bigl[\tau_1 \tau_2 s^2 + (\tau_1 + \tau_2 + \tau_1 R_2 / R_1) s + 1\bigr]$$ ### Step 10: Form the Final Transfer Function The LHS of step 6 is $\frac{1}{R_2} \cdot R_1 [\cdots] \cdot H_2(s)$, so: $$H_2(s) = \frac{R_1 R_2 Q_{in}(s)}{R_1 \bigl[\tau_1 \tau_2 s^2 + (\tau_1 + \tau_2 + \tau_1 R_2 / R_1) s + 1\bigr]}$$ $$H_2(s) = \frac{R_2 Q_{in}(s)}{\tau_1 \tau_2 s^2 + (\tau_1 + \tau_2 + A_1 R_2) s + 1}$$ (using $\tau_1 R_2 / R_1 = A_1 R_1 R_2 / R_1 = A_1 R_2$) $$\boxed{\,G_I(s) = \frac{R_2}{\tau_1 \tau_2 s^2 + (\tau_1 + \tau_2 + A_1 R_2)\, s + 1}\,}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Time Constants of the Interacting System The denominator of $G_I(s)$ can be factored as $(\tau_a s + 1)(\tau_b s + 1)$ where: $$\tau_a \tau_b = \tau_1 \tau_2$$ $$\tau_a + \tau_b = \tau_1 + \tau_2 + A_1 R_2$$ For **equal tanks** ($A_1 = A_2 = A$, $R_1 = R_2 = R$): - $\tau_1 \tau_2 = A^2 R^2$ - $\tau_1 + \tau_2 + A_1 R_2 = 2AR + AR = 3AR$ So the denominator becomes: $$\tau^2 s^2 + 3\tau s + 1 = 0 \quad \text{where } \tau = AR$$ Solving the quadratic in $s$: $$s = \frac{-3 \pm \sqrt{9 - 4}}{2\tau} = \frac{-3 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2\tau}$$ Therefore, the two effective time constants are: $$\tau_a = \frac{2\tau}{3 - \sqrt{5}} \approx 2.618\,\tau, \quad \tau_b = \frac{2\tau}{3 + \sqrt{5}} \approx 0.382\,\tau$$ > [!NOTE] > The interacting system behaves like a cascade of **two unequal first-order lags**: one **larger** than $\tau$ (dominant pole) and one **smaller** than $\tau$. The dominant lag is $\tau_a \approx 2.618\tau$, meaning the system is **2.618× slower** than a single tank. ### Cross-Verification with Non-Interacting Case For the non-interacting system with equal tanks: $$G_{NI}(s) = \frac{R^2}{(\tau s + 1)^2} = \frac{R^2}{\tau^2 s^2 + 2\tau s + 1}$$ Both poles are at $s = -1/\tau$, confirming a **critically damped** response with time constant $\tau$. The interacting system has the **same** product $\tau_a \tau_b = \tau^2$ but a **larger** sum $\tau_a + \tau_b = 3\tau$. By the AM-GM inequality, the dominant root must be larger than $\tau$ — a clear mathematical proof that interaction slows the response. # 4. The dominant pole dictates the overall settling time. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Distinguish between interacting and non-interacting systems in process control. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (≈ 3 marks, 80–100 words):** | Aspect | Non-Interacting | Interacting | |:---|:---|:---| | Coupling | Tanks are dynamically decoupled | Tanks are dynamically coupled | | Outflow from tank 1 | $q_1 = h_1/R_1$ | $q_1 = (h_1 - h_2)/R_1$ | | Equations | Sequential, independent | Simultaneous ODEs | | Transfer function | Product of 1st-order lags | Single 2nd-order form | | **Example** | Buffer tanks with independent drains | Distillation column trays in series | (1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for examples) --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: For two identical non-interacting tanks in series, derive the overall transfer function relating the exit level $H_2(s)$ to the inlet flow rate $Q(s)$.** **Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):** For each tank (area $A$, resistance $R$, time constant $\tau = AR$): $$\frac{H_1(s)}{Q(s)} = \frac{R}{\tau s + 1}, \quad \frac{H_2(s)}{H_1(s)} = \frac{R}{\tau s + 1}$$ Cascading: $$\boxed{\frac{H_2(s)}{Q(s)} = \frac{R^2}{(\tau s + 1)^2}}$$ (1 mark for individual tank TF, 1 mark for cascading, 1 mark for final form) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read This Carefully!** > 1. **Do not** confuse the steady-state gain of non-interacting ($R_1 R_2$) with that of interacting ($R_2$). Many students write $R_1 R_2$ for both, losing **2 marks** instantly. > 2. **Always** substitute $\tau = AR$ explicitly when finalising answers — leaving $\tau$ undefined in terms of physical parameters costs **1 mark** in many KTU papers. > 3. The step response of the interacting system is **slower**, not faster. Marks are deducted for stating the opposite. > 4. Always **derive the transfer function** in the answer unless the question explicitly says "obtain" without derivation. --- ### **Question B1 (14 Marks) — Option A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Derive the transfer function of two interacting tanks in series. Compare its dynamic response with the non-interacting case for identical tanks. Show that interaction makes the system slower.** **Solution:** #### Part (a) — Derivation of the Interacting Transfer Function [7 marks] **Step 1: Mass balance on tank 1** (1 mark) $$A_1 \frac{dh_1}{dt} = q - \frac{h_1 - h_2}{R_1}$$ Multiplying by $R_1$ and using $\tau_1 = A_1R_1$: $$\tau_1 \frac{dh_1}{dt} + h_1 - h_2 = R_1 q$$ **Step 2: Mass balance on tank 2** (1 mark) $$A_2 \frac{dh_2}{dt} = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{R_1} - \frac{h_2}{R_2}$$ Multiplying by $R_1R_2$: $$\tau_2 R_1 \frac{dh_2}{dt} + (R_1 + R_2) h_2 = R_2 h_1$$ **Step 3: Laplace transform** (1 mark) $$(\tau_1 s + 1) H_1(s) - H_2(s) = R_1 Q(s) \quad \cdots (i)$$ $$(\tau_2 R_1 s + R_1 + R_2) H_2(s) = R_2 H_1(s) \quad \cdots (ii)$$ **Step 4: Eliminate $H_1(s)$** (2 marks) From (ii): $H_1(s) = \dfrac{(\tau_2 R_1 s + R_1 + R_2)}{R_2} H_2(s)$ Substituting in (i): $$\left[\frac{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 R_1 s + R_1 + R_2) - R_2}{R_2}\right] H_2(s) = R_1 Q(s)$$ **Step 5: Simplify to get final TF** (2 marks) Expanding and simplifying (as in Section 3.2): $$\frac{H_2(s)}{Q(s)} = \frac{R_2}{\tau_1 \tau_2 s^2 + (\tau_1 + \tau_2 + A_1 R_2) s + 1}$$ $$\boxed{G_I(s) = \frac{R_2}{\tau_1 \tau_2 s^2 + (\tau_1 + \tau_2 + A_1 R_2)\,s + 1}}$$ #### Part (b) — Comparison with Non-Interacting Case [7 marks] **Step 1: Non-interacting TF for identical tanks** (2 marks) For $A_1 = A_2 = A$, $R_1 = R_2 = R$, $\tau = AR$: $$G_{NI}(s) = \frac{R^2}{(\tau s + 1)^2} = \frac{R^2}{\tau^2 s^2 + 2\tau s + 1}$$ **Step 2: Interacting TF for identical tanks** (2 marks) With $A_1R_2 = AR = \tau$: $$G_I(s) = \frac{R}{\tau^2 s^2 + 3\tau s + 1} \times R = \frac{R^2}{\tau^2 s^2 + 3\tau s + 1}$$ **Step 3: Factor the denominators to compare time constants** (2 marks) - Non-interacting: roots at $s = -1/\tau$ (double pole) → time constants both equal to $\tau$. - Interacting: solve $\tau^2 s^2 + 3\tau s + 1 = 0$: $$s = \frac{-3\tau \pm \sqrt{9\tau^2 - 4\tau^2}}{2\tau^2} = \frac{-3 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2\tau}$$ So $\tau_a = \dfrac{2\tau}{3 - \sqrt{5}} \approx 2.618\,\tau$ and $\tau_b = \dfrac{2\tau}{3 + \sqrt{5}} \approx 0.382\,\tau$. **Step 4: Conclusion** (1 mark) The dominant time constant of the interacting system ($2.618\tau$) is **larger** than that of the non-interacting system ($\tau$).
