Therefore, interaction makes the response **slower** and the system requires more time to reach steady state. The interacting system has a *split double pole* into two real poles — one dominant (slow) and one minor (fast). --- ### **Question B2 (14 Marks) — Option B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Two tanks of equal cross-section are connected in series. Tank 1 has area $A_1 = 1$ m² and resistance $R_1 = 2$ s/m²; Tank 2 has area $A_2 = 2$ m² and resistance $R_2 = 1$ s/m². Determine the transfer function $H_2(s)/Q(s)$ for (i) the non-interacting and (ii) the interacting arrangement. Comment on the difference in dynamic response.** **Solution:** #### Given Data - $A_1 = 1$ m², $R_1 = 2$ s/m² → $\tau_1 = A_1 R_1 = 2$ s - $A_2 = 2$ m², $R_2 = 1$ s/m² → $\tau_2 = A_2 R_2 = 2$ s #### Part (a) — Non-Interacting Transfer Function [7 marks] **Step 1:** Each tank acts as a first-order lag. $$\frac{H_1(s)}{Q(s)} = \frac{R_1}{\tau_1 s + 1} = \frac{2}{2s + 1}$$ $$\frac{H_2(s)}{H_1(s)} = \frac{R_2/R_1}{\tau_2 s + 1} = \frac{0.5}{2s + 1}$$ **Step 2:** Cascade multiplication. $$\frac{H_2(s)}{Q(s)} = \frac{2 \times 0.5}{(2s + 1)^2} = \frac{1}{(2s + 1)^2}$$ **Step 3:** Simplify denominator. $$(2s+1)^2 = 4s^2 + 4s + 1$$ $$\boxed{G_{NI}(s) = \frac{1}{4s^2 + 4s + 1}}$$ (1 mark each for individual TFs, 2 marks for cascade, 1 mark for simplification, 2 marks for comparison setup) #### Part (b) — Interacting Transfer Function [7 marks] **Step 1:** Apply the interacting-tank formula with $A_1 R_2 = 1 \times 1 = 1$ s. $$G_I(s) = \frac{R_2}{\tau_1 \tau_2 s^2 + (\tau_1 + \tau_2 + A_1 R_2) s + 1}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute numerical values. - $\tau_1 \tau_2 = 2 \times 2 = 4$ - $\tau_1 + \tau_2 + A_1 R_2 = 2 + 2 + 1 = 5$ $$G_I(s) = \frac{1}{4s^2 + 5s + 1}$$ **Step 3:** Find the effective time constants (poles). Solve $4s^2 + 5s + 1 = 0$: $$s = \frac{-5 \pm \sqrt{25 - 16}}{8} = \frac{-5 \pm 3}{8}$$ So $s_1 = -0.25$ and $s_2 = -1$. Therefore: $$\tau_a = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4 \text{ s}, \quad \tau_b = \frac{1}{1} = 1 \text{ s}$$ **Step 4:** Compare with non-interacting poles. Non-interacting poles (from $4s^2 + 4s + 1 = 0$): $$s = \frac{-4 \pm 0}{8} = -0.5 \text{ (double pole)}$$ So non-interacting has a **double pole at $-0.5$** (time constant 2 s on each side). **Step 5:** Comment on dynamic response. - The non-interacting system has both poles at $-0.5$, giving a critically damped response with time constant 2 s. - The interacting system has poles at $-0.25$ and $-1$. The pole at $-0.25$ is **dominant** (closest to the imaginary axis) and gives a time constant of **4 s** — *twice* as slow as the non-interacting case. - **Conclusion:** Interaction has split the double pole into a slow pole ($-0.25$) and a fast pole ($-1$). The slow pole dominates the response, making the interacting system **markedly slower** to reach steady state. (2 marks for TF, 2 marks for pole calculation, 2 marks for comment, 1 mark for correct final statement) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorise Before Exam** - [ ] **Non-Interacting:** $q_1 = h_1/R_1$ → transfer function is a **product** of two 1st-order lags. - [ ] **Interacting:** $q_1 = (h_1 - h_2)/R_1$ → transfer function is a **single 2nd-order** form. - [ ] **Non-Interacting TF:** $G_{NI}(s) = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}$ - [ ] **Interacting TF:** $G_I(s) = \dfrac{R_2}{\tau_1 \tau_2 s^2 + (\tau_1 + \tau_2 + A_1 R_2) s + 1}$ - [ ] **Steady-state gain** of interacting system is $R_2$, **not** $R_1 R_2$. - [ ] **Time constant** of a tank: $\tau = A \times R$ (area × resistance). - [ ] For **equal tanks** ($A_1 = A_2 = A$, $R_1 = R_2 = R$): the interacting system has effective time constants $\tau_a \approx 2.618 \tau$ and $\tau_b \approx 0.382 \tau$, summing to $3\tau$. - [ ] **Interaction always slows the response** — dominant pole moves closer to the imaginary axis. - [ ] The interacting system is **overdamped** (for positive $A, R$); the non-interacting system is **critically damped** for equal tanks. - [ ] The **AM-GM inequality** rigorously proves that the dominant time constant in the interacting case exceeds that in the non-interacting case. - [ ] In KTU exam answers, always **show the Laplace transform step explicitly** — it carries 2–3 marks alone. - [ ] Always substitute $\tau_i = A_i R_i$ at the final step of the derivation for full marks. - [ ] Real-world examples: distillation columns (interacting), buffer tanks with separate outlets (non-interacting).

topics.title: Linear/Non-Linear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET502 - PROCESS DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linear/Non-Linear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Linear and Non-Linear Systems

In **Process Dynamics and Control (PCAET502)**, the classification of a system or process as **Linear** or **Non-Linear** is the very first step in mathematical modeling. Almost all real chemical/physical processes are inherently **non-linear**, but we **linearize** them around a steady-state operating point to apply classical control theory (Laplace Transforms, Transfer Functions, Bode Plots).

---

## 1.2 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

### Linear System
A system described by a differential equation is said to be **linear** if it satisfies the two fundamental mathematical properties of **Superposition** and **Homogeneity (Scaling)**. In simple terms, a linear system produces an output that is **directly proportional** to the input, and the principle of superposition holds true.

### Non-Linear System
A system is called **non-linear** if it does **NOT** satisfy the superposition principle. The output is **not directly proportional** to the input, and the principle of superposition **cannot be applied**. Most physical processes exhibit some form of non-linearity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Statement:** *A system governed by a differential equation of the form*
> $$a_n \frac{d^n y}{dt^n} + a_{n-1} \frac{d^{n-1} y}{dt^{n-1}} + \dots + a_1 \frac{dy}{dt} + a_0 y = f(t)$$
> *is **linear** if all coefficients $a_0, a_1, \dots, a_n$ are either **constants** or functions of the **independent variable only** (e.g., time $t$). If any coefficient is a function of the **dependent variable** $y$ or its derivatives, the system is **non-linear**.*

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **car's steering wheel**:
- A **linear steering system** means if you turn the wheel by **10°**, the car turns by exactly **one unit**, and turning by **20°** turns the car by exactly **two units**. The relationship is perfectly proportional, like a perfectly straight ruler.
- A **non-linear steering system** (power steering at very high speeds) means turning the wheel by **10°** might turn the car by **5 units**, but turning by **20°** might turn it by **25 units** (a disproportionate response). The "ruler" is bent or curved.

In chemical processes, this is like a **tank level vs. flow rate** relationship:
- If the outflow is through a **pump** (constant flow), Level $\propto$ (Inflow - Outflow) → **Linear**.
- If the outflow is through a **valve/orifice**, Outflow $\propto \sqrt{\text{Level}}$ → **Non-Linear** (square root function).

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - The **Steady-State Operating Point** (denoted with an overbar, e.g., $\bar{x}$, $\bar{y}$) is the foundation of linearization. **All linearization is performed around this point.**
> - **Deviation Variables** (denoted by capital letters like $X$, $Y$) are used to express variables as deviations from their steady-state values. This is a standard KTU notation: $X = x - \bar{x}$.
> - The **linearization error** is proportional to the square of the deviation from the steady state. Smaller deviations $\Rightarrow$ better linear approximation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Conditions for Linearity

A system $y = f(x)$ is **linear if and only if** it satisfies both of these properties for all inputs $x_1$ and $x_2$ and any scalar $\alpha$:

### Property 1: Additivity (Superposition)
$$f(x_1 + x_2) = f(x_1) + f(x_2)$$

### Property 2: Homogeneity (Scaling)
$$f(\alpha x) = \alpha f(x)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Combined Test (Most Common in KTU Exams):**
> $$f(\alpha x_1 + \beta x_2) = \alpha f(x_1) + \beta f(x_2)$$
> If this combined condition holds, the system is **linear**. Otherwise, it is **non-linear**.

### Logic Steps for Checking Linearity:
1. Identify the input-output relationship $y = f(x)$.
2. Apply the combined homogeneity-additivity test.
3. If the function contains $x^2$, $\sqrt{x}$, $\sin(x)$, $e^x$, $\ln(x)$, $1/x$, or any product of variables, it is **almost always non-linear**.
4. If the function is a polynomial of degree 1 (i.e., $y = ax + b$), it is **linear** (affine linear).

---

## 2.2 Types of Non-Linearities

### 2.2.1 Inherent (Natural) Non-Linearities
These arise from fundamental physical/chemical laws:
- **Square Root / Orifice Flow:** $F_{out} = C_d A \sqrt{2g h}$ (flow through valve)
- **Arrhenius Reaction Rate:** $k = k_0 e^{-E/RT}$
- **Radiation Heat Transfer:** $q \propto T^4$ (Stefan-Boltzmann Law)
- **Vapor Pressure (Antoine Equation):** Non-linear in temperature
- **Gas Density:** $\rho = \frac{PM}{RT}$ (involves division and multiplication)

### 2.2.2 Artificial (Intentional) Non-Linearities
These are introduced by control elements:
- **Relay / On-Off Control:** Output is either fully ON or fully OFF.
- **Dead Zone:** No response until input crosses a threshold.
- **Saturation:** Output cannot exceed physical limits (e.g., a valve cannot open more than 100%).

> [!WARNING]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Classify the non-linearity as inherent or artificial."* A valve saturation is **artificial**, but the orifice equation is **inherent**.

---

## 2.3 Why Linearize? The Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Linear systems have analytical solutions** — we can apply **Laplace Transforms**, get **Transfer Functions**, and design **P/PI/PID controllers** easily.
- **Non-linear systems generally require numerical simulation** (e.g., MATLAB/SIMULINK), which is computationally expensive.
- **Linearization** lets us use powerful classical tools: **stability analysis (Routh-Hurwitz, Root Locus, Bode, Nyquist)**.
- This is the **practical foundation of all process control** taught in undergraduate programs.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description / Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Linearity Test | $f(\alpha x_1 + \beta x_2) = \alpha f(x_1) + \beta f(x_2)$ | Combined additivity & homogeneity |
| 2 | Deviation Variable | $X(t) = x(t) - \bar{x}$ | Dimensionless / Same as $x$ |
| 3 | Linearization (Taylor Series, 1st Order) | $y \approx \bar{y} + \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right\vert_{\bar{x}} (x - \bar{x})$ | Valid for small deviations |
| 4 | Linearized Output in Deviation Form | $Y(t) = K \cdot X(t)$ | Where $K = \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right\vert_{\bar{x}}$ |
| 5 | Orifice Flow (Non-Linear) | $F = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P}$ | Typical control valve equation |
| 6 | Multi-Variable Linearization | $Y \approx \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}\right\vert_{\bar{}} X_1 + \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2}\right\vert_{\bar{}} X_2 + \dots$ | First-order partial derivatives |
| 7 | Arrhenius Rate (Non-Linear) | $k = k_0 e^{-E/RT}$ | $E$ in J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Substitution Rule:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for "evaluated at" instead of `|` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax in your own notes.

---

## 2.5 Multi-Variable Linearization (Most Important for KTU 14-mark Questions)

For a function of multiple variables, the linearized form using **first-order Taylor series expansion** is:

$$f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \approx f(\bar{x}_1, \bar{x}_2, \dots, \bar{x}_n) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\right\vert_{\bar{x}} (x_i - \bar{x}_i)$$

In **deviation variable form** (where $Y = y - \bar{y}$ and $X_i = x_i - \bar{x}_i$):

$$Y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\right\vert_{\bar{x}} X_i$$

This is the **master equation** for linearization in process control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Linearization of a Single-Variable Function

### Problem Setup
Consider a non-linear static function $y = f(x)$. We want to find an approximate **linear** relationship $y \approx a x + b$ that is valid in a small neighborhood around the steady-state operating point $\bar{x}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Write the Taylor Series Expansion of $f(x)$ about $\bar{x}$**

The infinite Taylor series of a function $f(x)$ expanded about the point $\bar{x}$ is:

$$f(x) = f(\bar{x}) + f'(\bar{x})(x - \bar{x}) + \frac{f''(\bar{x})}{2!}(x - \bar{x})^2 + \frac{f'''(\bar{x})}{3!}(x - \bar{x})^3 + \dots$$

**Step 2: Truncate to First-Order (Linear Approximation)**

For small deviations from the steady state, the higher-order terms $(x - \bar{x})^2, (x - \bar{x})^3, \dots$ are **negligible** (since a small number squared is even smaller). We retain only the first two terms:

$$f(x) \approx f(\bar{x}) + f'(\bar{x})(x - \bar{x})$$

**Step 3: Recognize the Steady-State Value**

At steady state, $y = \bar{y}$ when $x = \bar{x}$. Therefore, $f(\bar{x}) = \bar{y}$. Substituting:

$$y \approx \bar{y} + f'(\bar{x})(x - \bar{x})$$

**Step 4: Convert to Deviation Variable Form**

Define deviation variables:
$$Y = y - \bar{y} \quad \text{and} \quad X = x - \bar{x}$$

Subtracting $\bar{y}$ from both sides:

$$y - \bar{y} \approx f'(\bar{x})(x - \bar{x})$$

$$Y \approx K \cdot X$$

where the **linearized gain** $K$ is the partial derivative evaluated at the steady state:

$$K = f'(\bar{x}) = \left.\frac{df}{dx}\right\vert_{x = \bar{x}}$$

This is the **linearized first-order system** in deviation variable form.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Linearization of the Orifice Equation (Classic KTU Problem)

### Problem Statement
The volumetric flow rate $F$ through a valve is given by:
$$F = C \sqrt{P}$$
where $C$ is a constant, and $P$ is the upstream pressure. Linearize this equation about the steady-state values $\bar{F}$ and $\bar{P}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Write the function explicitly**

$$F(P) = C \sqrt{P} = C P^{1/2}$$

**Step 2: Identify the steady-state relationship**

At steady state:
$$\bar{F} = C \sqrt{\bar{P}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the partial derivative $\frac{dF}{dP}$**

$$\frac{dF}{dP} = C \cdot \frac{1}{2} P^{-1/2} = \frac{C}{2\sqrt{P}}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the derivative at the steady state**

$$\left.\frac{dF}{dP}\right\vert_{\bar{P}} = \frac{C}{2\sqrt{\bar{P}}}$$

**Step 5: Use the linearization formula**

$$F \approx \bar{F} + \left.\frac{dF}{dP}\right\vert_{\bar{P}} (P - \bar{P})$$

**Step 6: Substitute the derivative value**

$$F \approx \bar{F} + \frac{C}{2\sqrt{\bar{P}}} (P - \bar{P})$$

**Step 7: Convert to deviation variables**

Let $F' = F - \bar{F}$ and $P' = P - \bar{P}$. Then:

$$F' = \frac{C}{2\sqrt{\bar{P}}} P'$$

**Step 8: Express the linearized gain $K$ in terms of $\bar{F}$**

Since $\bar{F} = C \sqrt{\bar{P}}$, we have $C = \bar{F} / \sqrt{\bar{P}}$. Substituting:

$$K = \frac{C}{2\sqrt{\bar{P}}} = \frac{\bar{F}/ \sqrt{\bar{P}}}{2\sqrt{\bar{P}}} = \frac{\bar{F}}{2\bar{P}}$$

**Final Linearized Equation:**

$$F' = \frac{\bar{F}}{2\bar{P}} \cdot P'$$

This is the **classic KTU result** — a non-linear square-root function is linearized into a proportional relationship where the gain depends on the operating point.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Multi-Variable Linearization (Tank Heating)

### Problem Statement
The temperature $T$ of a tank with mass $m$ and specific heat $C_p$ is heated by a steam coil with heat transfer coefficient $U$ and area $A$. The temperature is governed by:
$$m C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = U A (T_s - T) - F \rho C_p (T - T_i)$$

Linearize this equation about the steady state.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Substitute the non-linear terms explicitly**

The non-linear term here is the heat input $UA(T_s - T)$, which is **linear in $T$** but the system is non-linear because $T$ is multiplied by $F$ in the outflow term. The fully expanded form:

$$m C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = U A T_s - U A T - F \rho C_p T + F \rho C_p T_i$$

**Step 2: Identify deviation variables**

Let:
$$T' = T - \bar{T}, \quad F' = F - \bar{F}, \quad T_s' = T_s - \bar{T}_s, \quad T_i' = T_i - \bar{T}_i$$

**Step 3: Expand in deviation form by substituting $T = \bar{T} + T'$, etc.**

For the term $F T$, the product of two deviation variables $F' \cdot T'$ is **second-order** and is neglected (linearization assumption):

$$FT \approx \bar{F} \bar{T} + \bar{F} T' + \bar{T} F'$$

**Step 4: Apply the linearization to each term**

The original equation in deviations becomes:

$$m C_p \frac{dT'}{dt} = U A (T_s' - T') - \bar{F} \rho C_p T' - \rho C_p (\bar{T} - T_i) F' + \bar{F} \rho C_p T_i'$$

(Note: the $F' T'$ cross-term is dropped.)

**Step 5: Rearrange in standard linear ODE form**

$$m C_p \frac{dT'}{dt} + (U A + \bar{F} \rho C_p) T' = U A T_s' - \rho C_p (\bar{T} - T_i) F' + \bar{F} \rho C_p T_i'$$

**Final Linearized Equation:**

$$\tau \frac{dT'}{dt} + T' = K_1 T_s' + K_2 F' + K_3 T_i'$$

where the **time constant** and **gains** are functions of the steady state:

$$\tau = \frac{m C_p}{U A + \bar{F} \rho C_p}$$

$$K_1 = \frac{U A}{U A + \bar{F} \rho C_p}, \quad K_2 = \frac{-\rho C_p (\bar{T} - T_i)}{U A + \bar{F} \rho C_p}, \quad K_3 = \frac{\bar{F} \rho C_p}{U A + \bar{F} \rho C_p}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Classification Quick Reference

| Function Form | Linearity | Reason | Example |
|---------------|-----------|--------|---------|
| $y = ax + b$ | Linear | Polynomial of degree 1 | Ohm's Law: $V = IR$ |
| $y = a x^2$ | Non-Linear | $x^2$ term | Tank area $\propto$ height$^2$ |
| $y = \sqrt{x}$ | Non-Linear | Square root | Orifice flow: $F = C\sqrt{h}$ |
| $y = a \sin(x)$ | Non-Linear | Trigonometric | Pendulum motion (large angles) |
| $y = e^x$ | Non-Linear | Exponential | Arrhenius rate constant |
| $y = 1/x$ | Non-Linear | Reciprocal | Gas density vs. pressure |
| $y = x_1 \cdot x_2$ | Non-Linear (bilinear) | Product of variables | Power: $P = V \cdot I$ |
| $y = x_1 + x_2$ | Linear | Sum of variables | Mass balance: $F_{in} + F_{gen}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Tree of Process Systems

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete classification of systems as covered in KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process System]:::startNode
    A --> B{Does it satisfy Superposition?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Linear System]:::linearNode
    B -->|No| D[Non-Linear System]:::nonLinearNode

    C --> C1[Time-Invariant LTI]
    C --> C2[Time-Varying Linear]
    C --> C3[Linearized Approximation]

    D --> D1[Inherent Non-Linearity]:::subNode
    D --> D2[Artificial Non-Linearity]:::subNode

    D1 --> D1a[Orifice Flow: F = C sqrt h]
    D1 --> D1b[Arrhenius: k = A exp -E over RT]
    D1 --> D1c[Radiation: q proportional T4]
    D1 --> D1d[Chemical Kinetics]

    D2 --> D2a[Relay On-Off Control]
    D2 --> D2b[Dead Zone]
    D2 --> D2c[Saturation]
    D2 --> D2d[Backlash Hysteresis]

    C3 --> E[Linearization Method]:::linNode
    E --> E1[Taylor Series First Order]
    E --> E2[Deviation Variable Transformation]
    E --> E3[Graphical Tangent Line Method]

    classDef startNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef linearNode fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef nonLinearNode fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef subNode fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef linNode fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0891b2,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Linearization Workflow

This diagram illustrates the **engineering workflow** for linearizing a non-linear process model around its steady state.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NS[Non-Linear Process Model]:::inputNode
    SS[Identify Steady State x-bar, y-bar]:::stepNode
    PD[Compute Partial Derivatives at Steady State]:::stepNode
    TS[Taylor Series Expansion First Order]:::stepNode
    DV[Convert to Deviation Variables X, Y]:::stepNode
    LE[Linearized Model: Y = K times X]:::outputNode
    VAL[Validity: Small Deviations Only]:::checkNode

    NS --> SS
    SS --> PD
    PD --> TS
    TS --> DV
    DV --> LE
    LE --> VAL

    VAL -.->|Valid| AP[Apply Laplace Transform / Transfer Function]:::outputNode
    VAL -.->|Invalid| NS

    classDef inputNode fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef stepNode fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0ea5e9,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputNode fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef checkNode fill:#ea580c,stroke:#ea580c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Linear vs Non-Linear Response

This diagram contrasts the input-output behavior of linear and non-linear systems to a step change input.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LinearResponse[Linear System Response]
        L1[Step Input Magnitude X] --> L2[Constant Gain K]:::linearStep
        L2 --> L3[Output Y = K times X]:::linearStep
        L3 --> L4[Proportional Direct Response]:::linearStep
    end

    subgraph NonLinearResponse[Non-Linear System Response]
        NL1[Step Input Magnitude X] --> NL2[Variable Gain f prime of x]:::nonLinearStep
        NL2 --> NL3[Output Y depends on Operating Point]:::nonLinearStep
        NL3 --> NL4[Disproportionate Curved Response]:::nonLinearStep
    end

    classDef linearStep fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef nonLinearStep fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a linear system. State the two conditions that must be satisfied for a system to be linear.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
A system is **linear** if it obeys the **principle of superposition**. The two conditions are:

**(1) Additivity (Superposition):**
$$f(x_1 + x_2) = f(x_1) + f(x_2)$$

**(2) Homogeneity (Scaling):**
$$f(\alpha x) = \alpha f(x)$$

where $\alpha$ is a scalar constant. Both conditions together imply $f(\alpha x_1 + \beta x_2) = \alpha f(x_1) + \beta f(x_2)$.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Distinguish between inherent and artificial non-linearities. Give one example of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Type | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Inherent** | Arises from fundamental physical/chemical laws of the process. | $F = C\sqrt{\Delta P}$ for valve flow |
| **Artificial** | Introduced intentionally by control elements or final control elements. | Saturation of a control valve (0–100%) |

Inherent non-linearities **cannot be removed** without changing the process. Artificial non-linearities are **design choices** in the control hardware.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** A non-linear process is described by the equation:
$$y = 2 x^2 + 3 x$$
**(i)** Check whether the system is linear or non-linear. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Linearize the equation about the operating point $\bar{x} = 2$. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** The flow through a control valve is given by $F = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P}$, where $C_v = 2.5$ and the steady-state pressure drop is $\bar{P} = 4$ bar.
**(i)** Find the linearized relationship between $F'$ and $P'$. **(4 marks)**
**(ii)** Calculate the steady-state flow $\bar{F}$ and the linearized gain $K$. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a)(i): Checking Linearity — 3 Marks**

Test the function $y = 2x^2 + 3x$ for superposition.

Compute $f(x_1 + x_2)$:
$$f(x_1 + x_2) = 2(x_1 + x_2)^2 + 3(x_1 + x_2) = 2x_1^2 + 4x_1 x_2 + 2x_2^2 + 3x_1 + 3x_2$$

Compute $f(x_1) + f(x_2)$:
$$f(x_1) + f(x_2) = (2x_1^2 + 3x_1) + (2x_2^2 + 3x_2) = 2x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 + 3x_1 + 3x_2$$

Comparing, $f(x_1 + x_2) \neq f(x_1) + f(x_2)$ because of the **cross-term** $4 x_1 x_2$. Hence, the system is **non-linear**. **(3 marks)**

---

**Part (a)(ii): Linearization about $\bar{x} = 2$ — 4 Marks**

**Step 1:** Steady-state value of $y$:
$$\bar{y} = 2(2)^2 + 3(2) = 8 + 6 = 14$$

**Step 2:** Compute derivative:
$$\frac{dy}{dx} = 4x + 3$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate at $\bar{x} = 2$:
$$\left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right\vert_{\bar{x}=2} = 4(2) + 3 = 11$$

**Step 4:** Apply linearization formula:
$$y \approx \bar{y} + \left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right\vert_{\bar{x}} (x - \bar{x})$$
$$y \approx 14 + 11(x - 2)$$
$$y \approx 11x - 8$$

**Step 5:** In deviation form $Y = y - 14$ and $X = x - 2$:
$$Y = 11 X$$ **(4 marks)**

---

**Part (b)(i): Linearized Relationship — 4 Marks**

**Step 1:** Function: $F = C_v \sqrt{P} = C_v P^{1/2}$

**Step 2:** Compute derivative:
$$\frac{dF}{dP} = \frac{C_v}{2\sqrt{P}}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate at $\bar{P} = 4$:
$$\left.\frac{dF}{dP}\right\vert_{\bar{P}=4} = \frac{2.5}{2\sqrt{4}} = \frac{2.5}{4} = 0.625$$

**Step 4:** Apply linearization:
$$F \approx \bar{F} + 0.625 (P - 4)$$
$$F' = 0.625 \, P'$$ **(4 marks)**

---

**Part (b)(ii): Steady-State Flow and Gain — 3 Marks**

**Step 1:** Compute $\bar{F}$:
$$\bar{F} = 2.5 \times \sqrt{4} = 2.5 \times 2 = 5 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the linearized gain:
$$K = \frac{\bar{F}}{2\bar{P}} = \frac{5}{2 \times 4} = \frac{5}{8} = 0.625 \text{ units/bar}$$ **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a)** A chemical reactor exhibits a non-linear temperature dependence given by the Arrhenius equation:
$$k = k_0 \, e^{-E/RT}$$
**(i)** Linearize this expression about a steady-state temperature $\bar{T}$ and express the result in deviation variable form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mixing tank has two input streams. The exit concentration $C$ is governed by:
$$V \frac{dC}{dt} = F_1 C_1 + F_2 C_2 - (F_1 + F_2) C$$
**(i)** Linearize the equation about the steady state. **(4 marks)**
**(ii)** Show that the linearized equation can be written in the standard form $\tau \frac{dC'}{dt} + C' = K_1 C_1' + K_2 C_2' + K_3 F_1' + K_4 F_2'$ and identify the time constant and gains. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a)(i): Linearization of Arrhenius Equation — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** The function to linearize is $k = k_0 e^{-E/RT}$. Here, $T$ is the independent variable.

**Step 2:** Compute the steady-state value:
$$\bar{k} = k_0 e^{-E/R\bar{T}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the partial derivative:
$$\frac{dk}{dT} = k_0 \cdot e^{-E/RT} \cdot \left(-\frac{-E}{RT^2}\right) = k_0 e^{-E/RT} \cdot \frac{E}{RT^2}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate at $\bar{T}$:
$$\left.\frac{dk}{dT}\right\vert_{\bar{T}} = k_0 e^{-E/R\bar{T}} \cdot \frac{E}{R\bar{T}^2} = \bar{k} \cdot \frac{E}{R\bar{T}^2}$$

**Step 5:** Apply Taylor series linearization:
$$k \approx \bar{k} + \bar{k} \cdot \frac{E}{R\bar{T}^2} \cdot (T - \bar{T})$$

**Step 6:** Convert to deviation form $K' = k - \bar{k}$ and $T' = T - \bar{T}$:
$$K' = \bar{k} \cdot \frac{E}{R\bar{T}^2} \cdot T'$$

This shows that a small temperature change produces a linear change in rate constant, with a gain proportional to $\bar{k} E / (R \bar{T}^2)$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Part (b)(i): Linearization of Mixing Tank — 4 Marks**

**Step 1:** Write the non-linear product terms explicitly. The terms $F_1 C$, $F_2 C$, and $F_1 C_1$ contain products of variables.

**Step 2:** Apply deviation variables: $C' = C - \bar{C}$, $F_1' = F_1 - \bar{F}_1$, etc.

**Step 3:** Linearize the product $F_1 C$:
$$F_1 C \approx \bar{F}_1 \bar{C} + \bar{F}_1 C' + \bar{C} F_1'$$
(dropping the second-order term $F_1' C'$).

**Step 4:** Similarly:
$$F_2 C \approx \bar{F}_2 \bar{C} + \bar{F}_2 C' + \bar{C} F_2'$$
$$F_1 C_1 \approx \bar{F}_1 \bar{C}_1 + \bar{F}_1 C_1' + \bar{C}_1 F_1'$$
$$F_2 C_2 \approx \bar{F}_2 \bar{C}_2 + \bar{F}_2 C_2' + \bar{C}_2 F_2'$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the original equation. The constant steady-state terms cancel, and the linearized ODE becomes:

$$V \frac{dC'}{dt} = \bar{F}_1 C_1' + \bar{C}_1 F_1' + \bar{F}_2 C_2' + \bar{C}_2 F_2' - (\bar{F}_1 + \bar{F}_2) C' - \bar{C}(F_1' + F_2')$$

**(4 marks)**

---

**Part (b)(ii): Standard Form — 3 Marks**

**Step 1:** Let $\bar{F} = \bar{F}_1 + \bar{F}_2$. Rearrange the linearized equation:

$$V \frac{dC'}{dt} + \bar{F} C' = \bar{F}_1 C_1' + \bar{F}_2 C_2' + (\bar{C}_1 - \bar{C}) F_1' + (\bar{C}_2 - \bar{C}) F_2'$$

**Step 2:** Divide through by $\bar{F}$:

$$\frac{V}{\bar{F}} \frac{dC'}{dt} + C' = \frac{\bar{F}_1}{\bar{F}} C_1' + \frac{\bar{F}_2}{\bar{F}} C_2' + \frac{\bar{C}_1 - \bar{C}}{\bar{F}} F_1' + \frac{\bar{C}_2 - \bar{C}}{\bar{F}} F_2'$$

**Step 3:** Identify parameters:
$$\tau = \frac{V}{\bar{F}}, \quad K_1 = \frac{\bar{F}_1}{\bar{F}}, \quad K_2 = \frac{\bar{F}_2}{\bar{F}}, \quad K_3 = \frac{\bar{C}_1 - \bar{C}}{\bar{F}}, \quad K_4 = \frac{\bar{C}_2 - \bar{C}}{\bar{F}}$$

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to drop second-order terms** like $F_1' C'$ — this is the **most common error** in linearization problems. Always drop the product of two deviation variables.
> 2. **Not defining deviation variables** explicitly before starting the derivation. Examiners expect you to write $X = x - \bar{x}$ clearly.
> 3. **Mixing up the steady-state value** — students often write $\bar{y} = f(\bar{x})$ but forget to **subtract it** to get the deviation form.
> 4. **Sign errors in derivatives** — especially with the Arrhenius equation and orifice flow equation. Double-check your differentiation.
> 5. **Confusing "inherent" with "artificial" non-linearity** — a question worth 3 marks can be lost if definitions are swapped.
> 6. **Skipping the Taylor series step** — even if you know the final answer, you must show the **Taylor series expansion** explicitly. The partial derivative step is **not enough** without showing the formula $y \approx \bar{y} + f'(\bar{x})(x - \bar{x})$.
> 7. **Not specifying units** of the linearized gain $K$ — the unit is always the ratio of output unit to input unit (e.g., m³/h per bar).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Linear systems** satisfy **superposition** ($f(x_1+x_2) = f(x_1)+f(x_2)$) and **homogeneity** ($f(\alpha x) = \alpha f(x)$).
- **Non-linear systems** violate superposition; the output is **not proportional** to the input.
- Most real chemical processes are **inherently non-linear**: orifice flow ($\sqrt{h}$), Arrhenius kinetics ($e^{-E/RT}$), radiation ($T^4$).
- **Artificial non-linearities** come from control hardware: relays, dead zones, saturation.
- **Linearization** is done using the **first-order Taylor series expansion** about a steady-state operating point.
- The **linearization formula** (single variable):
$$Y \approx f'(\bar{x}) \cdot X = K \cdot X$$
- The **multi-variable linearization formula**:
$$Y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\right\vert_{\bar{x}} X_i$$
- **Deviation variables** ($X = x - \bar{x}$, $Y = y - \bar{y}$) are used so that the **steady state is the new origin**.
- The **linearization error** is of order $(x - \bar{x})^2$ and is **neglected**.
- The **linearized gain $K$** depends on the **operating point** $\bar{x}$ — different steady states give different gains.
- For a non-linear function $y = f(x)$, the linearized form near $\bar{x}$ is geometrically a **tangent line** to the curve at the point $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$.
- **Validity:** Linearization is valid only for **small deviations** from the steady state (typically 5–10%).
- All higher-order terms in the Taylor series (like $(x-\bar{x})^2$, $(x-\bar{x})^3$) are **dropped** in linearization.
- The classical control techniques (Laplace Transform, Transfer Functions, Bode, Nyquist, Root Locus) are applicable **only to linear systems** — this is the entire reason we linearize.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selection of Control Action for Them
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET502 - PROCESS DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Selection of Control Action for Thermal Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 1 — Selection of Control Action for Thermal Processes

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> **Control Action** is the predetermined mathematical relationship between the *controller output* (manipulated variable, $m(t)$) and the *actuating error signal* ($e(t) = \text{Set Point} - \text{Process Variable}$). The **selection of a control action** is the engineering decision-making process by which a designer chooses the most appropriate controller mode (On–Off, Proportional, Integral, Derivative, or a combination) based on the **dynamic characteristics of the process** (time constant $\tau$, dead time $t_d$, process gain $K_p$, degree of self-regulation) and the **desired closed-loop performance** (offset, stability, speed of response, and overshoot).

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this topic is part of **CO1**: *"Apply the principles of process dynamics and develop models for first-order and higher-order systems."* The selection logic is a direct extension of the process model obtained in the prior topics of Module 1.

The five canonical control actions recognised by KTU are:

1. **On–Off (Two-Position) Control**
2. **Proportional (P) Control**
3. **Integral (I) Control**
4. **Proportional–Integral (PI) Control**
5. **Proportional–Derivative (PD) Control**
6. **Proportional–Integral–Derivative (PID) Control**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — "Why Thermal Processes?"**
> Thermal processes (heat exchangers, CSTR jacket temperature, reboilers, furnace outlet, distillation tray temperature) almost universally exhibit **large time constants** (seconds to hours), **appreciable dead time** (transport delay through piping), and **significant inverse-response or non-linearity** with load changes. Hence the *selection* of the right control action — not merely the tuning of it — is the most frequent 14-mark exam question in this module.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Cruise Control vs. The Home Geyser"**

Imagine you are driving a car. You want to maintain a speed of **60 km/h**.

- If a hill comes, you press the accelerator *a little* → **Proportional (P)**.
- If the car still drifts 2 km/h below, you keep adding a small fixed extra pedal *over time* until error vanishes → **Integral (I)**.
- If you *see a hill coming*, you preemptively push the accelerator *before* the car actually slows down → **Derivative (D)**.

Now contrast this with a **home geyser** controlling water at 60 °C. The element is either **ON** or **OFF** — there is no proportional pushing. A simple **thermostat (On–Off)** works perfectly.

- **Cars (fast, precise)** → **PID** (continuous, anticipating).
- **Geyser (slow, sluggish, large lag, tolerance for small oscillation)** → **On–Off or P-only** is sufficient.

This is precisely *selection of control action* — **matching the controller's "personality" to the process's "temperament."**

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics Used in Selection (KTU Tag)

These **bold** metrics appear verbatim in KTU 2024 marking schemes:

- **Offset (Steady-State Error, $e_{ss}$):** Persistent error remaining after transients. *P-only* suffers from this.
- **Decay Ratio (DR):** Ratio of successive peaks of a damped oscillation; target **0.25** (quarter-amplitude decay).
- **Overshoot (Mp):** Peak excursion beyond set point; thermal loops usually allow **5–15 %**.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time to enter and stay within $\pm 5\%$ of set point.
- **Rise Time ($t_r$):** Time to first reach the set point.
- **Integral of Absolute Error (IAE) = $\int_0^{\infty} \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$** — used as a selection benchmark.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔍 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Control Actions — Mathematical Models

The general parallel-form controller output is given by:

$$m(t) \;=\; \bar{m} \;+\; K_c\!\left[\, e(t) \;+\; \frac{1}{\tau_I}\!\int_0^{t} e(t')\,dt' \;+\; \tau_D \,\frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

where:
- $m(t)$ → controller output (sent to final control element).
- $\bar{m}$ → bias value (steady-state output when $e = 0$).
- $K_c$ → **proportional gain** (dimensionless for most thermal loops, can have engineering units).
- $\tau_I$ → **integral (reset) time**, in *minutes*.
- $\tau_D$ → **derivative (rate) time**, in *minutes*.
- $e(t)$ → error = $SP - PV$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Setting any of the three tuning knobs to extreme values reduces the equation to a simpler mode:
> - $K_c \to 0$, others zero → **On–Off** (with relay)
> - $\tau_I = \infty$, $\tau_D = 0$ → **P-only**
> - $K_c$ alone with $\tau_I$ finite → **PI**
> - $K_c$ alone with $\tau_D$ finite → **PD**
> - All three finite → **PID**

### 2.1.1 On–Off Control Action

$$m(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} m_{\max} & \text{if } e(t) \geq 0 \\ m_{\min} & \text{if } e(t) < 0 \end{cases}$$

- **Transfer function is non-linear** (relay with hysteresis $\delta$).
- Causes **limit cycling** — the amplitude of cycling is roughly equal to the hysteresis band.

### 2.1.2 Proportional (P) Action

$$m(t) \;=\; \bar{m} \;+\; K_c\, e(t)$$

- **Proportional Band (PB):** $PB = \dfrac{100}{K_c} \;\; [\%]$.
- Range of PB: **5 % to 500 %** in industrial controllers.
- **Drawback:** Produces a steady-state **offset** $e_{ss} = \dfrac{-L}{K_c\,K_p + 1}$ for a step load change $L$.

### 2.1.3 Integral (I) Action

$$\frac{dm(t)}{dt} \;=\; \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\, e(t) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad m(t) \;=\; \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\!\int_0^t e(t')\,dt'$$

- **Reset rate** = $\dfrac{1}{\tau_I}$ (repeats per minute).
- **Removes offset completely** (with first-order or higher plant).
- **Drawback:** Adds **phase lag of 90°** at all frequencies → reduces stability.

### 2.1.4 Derivative (D) Action

$$m(t) \;=\; K_c\,\tau_D\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

- Acts on the **rate of change** of error, never on constant error.
- **Anticipatory** — provides phase lead, useful in **slow thermal loops** to recover stability margin.
- Never used alone — amplifies measurement noise.
- Typical range: $\tau_D = 0.1\,(\text{min})$ to $\mathbf{20\,min}$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **only cheat sheet you need** for this topic in the KTU 2024 ESE. Every numerical in the question bank below will use one of these expressions.

| # | Control Mode | Output Equation $m(t)$ | Laplace / Transfer Function $G_c(s)$ | Offset? | Stability Effect | Typical Use |
|---|--------------|------------------------|---------------------------------------|---------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **On–Off** | $m = m_{\max}$ if $e \ge 0$ else $m_{\min}$ | Non-linear (relay + hysteresis $\delta$) | Yes, but small | Limit cycle, no decay | Domestic thermostats, geysers, alarms |
| 2 | **P** | $\bar{m} + K_c e(t)$ | $K_c$ | **Yes** ($e_{ss} = -L/(1+K_c K_p)$) | Reduces gain, increases damping | Level control, slow pressure |
| 3 | **I** | $K_c\!\int e\,dt/\tau_I$ | $\dfrac{K_c}{\tau_I s}$ | **No** | Destabilises (adds 90° lag) | Rare alone; usually in PI/PID |
| 4 | **PI** | $K_c(e + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\!\int e\,dt)$ | $K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right) = K_c\dfrac{\tau_I s + 1}{\tau_I s}$ | **No** | Slight destabilisation; removes offset | **Most thermal loops** |
| 5 | **PD** | $K_c(e + \tau_D \dfrac{de}{dt})$ | $K_c(1 + \tau_D s)$ | **Yes** (still P-like) | **Stabilises** (adds phase lead) | Temperature loops with measurement lag |
| 6 | **PID** | $K_c\!\left(e + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\!\int e\,dt + \tau_D \frac{de}{dt}\right)$ | $K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right)$ | **No** | Best compromise | Cascade master, reactor temperature |

### 2.2.1 Key Tuning Relationships (Cohen–Coon / Ziegler–Nichols preview)

For a **first-order plus dead time (FOPDT)** thermal process

$$G_p(s) \;=\; \frac{K_p\, e^{-t_d s}}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

- $K_p$ → process steady-state gain.
- $\tau_p$ → process time constant (minutes, large for thermal).
- $t_d$ → dead time (transport + measurement).

**Ziegler–Nichols (open-loop) tuning rules:**

| Controller | $K_c$ | $\tau_I$ | $\tau_D$ |
|------------|-------|----------|----------|
| **P** | $\dfrac{\tau_p}{K_p\, t_d}$ | — | — |
| **PI** | $\dfrac{0.9\,\tau_p}{K_p\, t_d}$ | $3\,t_d$ | — |
| **PID** | $\dfrac{1.2\,\tau_p}{K_p\, t_d}$ | $2\,t_d$ | $0.5\,t_d$ |

**Selection rule of thumb (KTU-2024 favourite):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $\dfrac{t_d}{\tau_p} \;<\; 0.1$ → **PI or PID** acceptable.
> $0.1 \le \dfrac{t_d}{\tau_p} \le 1.0$ → **PI preferred**; avoid aggressive D.
> $\dfrac{t_d}{\tau_p} \;>\; 1.0$ → **Use cascade or model-predictive**, not pure PID.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CSTR jacket temperature:** mostly **PI** because of large $\tau_p$ and slow drift.
- **Distillation column tray temperature:** **PID** with cascade pressure-compensation (D handles sudden vapour-load changes).
- **Furnace outlet (reformer):** **PI** with high reset time (10–30 min) to avoid tripping on transient puff.
- **Steam-heated heat exchanger:** **PI** is industry default; D causes noisy control due to steam pressure spikes.
- **Cryogenic LNG vaporiser:** **PID** with very small $\tau_D$ (0.05–0.2 min) — fast load dynamics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Closed-Loop Transfer Function for a Thermal Process under PI Control

**Given:**
A first-order thermal process $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$ (e.g., a perfectly mixed tank being heated) under PI control.

**Goal:** Derive the closed-loop transfer function $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}$ and find the steady-state error $e_{ss}$ for a unit step change in load $L(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$.

### Step 1: Write the controller transfer function

$$G_c(s) \;=\; K_c\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s}\right) \;=\; K_c\,\frac{\tau_I s + 1}{\tau_I s}$$

### Step 2: Open-loop transfer function $G_{OL}(s)$

$$G_{OL}(s) \;=\; G_c(s)\,G_p(s) \;=\; \frac{K_c(\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s} \cdot \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

$$G_{OL}(s) \;=\; \frac{K_c K_p\,(\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s\,(\tau_p s + 1)}$$

### Step 3: Closed-loop transfer function (negative unity feedback)

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{G_{OL}(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1)}}{1 + \dfrac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1)}}$$

### Step 4: Rationalise the denominator

Multiply numerator and denominator by $\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1)$:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1) + K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}$$

### Step 5: Expand the denominator to obtain characteristic equation

$$\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1) + K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1) \;=\; \tau_I \tau_p s^2 + \tau_I s + K_c K_p \tau_I s + K_c K_p$$

$$=\; \tau_I \tau_p\, s^2 \;+\; \tau_I (1 + K_c K_p)\, s \;+\; K_c K_p$$

### Step 6: Closed-loop transfer function (final compact form)

$$\boxed{\;\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I \tau_p s^2 + \tau_I(1 + K_c K_p) s + K_c K_p}\;}$$

This is a **second-order system** with:
- Natural frequency $\omega_n^2 = \dfrac{K_c K_p}{\tau_I \tau_p}$.
- Damping ratio $\zeta = \dfrac{1 + K_c K_p}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\tau_I}{K_c K_p \tau_p}}$.

### Step 7: Compute steady-state error for load change

For a unit-step load disturbance $L(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$, the error transfer function is

$$E(s) \;=\; \frac{-L(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} \;\cdot\; G_p(s)$$

By **Final Value Theorem**:

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \lim_{s\to 0}\, s\,E(s) \;=\; \lim_{s\to 0}\, \frac{-s \cdot \frac{1}{s} \cdot G_p(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} \;=\; \frac{-K_p}{1 + G_{OL}(0)}$$

Now $G_{OL}(0) = \lim_{s\to 0} \dfrac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1)} \to \infty$ (because of $1/s$ pole).

Hence,

$$\boxed{\;e_{ss} \;=\; 0\;}$$

**Conclusion:** PI control **eliminates steady-state offset** for a step load change on a first-order thermal process — the mathematical reason why **PI is the default for almost all temperature loops** in industry.

## 3.2 Derivation — Offset in P-only Control (for contrast)

**Given:** Same plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$, with P-controller $G_c(s) = K_c$.

**Step 1:** Open-loop: $G_{OL}(s) = \dfrac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$.

**Step 2:** Load-to-error transfer function:

$$E(s) \;=\; \frac{-G_p(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Final Value Theorem:

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \lim_{s\to 0}\, s\cdot E(s) \;=\; \frac{-K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

**Step 4:** For a step load $L = 1$:

$$\boxed{\;e_{ss} \;=\; \frac{-K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}\;\neq\; 0\;}$$

**Physical interpretation:** P-only cannot drive the error to zero because it needs a *persistent* error to *sustain* a manipulated-variable change. PI integrates this error over time, slowly pushing $e \to 0$.

## 3.3 Derivation — Selection Criterion "Routh Array for Thermal Loop"

A thermal plant is often modelled as **first-order plus dead time** (FOPDT). For a PI controller, the closed-loop denominator becomes **transcendental** due to $e^{-t_d s}$. For analysis, a **Padé 1/1 approximation** is used:

$$e^{-t_d s} \;\approx\; \frac{1 - 0.5\,t_d s}{1 + 0.5\,t_d s}$$

For a PI controller on such a plant, the **Routh-Hurwitz stability limit** on $K_c$ is:

$$K_{c,\max} \;=\; \frac{\tau_p}{K_p\, t_d}$$

This is precisely the **Ziegler–Nichols "ultimate" gain** for the FOPDT plant, and is the *theoretical* upper limit before sustained oscillation begins. Operating at $K_c = 0.5\,K_{c,\max}$ is the textbook answer KTU expects.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Characteristic vs. Recommended Control Action

> [!NOTE]
> This **matrix is the heart of "Selection of Control Action"** and is the most-tested 7-mark sub-part in KTU 2024 ESE.

| Process Characteristic | Symptom in $G_p(s)$ | Recommended Mode | Justification |
|------------------------|---------------------|------------------|---------------|
| Very large time constant ($\tau_p > 30$ min) | Slow tank, furnace | **PI** | Offset must be removed; no noise problem |
| Pure dead-time dominant ($t_d/\tau_p > 1$) | Long pipe, analyser loop | **PI** + **Smith Predictor** (advanced) | D cannot help; needs compensation |
| Oscillatory load changes (cycling steam pressure) | Periodic disturbance | **PID** with moderate D | D anticipates the swing |
| Noisy measurement (RTD with $0.5$ Hz EMI) | High-frequency noise on $e(t)$ | **PI** (no D) | D amplifies noise |
| First-order, self-regulating, small offset tolerable | Pressure, level | **P-only** | Simpler tuning; offset is a small static deviation |
| Thermal with cascading inner flow loop | Reactor with jacket flow | **PID master, PI slave** | Cascade = best practice |
| Strong non-linearity (gain varies 4:1) | pH, distillation | **PI with gain-scheduling** | Single-mode tuning insufficient |
| Two-position acceptable (hysteresis $\le 1\%$ span) | Domestic, alarm | **On–Off** | Cheaper, simpler |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem [KTU 2023 ESE style]:**
A jacketed CSTR temperature loop has the experimentally identified FOPDT model

$$G_p(s) \;=\; \frac{2.5\, e^{-1.2 s}}{8.0\, s + 1}$$

Determine the **recommended control action and the Ziegler–Nichols PI tuning** for this loop. Justify the choice.

### Step 1: Identify process parameters

$K_p = 2.5$, $\tau_p = 8.0$ min, $t_d = 1.2$ min.

### Step 2: Compute the dead-time-to-time-constant ratio

$$\frac{t_d}{\tau_p} \;=\; \frac{1.2}{8.0} \;=\; 0.15$$

### Step 3: Apply the selection criterion

Because $0.1 < t_d/\tau_p < 1.0$ and the process is **slow and noisy (industrial RTD)**, the recommended action is **PI** (not PID) — derivative action is omitted because the noise on $PV$ would be amplified by $\tau_D$ and destabilise the loop.

### Step 4: Apply Ziegler–Nichols PI rule

$$K_c \;=\; \frac{0.9\,\tau_p}{K_p\, t_d} \;=\; \frac{0.9 \times 8.0}{2.5 \times 1.2} \;=\; \frac{7.2}{3.0} \;=\; 2.4$$

$$\tau_I \;=\; 3\, t_d \;=\; 3 \times 1.2 \;=\; 3.6 \;\text{min}$$

### Step 5: State final answer

> **Recommended control action: PI** with $K_c = 2.4$, $\tau_I = 3.6$ min. A cascade master–slave configuration with a jacket-water flow slave loop would further improve robustness (advanced, beyond syllabus).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗂️ SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Selection Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram captures the **decision logic** for selecting a control action on a thermal process. This is the exact flow examiners expect to see in a 7-mark "justify the choice" sub-question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Thermal Process Identified] --> B[Obtain FOPDT Model Gp of s]
    B --> C{Compute t_d over tau_p ratio}
    C -->|Ratio less than 0.1| D[Fast Self Regulating Process]
    C -->|Ratio 0.1 to 1.0| E[Moderate Dead Time]
    C -->|Ratio greater than 1.0| F{Dominant Dead Time}
    D --> G{Noise Level on PV}
    G -->|Low Noise| H[Recommended PID with moderate D]
    G -->|High Noise| I[Recommended PI only]
    E --> J{Offset Tolerance}
    J -->|Zero Offset Required| K[Recommended PI]
    J -->|Small Offset Acceptable| L[Recommended P only]
    F --> M{Application Criticality}
    M -->|Critical Loop| N[Recommended PI plus Smith Predictor]
    M -->|Non Critical| O[Recommended On Off with Hysteresis]
    H --> P[Document Final Choice and Tuning]
    I --> P
    K --> P
    L --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop Temperature Control

> [!NOTE]
> The figure below maps the **physical & signal flow** of a jacketed CSTR temperature loop, identifying the role of the chosen control action in real time.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Set Point Temperature SP] --> SUM[Comparator Summing Junction]
    PV[Process Variable PV from RTD] --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR[Error Signal e of t]
    ERR --> CTRL[Controller Gc of s = PI or PID]
    CTRL --> OP[Controller Output 4 to 20 mA]
    OP --> FCV[Steam Control Valve Final Control Element]
    FCV --> JACK[Jacket Steam Flow Manipulated Variable]
    JACK --> CSTR[Reactor Vessel Thermal Process Gp of s]
    CSTR --> TEMP[Temperature Rise in Vessel]
    TEMP --> RTD[RTD Sensor Measurement]
    RTD --> TT[Transmitter Linearised]
    TT --> PV
    CTRL -. Feedback Tuning .-> TUNE[Tuning Parameters Kc, tau_I, tau_D]
```

## 4.3 Sequence Flow — How a PI Controller "Removes" Offset Step by Step

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SP as Set Point
    participant e as Error
    participant I as Integrator tau_I
    participant P as Proportional Kc
    participant V as Steam Valve
    participant T as Reactor Temperature
    SP->>e: SP set to 60 deg C
    e->>P: e is 5 deg C initially
    P->>V: immediate proportional push
    V->>T: steam opens, temperature rises
    T-->>e: PV becomes 59 deg C, e is 1 deg C
    e->>I: integrator accumulates 1 deg C for 5 min
    I->>V: small extra steam flow over time
    V->>T: temperature reaches 60 deg C
    T-->>e: PV is 60 deg C, e is 0 deg C
    e->>I: integrator holds its last value
    Note over I,V: Bias value sustained
    Note over e,I: Offset is zero, integral action has done its job
```

## 4.4 Diagram Explanation (Text Fallback)

If a student needs to hand-draw in the answer booklet:

1. Draw a **single-line block diagram** with arrows: $SP \to \bigotimes \to [G_c] \to [G_v] \to [G_p] \to PV$ (returned to $\bigotimes$).
2. Mark $G_c = K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$ for PI, and indicate the **bias $\bar{m}$** as a summing input.
3. Indicate the disturbance $L(s)$ entering at the process input (load change).
4. Use the **Padé approximation** to show where dead time appears.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🎯 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part-A question in KTU 2024 ESE carries 3 marks and typically expects a **two-paragraph answer** with one supporting equation.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, CO1]
**"Define 'Control Action' and list any four types commonly used in process control."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *control action* is the predetermined mathematical law by which a controller generates an output $m(t)$ from the error $e(t)$ measured between the set point and the process variable. The four common control actions used in process industries are:
1. **On–Off (Two-Position) Control** — the controller output has only two discrete states.
2. **Proportional (P) Control** — $m = \bar{m} + K_c e$.
3. **Proportional–Integral (PI) Control** — eliminates steady-state offset.
4. **Proportional–Derivative (PD) Control** — adds phase lead for faster response.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing with one-line role of each.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, CO1]
**"Why is PI control the most commonly used mode for thermal processes?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Thermal processes typically have **large time constants** (slow dynamics), **low-frequency disturbances** (load changes from upstream equipment), and a strong requirement for **zero steady-state error** to maintain product quality. The proportional action provides the immediate correction while the integral action **eliminates offset** by continuously integrating any residual error. Derivative action is generally avoided in thermal loops because:
- Measurement noise (RTD / thermocouple) is amplified by $\tau_D$.
- The process itself is naturally slow, so D-action offers limited speed-up.
Hence, **PI is the workhorse controller for temperature loops.**

*(1 mark each: why P, why I, why not D.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme, every 14-mark question has an **internal choice** (a student answers **either** Option A **or** Option B). Each option typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The full model solution is provided below.

---

### 📝 Question Paper Format

> **Q11. a)** Explain the various modes of control action used in process industries. Discuss the advantages and limitations of each mode in the context of thermal process control. **(7 marks)**
>    **b)** A jacketed reactor has the FOPDT model $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1.6\, e^{-0.8 s}}{6.0\, s + 1}$. Using Ziegler–Nichols tuning, recommend and compute the parameters of the most suitable controller. Justify your choice. **(7 marks)**

**[OR]**

> **Q12. a)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function of a first-order thermal process under PI control. Show that the steady-state error due to a unit step load change is zero. **(7 marks)**
>    **b)** A furnace outlet temperature is to be controlled. The process has $K_p = 3.0$, $\tau_p = 12$ min, $t_d = 1.5$ min, and the PV measurement is noisy. Recommend a control action and compute its tuning. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Q11(a) (7 marks)

**Modes of control action — advantages and limitations in thermal loops:**

1. **On–Off (2 marks)** — Simplest, cheapest, two-state output. *Advantage:* no tuning. *Limitation:* causes limit cycle, mechanical wear on valves, unsuitable when tight temperature tolerance is needed (e.g., polymerisation $\pm 0.5$ °C).

2. **Proportional (1.5 marks)** — Output is linearly proportional to error. *Advantage:* immediate corrective action, simple tuning via $K_c$. *Limitation:* produces **steady-state offset** — unacceptable in most temperature loops where set-point accuracy matters.

3. **Integral (1 mark)** — Output is the time-integral of error. *Advantage:* **removes offset completely**. *Limitation:* introduces 90° phase lag → destabilises; rarely used alone.

4. **Proportional–Integral (1 mark)** — Combines P and I. *Advantage:* removes offset, gives acceptable dynamic response. *Limitation:* tuning more complex; sluggish for fast load changes.

5. **Proportional–Derivative (0.5 marks)** — Adds rate action. *Advantage:* improves stability, useful in temperature loops with measurement lag. *Limitation:* amplifies measurement noise.

6. **PID (0.5 marks)** — Universal, gives best of all three. *Limitation:* complex tuning, noise sensitivity from D.

7. **Synthesis (0.5 marks):** For most **thermal** processes, the **PI controller** is the industry default because it provides offset-free control without the noise issues introduced by derivative action.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Q11(b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify FOPDT parameters (1 mark):**
$K_p = 1.6$, $\tau_p = 6.0$ min, $t_d = 0.8$ min.

**Step 2 — Compute dead-time ratio (1 mark):**
$\dfrac{t_d}{\tau_p} = \dfrac{0.8}{6.0} \approx 0.133$ → lies in the **moderate dead-time regime**.

**Step 3 — Select control action with justification (2 marks):**
Because $t_d/\tau_p = 0.133$ is in the range $0.1$–$1.0$, the loop is *moderately dead-time dominated*. With a typical RTD (low noise) and a reactor needing tight temperature control (no offset), the recommended mode is **PI** (or PID if measurement noise is filtered). We will use **PI** as a safer choice.

**Step 4 — Ziegler–Nichols PI tuning formulas (1 mark):**
$$K_c \;=\; \frac{0.9\,\tau_p}{K_p\, t_d}, \qquad \tau_I \;=\; 3\, t_d.$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation (2 marks):**

$$K_c \;=\; \frac{0.9 \times 6.0}{1.6 \times 0.8} \;=\; \frac{5.4}{1.28} \;=\; 4.22$$

$$\tau_I \;=\; 3 \times 0.8 \;=\; 2.4 \;\text{min}$$

**Conclusion:** Use a **PI controller** with $K_c \approx 4.22$ and $\tau_I = 2.4$ min. Proportional Band $PB = 100/K_c \approx 23.7\%$.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Q12(a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State the controller and plant (1 mark):**
$G_c(s) = K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right) = K_c\,\dfrac{\tau_I s + 1}{\tau_I s}$, and $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$.

**Step 2 — Form the open-loop transfer function (1 mark):**
$$G_{OL}(s) \;=\; G_c G_p \;=\; \frac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1)}$$

**Step 3 — Closed-loop transfer function (1.5 marks):**
$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{G_{OL}(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} \;=\; \frac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I \tau_p s^2 + \tau_I(1 + K_c K_p) s + K_c K_p}$$

**Step 4 — Derive the load-to-error transfer function (1 mark):**
$$E(s) \;=\; \frac{-G_p(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)} \cdot L(s) \;=\; \frac{-K_p}{\tau_p s + 1 + G_{OL}(s)(\tau_p s + 1)} \cdot L(s)$$

A simpler form is obtained from the standard block-diagram reduction:

$$E(s) \;=\; \frac{-G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)} \cdot L(s)$$

**Step 5 — Apply Final Value Theorem with $L(s) = 1/s$ (1.5 marks):**

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \lim_{s \to 0}\, s\cdot E(s) \;=\; \frac{-G_p(0)}{1 + \lim_{s\to 0} G_c(s) G_p(s)} \;\cdot\; 1$$

Now $G_p(0) = K_p$ and $\lim_{s\to 0} G_c(s) G_p(s) = \lim_{s\to 0} \dfrac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1)} = \infty$ because of the $1/s$ pole in $G_c(s)$.

Hence:

$$e_{ss} \;=\; \frac{-K_p}{1 + \infty} \;=\; 0 \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 6 — State the interpretation (1 mark):**
The integral action of the PI controller introduces a pole at the origin, which makes the loop Type-1. A Type-1 system tracks a step set-point with zero error and rejects a step load disturbance with zero steady-state error. This is the mathematical proof that **PI control eliminates offset**, which is why PI is the default for thermal loops.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Q12(b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify parameters (1 mark):**
$K_p = 3.0$, $\tau_p = 12$ min, $t_d = 1.5$ min, measurement **noisy**.

**Step 2 — Dead-time ratio (1 mark):**
$\dfrac{t_d}{\tau_p} = \dfrac{1.5}{12} = 0.125$ → moderate.

**Step 3 — Selection rationale (2 marks):**
- The loop requires **zero offset** (furnace outlet temperature is a quality variable) → integral action needed.
- The measurement is **noisy** → derivative action must be **avoided** (D amplifies noise; would cause valve chattering).
- Time constant is large (12 min) → D-action offers little speed-up.
- **Conclusion: PI control** is the recommended action.

**Step 4 — Tuning formulas (1 mark):**
$K_c = \dfrac{0.9 \tau_p}{K_p t_d}$, $\tau_I = 3 t_d$.

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation (2 marks):**

$$K_c \;=\; \frac{0.9 \times 12}{3.0 \times 1.5} \;=\; \frac{10.8}{4.5} \;=\; 2.4$$

$$\tau_I \;=\; 3 \times 1.5 \;=\; 4.5 \;\text{min}$$

**Step 6 — Proportional Band and final summary (extra credit):**
$PB = \dfrac{100}{K_c} = \dfrac{100}{2.4} \approx 41.7\%$.

**Final answer:** Use a **PI controller** with $K_c = 2.4$, $\tau_I = 4.5$ min. The relatively large proportional band (≈42 %) and large integral time (4.5 min) reflect the slow, noisy nature of the furnace loop.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic**
>
> 1. **Confusing $K_p$ and $K_c$ (2-mark loss).** $K_p$ is the *process* gain (units of °C / % steam); $K_c$ is the *controller* gain (dimensionless or °C / °C). Always label.
> 2. **Forgetting units of $t_d$ and $\tau_p$.** The Ziegler–Nichols formulas require **consistent units** (both in minutes, or both in seconds). Mixing minutes and seconds yields an answer off by 60.
> 3. **Choosing PID when measurement is noisy.** Examiners *deliberately* test this trap. A noisy RTD + PID = unstable valve. Always say **PI** when noise is stated.
> 4. **Not justifying the choice.** A bare numerical answer (e.g., "PI with $K_c = 2.4$") gets only 4 of 7 marks. The other 3 marks are for the **justification paragraph** referring to the $t_d/\tau_p$ ratio and the noise.
> 5. **Using $K_c = \tau_p/(K_p t_d)$ instead of $0.9 \tau_p/(K_p t_d)$ for PI.** That formula is for **P-only**. The 0.9 factor matters — it changes $K_c$ by 10 %.
> 6. **Skipping the steady-state error proof** in Q12(a). The proof that $e_{ss} = 0$ requires explicitly applying the **Final Value Theorem** and using the $1/s$ pole of $G_c$ — most students stop at $G_{OL}(0) = \infty$ without justification.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read Twice Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- ☐ **Six modes:** On–Off, P, I, PI, PD, PID. Master the output equation of each.
- ☐ **General PID equation:** $m(t) = \bar{m} + K_c\!\left[e + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I}\!\int e\,dt + \tau_D \dfrac{de}{dt}\right]$.
- ☐ **PI transfer function:** $G_c(s) = K_c \dfrac{\tau_I s + 1}{\tau_I s}$ — has a pole at the origin (Type-1 system).
- ☐ **P-only offset:** $e_{ss} = \dfrac{-K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$ (for unit load step).
- ☐ **PI/PID offset:** $e_{ss} = 0$ (for first-order plant, step load).
- ☐ **FOPDT model:** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-t_d s}}{\tau_p s + 1}$ — universal thermal-process model.
- ☐ **Selection rule via $t_d/\tau_p$:** < 0.1 (PID ok), 0.1–1.0 (PI), > 1.0 (cascade / Smith).
- ☐ **Noise disqualifies D-action.** If the problem says "noisy PV", answer **PI**.
- ☐ **Ziegler–Nichols PI:** $K_c = 0.9 \tau_p / (K_p t_d)$, $\tau_I = 3 t_d$.
- ☐ **Ziegler–Nichols PID:** $K_c = 1.2 \tau_p / (K_p t_d)$, $\tau_I = 2 t_d$, $\tau_D = 0.5 t_d$.
- ☐ **P-only ZN:** $K_c = \tau_p / (K_p t_d)$.
- ☐ **Proportional Band:** $PB = 100 / K_c$ [%].
- ☐ **Reset rate** = $1/\tau_I$ (repeats/min); **Derivative time** $\tau_D$ in minutes.
- ☐ **Thermal loops default = PI** (industry's workhorse).
- ☐ **Always justify** the choice using the FOPDT parameters and noise condition.
- ☐ **On–Off** → limit cycle, hysteresis band $\delta$; not for tight temperature control.
- ☐ **Phase lead** from D-action → useful in temperature loops with measurement lag, harmful with noise.
- ☐ **Closed-loop order** with PI on FOPDT = **second order** (2nd-order characteristic polynomial).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Liquid, Flow and Thermal Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET502 - PROCESS DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Study of Liquid, Flow and Thermal Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 1 — Study of Liquid, Flow and Thermal Processes

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 🔹 Liquid Level Process
A **liquid level process** is a process in which the manipulated or controlled variable is the height (level) of a liquid inside a tank, governed by the difference between the inflow rate and outflow rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *A liquid level process is a first-order capacity process in which the accumulation of mass in a tank of cross-sectional area $A$ is balanced by the difference between the volumetric inflow rate $F_i$ and the volumetric outflow rate $F_o$ through a valve of resistance $R$.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a water tank in your home with a tap at the bottom. When you open the inlet tap fully, water rises slowly. The rate at which the level rises depends on (i) how big the tank is (cross-sectional area $A$) and (ii) how much water flows out through the bottom tap. The bottom tap acts as the "resistance." A wider tank fills up more slowly — that is, it has a **larger time constant**.

### 🔹 Flow Process
A **flow process** is one in which the variable of interest is the volumetric or mass flow rate of a fluid through a pipe, valve, or orifice. Flow processes are usually **very fast** (almost instantaneous) because they involve no significant accumulation of mass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *A flow process is a near-instantaneous (pneumatic/electrical) transmission process in which the manipulated variable is the flow rate of a fluid. The relationship between flow and the driving pressure (or upstream level) is non-linear (square-root type) for most valves and orifices.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a garden hose. When you turn the tap, water sprays out almost instantly — there is no "delay" like in a tank. That is why flow processes have **near-zero capacity and time constant** (pure "dead time" or "instantaneous gain" processes). However, the relationship between the valve opening and the flow is not linear — open the tap twice as much, and you do **not** necessarily get twice the flow!

### 🔹 Thermal Process
A **thermal process** is one in which the controlled variable is the temperature of a fluid in a vessel, achieved by transferring heat into (heating) or out of (cooling) the process stream.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *A thermal process is a capacity-dominated process in which the dynamic behaviour is governed by an energy balance between the heat brought in by the inlet stream, the heat removed by the outlet stream, and the heat added (or removed) by a utility such as a steam coil or electric heater.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Picture a kettle on a stove. When you switch on the stove, the water temperature does not jump instantly to 100 °C — it rises gradually. Why? Because the water has **thermal capacity** (mass × specific heat). A larger kettle takes longer to heat up, just like a wider tank takes longer to fill. The "time constant" of a thermal process is set by the **thermal capacitance** and the overall heat-transfer coefficient.

---

### 1.2 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical SI Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | $\mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$ |
| $\rho$ | Density of water (reference) | $\mathbf{1000 \; kg/m^3}$ |
| $C_p$ | Specific heat of water | $\mathbf{4186 \; J/(kg \cdot K)}$ |
| $A$ | Tank cross-sectional area | $\mathbf{m^2}$ |
| $R$ | Valve resistance | $\mathbf{s/m^2}$ |
| $K_v$ | Valve coefficient | $\mathbf{m^{2.5}/s}$ |
| $U$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient | $\mathbf{W/(m^2 \cdot K)}$ |

> [!TIP]
> Always carry the **units** through every term in a derivation. KTU examiners award partial marks generously for **dimensional consistency**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Processes Studied in This Module

1. **Liquid Level Processes**
   - Single tank (First Order)
   - Two non-interacting tanks in series (Second Order, no zeros)
   - Two interacting tanks in series (Second Order, with a zero)

2. **Flow Processes**
   - Flow through a linear valve
   - Flow through a non-linear valve / orifice (requires linearization)

3. **Thermal Processes**
   - Stirred-tank heater with electric heater
   - Stirred-tank heater with steam coil (two inputs)
   - Mixing of two streams

Each process will be analyzed using the **fundamental law of conservation** (mass or energy) and then converted to a **transfer function** via the Laplace transform.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Modeling Philosophy

The **KTU 2024 Scheme** emphasizes building process models from **first principles** using the universal template:

$$\text{Rate of Accumulation} = \text{Rate of Inflow} - \text{Rate of Outflow} + \text{Rate of Generation}$$

For each process type, the appropriate "quantity" is selected:

| Process Type | Conserved Quantity | Generic Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Liquid Level | Mass / Volume | $A \frac{dh}{dt} = F_i - F_o$ |
| Flow | Momentum (Bernoulli) | $F = K \sqrt{\Delta P}$ |
| Thermal | Energy | $\rho C_p V \frac{dT}{dt} = F \rho C_p (T_{in} - T) + Q$ |

---

## 2.2 Liquid Level Processes — Operational Logic

### A. Single Tank (First-Order)

**Step 1 — Physical Setup:** A tank of cross-sectional area $A$ receives an inflow $F_i$. The outflow $F_o$ discharges through a valve at the bottom.

**Step 2 — Constitutive Law:** Assume the valve obeys a **linear resistance law** (laminar flow regime):
$$F_o = \frac{h}{R}$$
where $R$ is the **valve resistance** $\left( \text{s/m}^2 \right)$.

**Step 3 — Mass Balance:**
$$A \frac{dh}{dt} = F_i - \frac{h}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Deviation Variables:** Let $H = h - h_{ss}$, $F_i' = F_i - F_{ss}$.

**Step 5 — Laplace Transform (zero initial condition):**
$$AR \, s \, H(s) + H(s) = R \, F_i(s)$$

**Step 6 — Final Transfer Function:**
$$\boxed{ \frac{H(s)}{F_i(s)} = \frac{R}{\tau s + 1}, \qquad \tau = AR }$$

The system is **self-regulating** — a step change in $F_i$ produces a new steady-state level $h_{ss} = R \cdot F_{i,ss}$.

---

### B. Two Non-Interacting Tanks in Series (Second-Order, no zeros)

**Operating Principle:** The outflow of Tank 1 enters Tank 2 *without* depending on the level of Tank 2 (i.e., the connecting line is unrestricted).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Non-Interacting"?** The flow between the two tanks depends **only** on the level of Tank 1, not on Tank 2. The two tanks are dynamically decoupled — hence "non-interacting."

**Equations:**
$$A_1 \frac{dh_1}{dt} = F - \frac{h_1}{R_1}, \qquad A_2 \frac{dh_2}{dt} = \frac{h_1}{R_1} - \frac{h_2}{R_2}$$

**Overall Transfer Function (tank 2 level to inlet flow):**
$$\frac{H_2(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}, \quad \tau_1 = A_1 R_1, \; \tau_2 = A_2 R_2$$

The transfer function is a product of two independent first-order blocks.

---

### C. Two Interacting Tanks in Series (Second-Order, with a zero)

**Operating Principle:** The flow between the tanks depends on the **difference** of the two levels: $f_1 = (h_1 - h_2)/R_1$. The two tanks now dynamically **influence** each other.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Interacting"?** The outflow of Tank 1 decreases if Tank 2 level rises — there is a **dynamic feedback** between the two tanks. This produces a **zero** in the transfer function.

**Final Transfer Function:**
$$\frac{H_2(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{R_2 (A_1 R_1 s + 1)}{A_1 A_2 R_1 R_2 s^2 + (A_1 R_1 + A_2 R_2 + A_2 R_1) s + 1}$$

Compare this with the non-interacting case: the interacting case has an **extra term $A_2 R_1 s$** in the linear coefficient of the denominator (which makes the system **more oscillatory / less stable**) and a **zero in the numerator**.

---

## 2.3 Flow Processes — Operational Logic

### Non-Linear Flow Equation (Orifice / Valve)

For a typical control valve or orifice plate, the flow follows the **square-root law** (derived from Bernoulli's principle):
$$F = K_v \sqrt{h}$$
where $K_v$ is the valve flow coefficient and $h$ is the pressure drop (or upstream level).

### Linearization Using Taylor Series

The square-root function is **non-linear**, but control theory requires **linear** models. We linearize around the steady-state operating point $h_{ss}$:

$$F \approx F_{ss} + \left. \frac{\partial F}{\partial h} \right|_{ss} \cdot (h - h_{ss})$$

The partial derivative is the **linearized gain** $K_L$:
$$K_L = \frac{K_v}{2 \sqrt{h_{ss}}}$$

In deviation form:
$$F'(s) = K_L \cdot H(s)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Linearization is valid **only for small deviations** from the operating point (typically within $\pm 5\%$ to $\pm 10\%$). Outside this range, the linear model fails badly.

---

## 2.4 Thermal Processes — Operational Logic

### Stirred-Tank Heater with Electric Heater (Constant $Q$)

A perfectly mixed tank of volume $V$ is heated by an electric coil delivering **constant heat $Q$**. Inlet stream at $T_{in}$ mixes with the contents at $T$ and leaves at $T$.

**Energy Balance:**
$$\rho C_p V \frac{dT}{dt} = F \rho C_p (T_{in} - T) + Q$$

**Transfer Function (deviation variables, zero IC):**
$$\frac{T(s)}{T_{in}(s)} = \frac{1}{\tau s + 1}, \quad \tau = \frac{V}{F}$$

Note: When $Q$ is constant, only $T_{in}$ is an input; the temperature behaves as a **first-order lag**.

---

### Stirred-Tank Heater with Steam Coil (Two Inputs)

When the heat input is from a steam coil:
$$Q = UA(T_s - T)$$

**Energy Balance:**
$$\rho C_p V \frac{dT}{dt} = F \rho C_p (T_{in} - T) + UA(T_s - T)$$

**Transfer Functions:**
$$\frac{T(s)}{T_{in}(s)} = \frac{K_1}{\tau s + 1}, \qquad \frac{T(s)}{T_s(s)} = \frac{K_2}{\tau s + 1}$$

where
$$\tau = \frac{\rho C_p V}{F \rho C_p + UA}, \quad K_1 = \frac{F \rho C_p}{F \rho C_p + UA}, \quad K_2 = \frac{UA}{F \rho C_p + UA}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Thermal processes model **heat exchangers**, **chemical reactors with exothermic/endothermic reactions**, **distillation column reboilers**, and **furnaces**. The same mathematical structure appears in **CSTR concentration dynamics** (with $T \to C$ and $C_p \to$ reaction rate terms).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this table. It covers ~80% of the numerical questions in the KTU ESE on Module 1.

| # | Process | Transfer Function | Key Parameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single tank level | $\dfrac{H(s)}{F_i(s)} = \dfrac{R}{\tau s + 1}$ | $\tau = AR$ |
| 2 | Two non-interacting tanks | $\dfrac{H_2(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}$ | $\tau_1 = A_1 R_1, \; \tau_2 = A_2 R_2$ |
| 3 | Two interacting tanks | $\dfrac{H_2(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{R_2 (A_1 R_1 s + 1)}{A_1 A_2 R_1 R_2 s^2 + (A_1 R_1 + A_2 R_2 + A_2 R_1) s + 1}$ | $f_1 = (h_1 - h_2)/R_1$ |
| 4 | Linear flow through valve | $\dfrac{F(s)}{H(s)} = K_L$ | $K_L = K_v / (2\sqrt{h_{ss}})$ |
| 5 | Stirred tank heater ($Q$ const) | $\dfrac{T(s)}{T_{in}(s)} = \dfrac{1}{\tau s + 1}$ | $\tau = V/F$ |
| 6 | Stirred tank heater (steam coil) | $\dfrac{T(s)}{T_{in}(s)} = \dfrac{K_1}{\tau s + 1}, \quad \dfrac{T(s)}{T_s(s)} = \dfrac{K_2}{\tau s + 1}$ | $\tau = \dfrac{\rho C_p V}{F \rho C_p + UA}$ |
| 7 | Two non-interacting tanks — Step Response | $h_2(t) = K_1 K_2 F \left[ 1 - \dfrac{\tau_1 e^{-t/\tau_1} - \tau_2 e^{-t/\tau_2}}{\tau_1 - \tau_2} \right]$ | $K_1 K_2 = R_1 R_2$ |

> **NOTATION CONVENTIONS USED**
> $A$ = cross-sectional area, $R$ = linear resistance, $F$ = flow, $h$ = level, $T$ = temperature, $Q$ = heat input, $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient, $A_{coil}$ = heat-transfer area, $\rho$ = density, $C_p$ = specific heat, $V$ = volume, $T_s$ = steam temperature, $T_{in}$ = inlet temperature, $K_v$ = valve coefficient.

---

## 2.6 Stability & Dynamic Behaviour — Why It Matters in Engineering

| Process | Order | Time Constant | Real-World Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single tank | 1st | $AR$ | Slower for larger tanks or tighter valves |
| Two non-interacting tanks | 2nd (overdamped) | $\tau_1 + \tau_2$ (approx) | Smooth, sluggish response — easy to control |
| Two interacting tanks | 2nd (can be oscillatory) | Slightly lower | More difficult to control; can go unstable |
| Flow | 0th (instantaneous) | $\approx 0$ | Must rely on the *loop* for dynamics |
| Thermal | 1st | $V/F$ (or larger) | Slow — limiting factor in many chemical plants |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The Department of Chemical Engineering at KTU has consistently tested students on the **physical interpretation of $\tau$ and $K$** — not just algebraic manipulation. Always state what each parameter means in your answer.
