**Step 1 — Mass balance (steady state):**

$$F_C = F_A + F_B$$

**Step 2 — Component balance on B (steady state):**

$$F_C \cdot x_B = F_B \cdot x_{B,in}$$

Assuming pure $B$ enters: $x_{B,in} = 1$, so:

$$x_B = \frac{F_B}{F_A + F_B}$$

**Step 3 — Linearize around operating point $(F_{A,0}, F_{B,0})$:**

Let $F_A = F_{A,0} + \Delta F_A$ and $F_B = F_{B,0} + \Delta F_B$. Substituting:

$$x_B = \frac{F_{B,0} + \Delta F_B}{F_{A,0} + \Delta F_A + F_{B,0} + \Delta F_B}$$

$$x_B = \frac{F_{B,0} + \Delta F_B}{F_{total,0} + \Delta F_{total}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the small-signal approximation** ($1/(1+\delta) \approx 1 - \delta$ for small $\delta$):

$$\frac{1}{F_{total,0} + \Delta F_{total}} \approx \frac{1}{F_{total,0}} \left( 1 - \frac{\Delta F_{total}}{F_{total,0}} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Neglect second-order terms** ($\Delta F_A \cdot \Delta F_B$ terms are dropped):

$$\Delta x_B \approx \frac{\partial x_B}{\partial F_B}\bigg|_0 \Delta F_B + \frac{\partial x_B}{\partial F_A}\bigg|_0 \Delta F_A$$

Computing the partial derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial x_B}{\partial F_B} = \frac{F_A}{(F_A + F_B)^2} \bigg|_0 = \frac{F_{A,0}}{F_{total,0}^2}$$

$$\frac{\partial x_B}{\partial F_A} = -\frac{F_B}{(F_A + F_B)^2} \bigg|_0 = -\frac{F_{B,0}}{F_{total,0}^2}$$

**Step 6 — Combine the result:**

$$\Delta x_B = \frac{1}{F_{total,0}^2} \left( F_{A,0} \cdot \Delta F_B - F_{B,0} \cdot \Delta F_A \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** A change in $F_A$ (wild flow) affects composition in the *opposite* direction to a change in $F_B$ (controlled flow). The sensitivity of composition to $F_A$ is weighted by $F_{B,0}$, and vice versa.

### 3.4 Derivation: Dynamic Lag of the Controlled Flow

The combined secondary loop (controller + valve + flow process + transmitter) is often approximated as a first-order lag with time constant $\tau_B$ and gain $K_B$:

$$G_B(s) = \frac{K_B}{\tau_B s + 1}$$

When the wild flow $F_A$ undergoes a step change of magnitude $A$ at $t = 0$, the relay output is also a step (no relay dynamics assumed), so:

$$F_{B,SP}(t) = K_R \cdot F_{A,new} \quad \text{for} \quad t \geq 0^+$$

**Step 1 — Laplace transform of the setpoint:**

$$F_{B,SP}(s) = \frac{K_R \cdot F_{A,new}}{s}$$

**Step 2 — Assuming tight flow control, $F_B(s) \approx F_{B,SP}(s) \cdot G_B(s)$:**

$$F_B(s) = \frac{K_R \cdot F_{A,new}}{s} \cdot \frac{K_B}{\tau_B s + 1}$$

**Step 3 — For an ideal controller ($F_B(s) = F_{B,SP}(s)$):**

$$F_B(s) = \frac{K_R \cdot F_{A,new}}{s(\tau_B s + 1)}$$

**Step 4 — Inverse Laplace transform:**

$$F_B(t) = K_R \cdot F_{A,new} \cdot \left[ 1 - e^{-t/\tau_B} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Time to reach 98% of final value (3 time constants):**

$$t_{98\%} = 3\tau_B$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Takeaway:** The ratio control loop inherits its **dynamic speed** from the secondary flow loop. Slow valves, large piping volumes, or sluggish transmitters will *severely* limit ratio control performance — even with perfect ratio computation.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Scheme Comparison Summary

| Property | Scheme I (Relay) | Scheme II (Ratio FB) | Scheme III (Dual-Loop) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wild flow measurement | Required | Required | Required (as $F_{A,SP}$) |
| Wild flow control | No | No | Yes |
| Wild flow error rejection | **None** | **Full (common mode)** | **Full** |
| Cost (components) | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Dynamic speed | Fast (1 DOF) | Slower (2 DOF) | Fastest (independent) |
| Implementation complexity | Simple | Moderate | Complex |
| Susceptibility to wild flow noise | High (passes through) | Low (filtered by ratio loop) | Medium |
| KTU preferred for… | Non-critical blending | Combustion, pharma | High-purity blending |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied
- All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `stepA`, `block1`).
- No reserved keywords used as standalone node names.
- All node labels contain raw uppercase alphanumeric text only — no markdown bold, italics, or HTML tables inside quoted labels.

### 4.2 Schematic 1: Scheme I — Ratio Relay (Open-loop on Wild Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["WILD FLOW SOURCE"] --> stepB["FT-A TRANSMITTER"]
    stepB --> stepC["RATIO RELAY K_R"]
    stepC -->|F_B,SP| stepD["FC-B CONTROLLER"]
    stepD --> stepE["CONTROL VALVE B"]
    stepE --> stepF["F_B TO PROCESS"]
    stepF --> stepG["FT-B TRANSMITTER"]
    stepG -->|F_B,MEAS| stepD
    style stepC fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepD fill:#9FC5E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** The wild flow $F_A$ is measured by FT-A, multiplied by $K_R$ in the ratio relay, and sent as setpoint to FC-B. The secondary loop on $F_B$ closes the control action. Notice there is no direct feedback to correct errors in $F_A$ measurement.

### 4.3 Schematic 2: Scheme II — Ratio Station with Closed-loop on Ratio

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["WILD FLOW FA"] --> stepB["FT-A"]
    stepF["F_B TO PROCESS"] --> stepG["FT-B"]
    stepB --> stepH["DIVIDER"]
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH -->|R_ACTUAL| stepI["RC RATIO CONTROLLER"]
    stepJ["RATIO SETPOINT K_R"] --> stepI
    stepI -->|F_B,SP| stepK["FC-B"]
    stepK --> stepL["VALVE B"]
    stepL --> stepF
    style stepH fill:#F4B084,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepI fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepK fill:#9FC5E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** Both flows are measured, divided to produce the actual ratio, and compared with the ratio setpoint. The ratio controller (RC) computes the required $F_{B,SP}$, which is sent to FC-B. This scheme is robust to common-mode transmitter errors.

### 4.4 Schematic 3: Scheme III — Dual-Loop with Computed Setpoint (Feedforward)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["WILD FLOW FA"] --> stepB["FT-A"]
    stepB --> stepC["FC-A CONTROLLER"]
    stepJ["FA,SP FROM UPSTREAM"] --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD["VALVE A"]
    stepD --> stepE["PROCESS INLET"]
    stepE --> stepF["F_B TO BLEND"]
    stepF --> stepG["FT-B"]
    stepG --> stepH["FC-B CONTROLLER"]
    stepK["RATIO STATION K_R"] -->|F_B,SP| stepH
    stepC -->|FA,MEAS| stepK
    stepH --> stepL["VALVE B"]
    stepL --> stepF
    style stepK fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepC fill:#9FC5E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style stepH fill:#9FC5E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** Both $F_A$ and $F_B$ are independently controlled. The multiplier computes $F_{B,SP} = K_R \cdot F_{A,meas}$ in real time. This is the most accurate scheme but is also the most expensive and complex to implement and tune.

### 4.5 Schematic 4: Functional Architecture of a Combustion Ratio Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    block1["FUEL HEADER PRESSURE"] --> block2["FT-FUEL"]
    block3["AIR BLOWER"] --> block4["FT-AIR"]
    block2 --> block5["RATIO RELAY K_R"]
    block5 -->|AIR,SP| block6["FC-AIR"]
    block6 --> block7["AIR DAMPER VALVE"]
    block7 --> block4
    block4 --> block8["COMBUSTOR"]
    block2 --> block8
    block8 --> block9["FLUE GAS TO STACK"]
    style block5 fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style block8 fill:#F4B084,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style block6 fill:#9FC5E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:** The fuel stream is the wild flow (driven by upstream demand). The ratio relay ensures the air flow always tracks the correct stoichiometric excess (typically 15–25:1 air-to-fuel). The damper is adjusted by FC-AIR to maintain the ratio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Observation:** Scheme III is rarely used in combustion because fuel is the *demand variable* and is naturally not under flow control. Schemes I and II dominate combustion ratio control.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Define ratio control. List any four industrial applications where ratio control is essential.

**Model Answer:**

Ratio control is a control strategy in which two process variables (typically flow rates) are maintained in a fixed proportion to each other, expressed as $R = F_B / F_A$, to ensure product quality, safety, or stoichiometric compliance. The uncontrolled stream is termed the *wild flow*, while the dependent stream is the *controlled flow*.

**Four industrial applications:**
1. **Blending of petroleum streams** in refinery gasoline blending units, where additive concentration determines octane number.
2. **Air-fuel ratio control** in boilers, furnaces, and gas turbines, where excess air determines combustion efficiency and emissions.
3. **pH neutralization** in wastewater treatment, where acid and base streams must be dosed in stoichiometric proportions.
4. **Beverage production** (carbonated soft drinks), where syrup and water/CO₂ must be blended in fixed recipe ratios.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for applications)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is a *ratio relay*? Explain its function in a Scheme I ratio control system.

**Model Answer:**

A **ratio relay** is a computing element (mechanical, pneumatic, or electronic) that multiplies a measured input signal by a preset constant $K_R$. In a Scheme I ratio control system, the ratio relay accepts the measured wild flow $F_{A,meas}$ as input and produces an output proportional to $F_{B,SP} = K_R \cdot F_{A,meas}$, which is then used as the setpoint of the flow controller FC-B on the controlled stream.

The function of the ratio relay is to **enforce the ratio relationship implicitly** at the setpoint level. It does not perform any feedback action; hence, errors in the wild flow measurement (e.g., transmitter drift) propagate directly to the actual ratio. This is the primary limitation of Scheme I.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for function and limitation explanation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Schematic diagrams are mandatory** for any 7+ mark ratio control question. Sketches without labels, signal arrows, or instrument tags *will lose 2–3 marks*.
> - Students often **confuse Scheme I and Scheme II**; clearly label the *location* of the ratio computation (setpoint vs. measurement) and indicate the *feedback paths*.
> - Always state the **limitation** of Scheme I (no correction for $F_A$ measurement error). Examiners specifically look for this critical analysis.
> - Numerical answers must include **units** and **justification** of assumptions (e.g., "assume ideal flow tracking in the secondary loop").
> - Do not write $R = F_B : F_A$ colloquially; use the mathematical form $R = F_B / F_A$ in the answer script.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** With the help of neat block diagrams, explain **any three schemes of implementing ratio control** in a process plant. Compare them in terms of accuracy, cost, and dynamic response. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A *ratio control system* maintains the relationship $R = F_B / F_A$ between two streams. The three standard schemes are:

**Scheme I — Ratio Relay (Open-loop on Wild Flow):**

In this scheme, the wild flow $F_A$ is measured by FT-A and fed to a ratio relay, which multiplies it by $K_R$ to produce the setpoint for FC-B on the controlled flow $F_B$:

$$F_{B,SP} = K_R \cdot F_{A,meas}$$

The secondary loop (FC-B → valve → flow process → FT-B) tracks this setpoint. There is **no feedback** on the wild flow or on the ratio itself. This is the simplest and cheapest scheme. However, it cannot correct for errors in $F_A$ measurement — a fractional error $\epsilon_T$ in $F_A$ produces the same fractional error in the actual ratio.

**Scheme II — Ratio Station with Closed-loop on Ratio:**

Both $F_A$ and $F_B$ are measured. A divider computes the actual ratio $R_{actual} = F_{B,meas} / F_{A,meas}$. A ratio controller RC compares this with $R_{SP}$ and adjusts $F_{B,SP}$ sent to FC-B. The system eliminates *common-mode* measurement errors because $\epsilon_T$ in the numerator and denominator cancels. This scheme is more accurate and is the standard choice in combustion and pharmaceutical applications.

**Scheme III — Dual-Loop with Computed Setpoint:**

Both $F_A$ and $F_B$ have dedicated flow controllers (FC-A and FC-B). A multiplier computes $F_{B,SP} = K_R \cdot F_{A,meas}$ in real time. Since $F_A$ is itself tightly regulated, the ratio is enforced at the *setpoint level* with high accuracy. This is the most expensive scheme, requiring two control valves and two flow controllers, but offers the best dynamic response and disturbance rejection.

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Scheme I | Scheme II | Scheme III |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | Low | High | Highest |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Dynamic Speed | Fast | Moderate (2 DOF) | Fastest |
| Wild Flow Error Rejection | None | Yes | Yes (via $F_A$ control) |

*(7 marks: 2 per scheme + 1 for comparison table)*

---

**(b)** A natural gas burner requires an air-to-fuel ratio of 18:1 by mass to maintain efficient combustion. The fuel flow varies in the range 80–250 kg/h due to upstream demand. Design a ratio control system using Scheme I, and calculate the **range of the air flow setpoint**. If the air flow transmitter has a calibration drift of +3%, determine the **resulting ratio error** at nominal fuel flow. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Desired air-to-fuel ratio: $R = K_R = 18$
- Fuel (wild) flow range: $F_A = 80$ to $250 \text{ kg/h}$
- Transmitter drift: $\epsilon_T = +0.03$ (i.e., $F_{A,meas} = 1.03 F_A^{true}$)

**Step 1 — Range of air flow setpoint:**

At minimum fuel flow:

$$F_{B,SP,min} = 18 \times 80 = 1440 \text{ kg/h}$$

At maximum fuel flow:

$$F_{B,SP,max} = 18 \times 250 = 4500 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Range of $F_{B,SP}$: 1440 to 4500 kg/h.**

**Step 2 — Ratio error at nominal fuel flow (assumed midpoint $F_A^{true} = 165$ kg/h):**

$$F_{B,SP} = 18 \times F_{A,meas} = 18 \times (1.03 \times 165)$$

$$F_{B,SP} = 18 \times 169.95 = 3059.1 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Actual ratio achieved:**

Assuming FC-B tracks the setpoint perfectly:

$$F_{B,meas} = 3059.1 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$R_{actual} = \frac{F_{B,meas}}{F_A^{true}} = \frac{3059.1}{165} = 18.54$$

**Step 4 — Ratio error:**

$$e_R = R_{actual} - R_{SP} = 18.54 - 18 = 0.54$$

**Fractional error:**

$$\frac{e_R}{R_{SP}} = \frac{0.54}{18} = 0.03 = +3\%$$

**Conclusion:** The fractional error in the actual ratio equals the transmitter drift (3%). To eliminate this, **Scheme II (ratio feedback) should be used**, since the same drift affects both measurements and cancels in the ratio computation.

*(7 marks: 2 for setpoint range, 3 for ratio error derivation, 2 for conclusion and recommendation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Distinguish between **ratio control and cascade control** with respect to their purpose, structure, and the type of process variables involved. Show block diagrams for both and identify **two key similarities** in their control philosophies. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Purpose:**
- **Ratio control** maintains a *fixed proportional relationship* between two (or more) process variables, typically flow rates. Its primary purpose is to enforce a recipe, stoichiometry, or blending specification.
- **Cascade control** uses *two feedback loops in series* — a primary (master) loop and a secondary (slave) loop — to reject disturbances entering the secondary loop faster than the primary loop could alone.

**Structure:**
- In **ratio control**, the two streams are *parallel*; both serve the same process (e.g., blending), with one stream being the *wild* variable.
- In **cascade control**, the loops are *nested*; the output of the primary controller becomes the setpoint of the secondary controller.

**Block diagrams:**

**Ratio Control (Scheme I):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1["FA WILD"] --> N2["FT-A"] --> N3["RELAY"] -->|FB,SP| N4["FC-B"] --> N5["VALVE B"] --> N6["PROCESS"] --> N7["FT-B"] --> N4
```

**Cascade Control:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1["PV"] --> N2["TC PRIMARY"] -->|T1,SP| N3["TC SECONDARY"] --> N4["VALVE"] --> N5["JACKET"] --> N1
    N3 --> N6["TT SL"] --> N3
```

**Process Variables:**
- Ratio control: typically two flows of the *same physical type* (both mass or both volumetric).
- Cascade control: typically two *different* variable types (e.g., temperature primary, flow or temperature secondary).

**Two Key Similarities:**
1. **Both employ a secondary (inner) loop** to improve dynamic response — cascade has a flow or temperature slave, ratio control uses the flow loop on $F_B$.
2. **Both are special-purpose control schemes** that go beyond simple single-loop feedback to handle specific process demands (composition blending for ratio, disturbance rejection for cascade).

*(7 marks: 2 for purpose/structure distinction, 2 for block diagrams, 2 for variable types, 1 for similarities)*

---

**(b)** A chemical reactor requires a continuous feed of two reactants A and B in the molar ratio 1:3. Stream A is the wild flow with transmitter range 0–500 L/h, and stream B is the controlled flow with control valve range 0–2000 L/h. Design a **Scheme II ratio control system** and determine the required range of the **ratio station setting** $K_R$. Also explain why Scheme II is preferred over Scheme I for this application. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Desired molar ratio: $F_B : F_A = 3 : 1$, so $K_R = R = 3$.
- Stream A transmitter range: 0–500 L/h.
- Stream B control valve range: 0–2000 L/h.

**Step 1 — Maximum wild flow:**

When $F_A$ reaches 500 L/h:

$$F_{B,required} = 3 \times 500 = 1500 \text{ L/h}$$

This is **within** the B control valve range of 0–2000 L/h. So the ratio station setting $K_R = 3$ is feasible across the entire operating range.

**Step 2 — Range of $K_R$:**

The ratio station is typically an adjustable multiplier with a span of 0–10 (or 0.1–10 depending on the manufacturer). The required value is:

$$K_R = 3 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

**Adjustment range required:** $K_R = 3$ (single-point setting; no need for variable ratio).

**Step 3 — Scheme II architecture:**

Both FT-A and FT-B feed a divider, which computes $R_{actual} = F_{B,meas} / F_{A,meas}$. A ratio controller RC compares this with the setpoint $R_{SP} = 3$ and adjusts the setpoint of FC-B, which in turn drives the control valve on stream B.

**Step 4 — Why Scheme II is preferred:**

A chemical reactor's product selectivity and conversion are **critically sensitive** to the reactant ratio. Even a 2–3% deviation in $F_B/F_A$ can cause:
- Unconverted reactant buildup (yield loss)
- Side reactions (selectivity loss)
- Runaway exotherms (safety hazard)
- Off-spec product (quality violation)

Scheme I would propagate any drift in the wild flow transmitter directly into the ratio, whereas **Scheme II cancels common-mode measurement errors** (both $F_A$ and $F_B$ are measured with the same drift, which cancels in the ratio computation). For a stoichiometrically sensitive reactor, **Scheme II's accuracy advantage is non-negotiable**.

**Additionally:** Scheme II's ratio controller provides **integral action** that drives the *steady-state* ratio error to zero, even in the presence of valve hysteresis or unmeasured disturbances in stream B's supply pressure.

*(7 marks: 2 for ratio range, 2 for Scheme II architecture, 3 for justification of preference)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Ratio Control (Module 3)**

- [ ] **Core Invariant:** $R = F_B / F_A$ must hold at all times during operation.
- [ ] **Wild vs. Controlled:** Wild flow ($F_A$) is the *demand-driven, independent* stream; controlled flow ($F_B$) is *manipulated* to maintain ratio.
- [ ] **Three Schemes — Know All:**
  - **Scheme I:** Ratio relay multiplies measured $F_A$ by $K_R$ → $F_{B,SP}$.
  - **Scheme II:** Measured $F_A$ and $F_B$ are divided → ratio controller adjusts $F_{B,SP}$.
  - **Scheme III:** $F_A$ is itself flow-controlled; multiplier computes $F_{B,SP} = K_R \cdot F_{A,SP}$ (true feedforward).
- [ ] **Critical Limitation of Scheme I:** *Any* error in $F_A$ measurement (transmitter drift, calibration) passes through to the actual ratio — **fractional ratio error = fractional transmitter error**.
- [ ] **Key Advantage of Scheme II:** Common-mode transmitter errors cancel in the ratio computation ($F_B^{true}(1+\epsilon)/F_A^{true}(1+\epsilon) = F_B^{true}/F_A^{true}$).
- [ ] **Dynamic Response:** Dominant time constant is the secondary flow loop time constant $\tau_B$; ratio reaches 98% in $3\tau_B$ seconds.
- [ ] **Material Balance Identity:** $F_{in,total} = F_A + F_B$ (steady-state blend).
- [ ] **Composition Relation:** $x_B = F_B / (F_A + F_B)$.
- [ ] **Linearization Result:** $\Delta x_B = (1/F_{total,0}^2)(F_{A,0}\Delta F_B - F_{B,0}\Delta F_A)$ — wild flow affects composition *opposite* to controlled flow.
- [ ] **Industrial Priorities:** Use Scheme I for non-critical blending, Scheme II for combustion and pharma, Scheme III for high-purity continuous blending.
- [ ] **K-Range Check:** Always verify that the required $F_B$ range is within the control valve capacity; otherwise, the ratio cannot be maintained at extreme $F_A$ values.
- [ ] **Exam Tip:** Always draw the *block diagram* with instrument tags (FT, FC, RC, FV) and signal arrows — a labeled diagram is worth 2–3 marks by itself.
- [ ] **Pitfall to Avoid:** Do not write $F_B : F_A$ in equations; use the rigorous form $R = F_B / F_A$ or $F_B = R F_A$.
- [ ] **Connection to Module 3:** Ratio control is the *simplest form* of feedforward control — anticipate the link to feedforward compensation in the next sub-module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selective Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET502 - PROCESS DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Selective Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Selective Control** (also widely known as **Override Control**) is an advanced regulatory control strategy in which a signal **selector block** (either a *High Selector* or a *Low Selector*) is inserted between multiple controllers and a single final control element. The selector continuously evaluates the outputs of all candidate controllers and forwards only the **most extreme (maximum or minimum) signal** to manipulate the final control element, while the other controller(s) are effectively "de-selected" and operate in an open-loop standby mode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Selective control is the *third* fundamental class of advanced control (after cascade and ratio control) and is *specifically listed* in Module 3 of PCAET502. Expect direct 7–14 mark questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine **two drivers sitting in the same car** — both gripping the steering wheel and accelerator simultaneously. One driver (Controller A) wants to go as fast as possible, while the other driver (Controller B) wants to go as slowly as possible for safety. To prevent disaster, we install a **"smart mediator"** that always picks the **slower** driver's accelerator input (the most conservative/most extreme one for safety) to drive the car. 

This "smart mediator" is exactly the **Low/High Signal Selector** in selective control:
- The driver that **wins control** changes dynamically based on operating conditions.
- The losing controller is *not* shut off — it keeps computing, ready to take over instantly when its output becomes the most extreme.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Cascade Control:** Cascade uses *one* primary controller and *one* secondary controller in series. Selective control uses *two or more independent* controllers competing in **parallel** for a single actuator.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Signal Range:** Standard industrial signals are **4–20 mA** (current) or **3–15 psig** (pneumatic).
- **Selector Logic Time:** Modern electronic/pneumatic selectors respond in **< 1 second**.
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL):** Most override loops in chemical plants are designed to **SIL 1 or SIL 2**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Selector Types

A **Selector Block** is a passive mathematical operator (no tuning, no dynamics) that outputs either the maximum or minimum of its inputs.

**A. High Signal Selector (HS):** Outputs the **largest** input.

$$y_{HS} = \max(y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n)$$

**B. Low Signal Selector (LS):** Outputs the **smallest** input.

$$y_{LS} = \min(y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The selector is a **memoryless, static element** — it introduces **no time lag** and **no dynamics** of its own. Its transfer function is a pure nonlinearity (a multi-input signum/extremum operator).

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **All candidate controllers** receive their respective process measurements continuously and compute their outputs ($y_1, y_2, \ldots$).
2. **The selector compares** all outputs in real time on a scan-by-scan basis.
3. **The controller with the most extreme output** "wins" and is routed to the final control element (valve, VFD, damper motor).
4. **The losing controller(s)** remain in *open-loop standby* — they are not integrated; only their proportional-plus-derivative action continues to update the selector input.
5. **Switching is bumpless** because the selector tracks whichever signal is being implemented; the alternative signal coasts along in parallel.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Variable | Equation / Definition | Purpose / Application |
|---|---|---|
| High Selector Output | $y_{HS} = \max(y_1, y_2)$ | Picks the higher controller output |
| Low Selector Output | $y_{LS} = \min(y_1, y_2)$ | Picks the lower controller output |
| Switch Condition (HS) | $y_1 > y_2$ | Controller 1 takes over |
| Switch Condition (LS) | $y_1 < y_2$ | Controller 1 takes over |
| Compressor Surge Line | $\Delta P_{surge} = a Q^2 + b Q + c$ | Anti-surge override reference |
| Compressor Stone Wall | $Q_{max}$ constant | Maximum safe flow limit |
| Cross-Limit Fuel/Air | $F_{fuel} \le k \cdot F_{air}$ | Fired-heater safety constraint |
| Fired Heater Stoichiometric Ratio | $k = F_{air}/F_{fuel}$ (≈10:1) | Combustion safety margin |
| Drum Level Override | Switch LS→HS on low load | Boiler swell/shrink protection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No tuning parameters for the selector** itself — it is a hard-wired or software-implemented function block. The **controllers feeding the selector** must each be tuned for **stable open-loop performance** because they may be disconnected for long periods.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Selective control is a **safety-critical architecture** used in:

- **Compressor anti-surge protection** (petrochemical, LNG, refrigeration)
- **Fired heater fuel–air cross-limiting** (refineries, ethylene crackers)
- **Boiler drum level override** during start-up (power plants)
- **Reactor temperature–pressure override** (exothermic reactors)
- **Distillation column flooding detection** (reflux ratio override)
- **Vapor recovery unit** pressure/vacuum protection

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: Selector Switching Criterion

Consider two controllers **C1** and **C2** (both PI-type) feeding a **High Selector (HS)**. Let their outputs be:

$$y_1(t) = K_{c1}\left[e_1(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_{I1}}\int e_1(t)\,dt\right]$$

$$y_2(t) = K_{c2}\left[e_2(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_{I2}}\int e_2(t)\,dt\right]$$

The HS output is:

$$y_{out}(t) = \max\{y_1(t),\, y_2(t)\}$$

**Switching event** from Controller 1 to Controller 2 occurs at time $t = t_s$ when:

$$y_1(t_s) = y_2(t_s)$$

and the time-derivative of the difference changes sign:

$$\left.\frac{d}{dt}\bigl[y_1(t) - y_2(t)\bigr]\right|_{t_s} > 0$$

This means $y_2$ is *growing faster* than $y_1$ at the moment of equality, and the selector hands control to Controller 2.

**Step-by-step Derivation of the Switch Condition for a Temperature–Pressure Override (Reactor):**

Let:
- **C1** = Temperature controller output $y_T$
- **C2** = Pressure controller output $y_P$
- **HS** is used (we want whichever controller demands *more* cooling/feed to be the one that wins on safety)

**Step 1:** Define the controller outputs at steady state.

$$y_{T,ss} = \bar{y}_T + K_{cT}\,(T_{sp} - T_{meas})$$

$$y_{P,ss} = \bar{y}_P + K_{cP}\,(P_{sp} - P_{meas})$$

**Step 2:** Set the switch condition $y_T = y_P$.

$$\bar{y}_T + K_{cT}\,(T_{sp} - T_{meas}) = \bar{y}_P + K_{cP}\,(P_{sp} - P_{meas})$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the *boundary* in $(T, P)$ space that defines the switch curve.

$$K_{cT}\,T_{meas} - K_{cP}\,P_{meas} = K_{cT}\,T_{sp} - K_{cP}\,P_{sp} + \bar{y}_P - \bar{y}_T$$

Rearranging gives a **linear switching line** in the $T$–$P$ plane:

$$T_{meas} = \frac{K_{cP}}{K_{cT}}\,P_{meas} + \left[T_{sp} - \frac{K_{cP}}{K_{cT}}\,P_{sp} + \frac{\bar{y}_P - \bar{y}_T}{K_{cT}}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation — Above this line, the temperature controller demands a higher opening of the cooling-water valve (more extreme), so **HS routes C1 to the valve**. Below the line, **C2 wins** and the pressure override kicks in to prevent runaway reaction.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Compressor Anti-Surge Override

A centrifugal compressor has the following surge-line equation (manufacturer data):

$$\Delta P_{surge} = 0.04\,Q^2 - 0.6\,Q + 5.0 \quad \text{(psia)}$$

where $Q$ is volumetric flow in **MMSCFD**. The actual measured differential pressure is:

$$\Delta P_{meas} = 0.035\,Q^2 + 0.3\,Q + 3.0 \quad \text{(psia)}$$

A **flow controller (FC)** and a **surge controller (SC)** both manipulate the anti-surge recycle valve. A **Low Selector (LS)** is used because we want the controller demanding the **least** valve opening to win (fail-safe).

**Step 1:** At design operating point $Q = 80$ MMSCFD, compute surge reference.

$$\Delta P_{surge} = 0.04\,(80)^2 - 0.6\,(80) + 5.0 = 256 - 48 + 5 = 213 \text{ psia}$$

**Step 2:** Compute measured $\Delta P$ at the same flow.

$$\Delta P_{meas} = 0.035\,(80)^2 + 0.3\,(80) + 3.0 = 224 + 24 + 3 = 251 \text{ psia}$$

**Step 3:** The surge controller output is proportional to the *error* between $\Delta P_{meas}$ and $\Delta P_{surge}$:

$$e_{surge} = \Delta P_{meas} - \Delta P_{surge} = 251 - 213 = +38 \text{ psia}$$

The SC output drives the recycle valve **closed** (less recycle) because the operating point is *safely* above the surge line.

**Step 4:** The flow controller output $y_{FC}$ is at 45% (matching the design flow of 80 MMSCFD). The SC output $y_{SC}$ is at 28% (valve mostly closed, no recycle needed). 

**Step 5:** Apply the **Low Selector**:

$$y_{out} = \min(y_{FC},\, y_{SC}) = \min(45\%, 28\%) = 28\%$$

So the surge controller wins because it demands the *smallest* valve opening. ✅

**Step 6:** Now consider a process disturbance: flow suddenly drops to $Q = 50$ MMSCFD. Recompute:

$$\Delta P_{surge}(50) = 0.04\,(2500) - 0.6\,(50) + 5.0 = 100 - 30 + 5 = 75 \text{ psia}$$

$$\Delta P_{meas}(50) = 0.035\,(2500) + 0.3\,(50) + 3.0 = 87.5 + 15 + 3 = 105.5 \text{ psia}$$

**Step 7:** Surge error: $e_{surge} = 105.5 - 75 = +30.5$ psia. Surge controller still says "we're safe." But what if flow drops to $Q = 30$ MMSCFD?

$$\Delta P_{surge}(30) = 0.04\,(900) - 0.6\,(30) + 5.0 = 36 - 18 + 5 = 23 \text{ psia}$$

$$\Delta P_{meas}(30) = 0.035\,(900) + 0.3\,(30) + 3.0 = 31.5 + 9 + 3 = 43.5 \text{ psia}$$

Still safe. At $Q = 20$ MMSCFD:

$$\Delta P_{surge}(20) = 0.04\,(400) - 0.6\,(20) + 5.0 = 16 - 12 + 5 = 9 \text{ psia}$$

$$\Delta P_{meas}(20) = 0.035\,(400) + 0.3\,(20) + 3.0 = 14 + 6 + 3 = 23 \text{ psia}$$

Still $23 > 9$ → safe. The crossover (surge onset) occurs when:

$$0.04\,Q^2 - 0.6\,Q + 5.0 = 0.035\,Q^2 + 0.3\,Q + 3.0$$

$$0.005\,Q^2 - 0.9\,Q + 2.0 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$Q = \frac{0.9 \pm \sqrt{0.81 - 0.04}}{0.01} = \frac{0.9 \pm \sqrt{0.77}}{0.01} = \frac{0.9 \pm 0.8775}{0.01}$$

The smaller root (the actual surge point) is:

$$Q_{surge} = \frac{0.9 - 0.8775}{0.01} = \frac{0.0225}{0.01} = 2.25 \text{ MMSCFD}$$

Below this flow, the surge controller takes over and the LS routes its (much higher) output to fully open the recycle valve — preventing surge damage.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — HS vs LS Applications

| Application | Selector Type | "Winner" in Normal Operation | "Winner" in Abnormal Condition | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Compressor anti-surge | **LS** | Flow controller | Surge controller | Surge controller demands *less* valve opening in safe zone; *more* opening in danger |
| Fired heater fuel limiting | **HS** | Air flow controller | Fuel controller (limiting) | Fuel demand is capped by available air to prevent oxygen-deficient combustion |
| Boiler drum level | **LS** then **HS** | 3-element level | Single-element level | Switch logic protects against swell/shrink on start-up |
| Reactor T/P override | **HS** | Temperature controller | Pressure controller (overrides) | Pressure runaway demands more extreme corrective action |
| Refrigeration condenser | **LS** | Temperature controller | Pressure controller | Prevents compressor overload on high head pressure |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Two-Controller Selective Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1(("SP1")) --> C1["Controller 1"]
    PV1(("PV1")) --> C1
    SP2(("SP2")) --> C2["Controller 2"]
    PV2(("PV2")) --> C2
    C1 --> SEL{"High or Low\nSelector"}
    C2 --> SEL
    SEL --> FCV["Final Control\nElement (Valve)"]
    FCV --> PROC[("Process")]
    PROC --> PV1
    PROC --> PV2
    style SEL fill:#ffe066,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C1 fill:#a8d5ba,stroke:#333
    style C2 fill:#a8d5ba,stroke:#333
    style FCV fill:#f4a6a6,stroke:#333
    style PROC fill:#b8c6db,stroke:#333
```

### 4.2 Compressor Anti-Surge Override (Low Selector Architecture)

```mermaid
    FLOW_SP(("Flow SP")) --> FC["Flow Controller FC"]
    PV_FLOW(("Flow PV")) --> FC
    SURGE_SP(("Surge Line\nReference")) --> SC["Surge Controller SC"]
    PV_PRESS(("Differential\nPressure PV")) --> SC
    FC --> LSLS{"LOW SELECTOR"}
    SC --> LSLS
    LSLS --> RECYCLE_V["Anti-Surge\nRecycle Valve"]
    RECYCLE_V --> COMP[("Centrifugal\nCompressor")]
    COMP --> FLOW_PV
    COMP --> PRESS_PV

```

### 4.3 Fired Heater Fuel–Air Cross-Limiting Override

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T_SP(("T SP")) --> TC["Temperature\nController TC"]
    T_PV(("T PV")) --> TC
    F_AIR_PV(("Air Flow PV")) --> AFC["Air Flow\nController AFC"]
    F_AIR_SP(("Air Flow SP")) --> AFC
    F_FUEL_PV(("Fuel Flow PV")) --> FFC["Fuel Flow\nController FFC"]
    TC --> HS1{"HIGH\nSELECTOR 1"}
    FFC --> HS1
    HS1 --> FUEL_V["Fuel Valve"]
    AFC --> LSC{"LOW\nSELECTOR"}
    HS1 --> LSC
    LSC --> AIR_V["Air Damper"]
    FUEL_V --> HEATER[("Fired Heater")]
    AIR_V --> HEATER
    HEATER --> T_PV
    HEATER --> F_FUEL_PV
    HEATER --> F_AIR_PV
    style HS1 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
    style LSC fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px

    style AFC fill:#a8d5ba,stroke:#333
    style FFC fill:#a8d5ba,stroke:#333
    style FUEL_V fill:#f4a6a6,stroke:#333

```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Switching Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    STATE["Process Operating\nState Monitor"] --> DECIDE{"Which controller\noutput is most\nextreme?"}
    DECIDE -- "y1 is highest" --> R1["Route C1 → Final Element"]
    DECIDE -- "y2 is highest" --> R2["Route C2 → Final Element"]
    DECIDE -- "y3 is highest" --> R3["Route C3 → Final Element"]
    R1 --> FE["Final Control Element\n(Valve / VFD / Damper)"]
    R2 --> FE
    R3 --> FE
    FE --> PROC2[("Process Unit")]
    PROC2 --> STATE
    style DECIDE fill:#ffe066,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*Define Selective Control. Differentiate between a High Signal Selector and a Low Signal Selector with one industrial example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Selective control, also called override control, is an advanced control strategy in which a signal selector (high or low) chooses between the outputs of two or more controllers and routes the *most extreme* signal to a single final control element.

**High Signal Selector (1 mark):** Outputs the **maximum** of the controller outputs. **Example:** Fired-heater fuel limiting — the higher of (temperature controller output) and (fuel-flow limiter output) is sent to the fuel valve to prevent excess fuel.

**Low Signal Selector (1 mark):** Outputs the **minimum** of the controller outputs. **Example:** Compressor anti-surge — the lower of (flow controller output) and (surge controller output) is sent to the recycle valve to prevent surge.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*State any three industrial applications of selective control and identify the type of selector (HS/LS) used in each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

| # | Application | Selector Type |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compressor anti-surge protection | **Low Selector (LS)** |
| 2 | Fired heater fuel–air cross-limiting | **High Selector (HS)** |
| 3 | Boiler drum level on start-up | **LS (initially), then HS** |

---

### **Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compressor Anti-Surge Selective Control**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a centrifugal compressor anti-surge selective control system using a low signal selector. Explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The surge line of a compressor is described by $\Delta P_{surge} = 0.05\,Q^2 - 0.8\,Q + 6.0$ psia, where $Q$ is in MMSCFD. The measured differential pressure is $\Delta P_{meas} = 0.04\,Q^2 + 0.2\,Q + 4.0$ psia. Determine the **flow at which surge begins** and comment on the role of the low selector in protecting the compressor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) Block Diagram (5 marks) + Explanation (2 marks):**

```
   Flow SP ──► FC ──┐
   Flow PV ──► FC ──┤
                    ├──► LS ──► Recycle Valve ──► Compressor
   DP PV ───► SC ──┤                ▲
   Surge SP ► SC ──┘                │
                                    └──── (Recycle back to suction)
```

- **FC (Flow Controller):** Maintains design flow through the compressor.
- **SC (Surge Controller):** Computes error between measured $\Delta P$ and surge-line $\Delta P$.
- **LS (Low Selector):** Passes the **smaller** of the two controller outputs to the recycle valve. In normal operation FC output < SC output, so SC "wins" (valve mostly closed). When flow approaches the surge point, SC output rises above FC, and **SC takes over** to open the recycle valve and dump gas back to the suction, preventing surge.
- **Recycle Valve:** Final control element; opens more recycle → more anti-surge margin.

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Set $\Delta P_{meas} = \Delta P_{surge}$ to find the surge onset flow.

$$0.04\,Q^2 + 0.2\,Q + 4.0 = 0.05\,Q^2 - 0.8\,Q + 6.0$$

**Step 2:** Bring all terms to one side.

$$0 = 0.05\,Q^2 - 0.8\,Q + 6.0 - 0.04\,Q^2 - 0.2\,Q - 4.0$$

$$0 = 0.01\,Q^2 - 1.0\,Q + 2.0$$

**Step 3:** Solve using the quadratic formula.

$$Q = \frac{1.0 \pm \sqrt{1.0 - 0.08}}{0.02} = \frac{1.0 \pm \sqrt{0.92}}{0.02} = \frac{1.0 \pm 0.9592}{0.02}$$

**Step 4:** Two roots.

$$Q_1 = \frac{1.0 - 0.9592}{0.02} = \frac{0.0408}{0.02} = 2.04 \text{ MMSCFD}$$

$$Q_2 = \frac{1.0 + 0.9592}{0.02} = \frac{1.9592}{0.02} = 97.96 \text{ MMSCFD}$$

**Step 5:** The *physically meaningful* surge-onset flow is the **lower root** $Q_{surge} = 2.04$ MMSCFD. (The upper root is the stone-wall region.)

**Step 6:** Role of the Low Selector — As flow drops below 2.04 MMSCFD, the SC output rises rapidly (large positive error) and exceeds the FC output. The LS routes the SC signal to the recycle valve, which opens fully to return gas to the suction side. This **dumps volume** and pushes the operating point *away* from the surge line, preventing mechanical damage (bearing failure, blade erosion, motor overcurrent).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Selecting the *larger* root as the surge point. The surge point is the **lower** crossing of the two curves; the upper root is the **stone-wall** (choke) limit.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to state the *type* of selector and *why* it must be a Low Selector (fail-safe direction).
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing the **surge controller setpoint** (a curve, not a single value) with the measured variable. The SP for the SC is **not a constant**; it varies with flow.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not drawing the **recycle line** returning to the compressor suction — this is what makes the override *physically* protective.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Fired Heater Cross-Limiting Selective Control**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a fired-heater cross-limiting selective control system using both high and low signal selectors. Why is cross-limiting essential for combustion safety? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fired heater has a fuel-flow controller and an air-flow controller. The temperature controller output is 62%, the air-flow limiter output is 55%, and the fuel-flow controller output is 70%. The high-air/low-fuel selector logic is implemented. Identify which controller output actually reaches each final element and explain the safety implications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) Block Diagram (4 marks) + Explanation (3 marks):**

**Block Diagram:**

```
                              ┌──────────────┐
   T SP ──► TC ──► y_T ───────┤              │
                              │   HIGH SEL   ├──► Fuel Valve
   Fuel Limiter ──► y_FL ─────┤              │       │
                              └──────────────┘       │
                                                      ▼
                                                [Heater]
                                                      ▲
   Air SP ──► AFC ──► y_A ──────────────────────► Air Damper
                                                     │
                              ┌──────────────┐       │
              y_T / y_FL ─────┤   LOW SEL    ├──► Air Damper
              (selected)      │              │
                              └──────────────┘
```

**Working:** The temperature controller (TC) computes the *desired* fuel flow. A high selector (HS) compares it against a *fuel-flow limiter* output. The **HS routes the smaller demand** if the limiter is restrictive, else passes the TC output. The same selected signal is sent to a **low selector (LS)** along with the air-flow controller (AFC) output — whichever is **lower** becomes the air-damper command. This ensures the air supply *always* tracks (or exceeds) the fuel demand with a safe stoichiometric margin, preventing soot formation, flame instability, and explosion.

**Why cross-limiting is essential (3 marks):**
1. Prevents **oxygen-deficient combustion** → no soot, no CO, no flame-out.
2. Avoids **flashback** by ensuring air is established before fuel.
3. Satisfies **NFPA 85** and **OSHA** combustion-safety codes.

**(b) Identifying the winners (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Fuel side — High Selector compares $y_T = 62\%$ vs $y_{FL} = 70\%$. The higher of the two **wins on the high selector** for a *high* signal selector routed to the *air* damper… wait, examine the configuration: in the *standard* cross-limiting scheme, the HS is used to **limit** the fuel (the higher of TC and FL is *not* what we want). 

Let us re-read the problem carefully: "high-air/low-fuel selector logic." This means the **air damper receives the higher signal** (so air is generous) and the **fuel valve receives the lower signal** (so fuel is limited).

**Step 2:** Re-applying logic with the stated convention:

- **Air damper** (high-air rule): receives $\max(y_T, y_{FL}) = \max(62\%, 70\%) = 70\%$
- **Fuel valve** (low-fuel rule): receives $\min(y_T, y_{FL}) = \min(62\%, 70\%) = 62\%$ (i.e., the temperature controller output, which is more conservative).

The AFC output (55%) is *ignored* on the air side because the cross-limit signal is higher.

**Step 3:** Safety implications (3 marks):
- Fuel is **capped at 62%** by the temperature controller, even though the limiter is asking for 70%. The heater cannot receive more fuel than the temperature demand justifies.
- Air is **pushed to 70%** to ensure excess oxygen for the 62% fuel flow. Air-to-fuel ratio = $70/62 = 1.13$, which is *above* the stoichiometric ratio → safe combustion.
- If the AFC output (55%) had been routed, the air-fuel ratio would have been $55/70 = 0.79$ — *fuel-rich* — leading to incomplete combustion and explosion risk.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Misapplying the selector direction. In a *cross-limiting* loop, the **HS and LS are paired** — air gets the *higher* signal, fuel gets the *lower* signal. Do not blindly write $\max$ for fuel.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Not computing the actual air–fuel ratio and showing the safety margin quantitatively.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting the *cascade* structure inside the cross-limit loop — the air-flow controller (AFC) is the *secondary* of a cascade whose primary is the cross-limit selector. This is a high-yield follow-up question.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Selective control = **override control** using a signal selector (HS or LS).
- ✅ **No tuning parameters** for the selector itself — it is a static, memoryless nonlinearity.
- ✅ **HS = max**, **LS = min** of input signals.
- ✅ **LS is used for fail-safe / conservative action** (compressor anti-surge, low-flow protection).
- ✅ **HS is used for demand-following / permissive action** (fired heater air supply, high-pressure override).
- ✅ The **losing controller** stays alive in open-loop standby; tuning must be stable in both modes.
- ✅ **Bumpless transfer** is automatic because both signals are continuously updated.
- ✅ Compressor surge line is **flow-dependent**; override setpoint is a *curve*, not a single value.
- ✅ Cross-limiting requires **both HS and LS** working in coordinated fashion.
- ✅ Distinguish from cascade: cascade is *series* (one primary, one secondary), selective is *parallel* (two primaries, one actuator).
- ✅ Industrial examples: anti-surge, cross-limiting, drum level, reactor T/P override, condenser pressure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Based Controllers: Internal Model Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET502 - PROCESS DYNAMICS AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Model Based Controllers: Internal Model Control # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Internal Model Control (IMC)** is a model-based control strategy in which an explicit mathematical model of the process (the *internal model*) is incorporated inside the feedback control loop to predict the process output and generate a control action. The IMC structure, originally proposed by Garcia and Morari (1982), decouples the controller design into two independent tasks: **(i)** inversion of the process model to compute the controller, and **(ii)** addition of a low-pass filter to ensure robustness and physical realizability. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > IMC is a cornerstone topic in Model-Based Control. The KTU 2024 Scheme requires students to design the IMC controller for **FOPDT** and **SOPDT** processes, derive the **equivalent classical feedback controller**, and use IMC principles to obtain **PID tuning rules**. ## 1.2 The IMC Philosophy — "Predict, Then Act" The classical feedback controller reacts to the error *after* the disturbance has already moved the process. IMC, by contrast, *anticipates* this movement because it possesses a copy of the process inside the controller. It is essentially a **feed-forward action computed from a model**, wrapped inside a feedback loop for robustness. ## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The Cruise Control with a Map > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy: "The Driver with a Co-Pilot's Map"** > > Imagine you are driving a car on a hilly highway. > - **Classical feedback (PID):** You stare at the speedometer and *react* whenever the speed deviates from the setpoint. By the time you press the accelerator, the car has already slowed down on an incline. > - **Internal Model Control:** Your co-pilot holds a **map (the internal model)** that tells the driver: *"There is a 5% incline in 200 m."* The driver pre-emptively presses the accelerator **before** the speed can drop. If the actual speed deviates (e.g., due to a headwind not on the map), the speedometer feedback **corrects** the error. > > The **map = internal model G̃p(s)**, the **co-pilot's instructions = IMC controller Gc,IMC(s)**, and the **speedometer correction = feedback path**. ## 1.4 Block Diagram Symbols (KTU Convention) | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | $Y_{sp}(s)$ | Setpoint (reference) | | $U(s)$ | Manipulated variable (controller output) | | $D(s)$ | Load disturbance | | $G_p(s)$ | True (actual) process transfer function | | $\tilde{G}_p(s)$ | Internal model (approximation of $G_p(s)$) | | $G_{c,IMC}(s)$ | IMC controller | | $F(s)$ | IMC filter (low-pass, ensures causality/robustness) | | $Y(s)$ | Process output | | $\tilde{Y}(s)$ | Model output (predicted response) | | $E(s)$ | Feedback error $Y(s) - \tilde{Y}(s)$ | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** In a perfect model (i.e., $\tilde{G}_p(s) = G_p(s)$) and in the absence of disturbances, the feedback signal $E(s) = 0$, so the loop is effectively **open**. The control action is then purely feed-forward from the setpoint. ## 1.5 Standard IMC Algorithm (High-Level) For a process with transfer function $\tilde{G}_p(s)$, the IMC controller is generally constructed as: $$ G_{c,IMC}(s) = \tilde{G}_p^{-1}(s) \cdot F(s) $$ where $F(s)$ is a low-pass filter of the form: $$ F(s) = \frac{1}{(\lambda s + 1)^r} $$ The parameter $\lambda$ (lambda) is the **IMC tuning parameter**; $r$ is chosen so that $G_{c,IMC}(s)$ becomes **semi-proper or strictly proper** (no derivative action on the noise). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The IMC Block Diagram — Step-by-Step Operational Logic The standard IMC structure processes signals as follows: 1. The setpoint $Y_{sp}(s)$ is compared with the model output $\tilde{Y}(s)$ to form the input to the IMC controller: $Y_{sp}(s) - \tilde{Y}(s)$. 2. The IMC controller $G_{c,IMC}(s)$ converts this error into a control signal $U(s)$. 3. $U(s)$ is applied **simultaneously** to the real process $G_p(s)$ and to the internal model $\tilde{G}_p(s)$. 4. The real process output $Y(s)$ is compared with the model output $\tilde{Y}(s)$; their difference $E(s) = Y(s) - \tilde{Y}(s)$ is the **IMC feedback signal**. 5. If the model is perfect, $E(s) = 0$ and the loop behaves as open-loop feed-forward control. ## 2.2 IMC Design Procedure (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard) The systematic IMC design procedure consists of **four rigorous steps**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 4-Step IMC Design Recipe** > 1. **Factor the model** as $\tilde{G}_p(s) = \tilde{G}_p^+(s) \cdot \tilde{G}_p^-(s)$, where $\tilde{G}_p^+(s)$ contains all **right-half-plane (RHP) zeros and time delays** (non-invertible part), and $\tilde{G}_p^-(s)$ is the **minimum-phase part** (invertible). > 2. **Define the IMC controller** as the invertible part inverse times a filter: $G_{c,IMC}(s) = [\tilde{G}_p^-(s)]^{-1} \cdot F(s)$. > 3. **Select the filter** $F(s) = \dfrac{1}{(\lambda s + 1)^r}$ with order $r$ high enough to make the controller proper, and $\lambda > 0$ for stability. > 4. **Tune $\lambda$** — small $\lambda$ gives aggressive (fast) control; large $\lambda$ gives smooth (robust) control. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The table below consolidates all IMC and IMC-PID formulas expected in the KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination. | Process Type | Process Model $\tilde{G}_p(s)$ | IMC Controller $G_{c,IMC}(s)$ | Equivalent PID Settings | |---|---|---|---| | FOPDT | $\dfrac{K\,e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$ | $\dfrac{\tau s + 1}{K(\lambda s + 1)}$ | $K_c = \dfrac{\tau}{K(\lambda + \theta)}$, $\tau_I = \tau$, $\tau_D = 0$ (PI) | | SOPDT (overdamped) | $\dfrac{K\,e^{-\theta s}}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}$ | $\dfrac{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}{K(\lambda s + 1)^2}$ | $K_c = \dfrac{\tau_1 + \tau_2}{K(\lambda + \theta)}$, $\tau_I = \tau_1 + \tau_2$, $\tau_D = \dfrac{\tau_1 \tau_2}{\tau_1 + \tau_2}$ | | First-order + integrator | $\dfrac{K\,e^{-\theta s}}{s(\tau s + 1)}$ | $\dfrac{s(\tau s + 1)}{K(\lambda s + 1)^2}$ | $K_c = \dfrac{1}{K(\lambda + \theta)}$, $\tau_I = \tau + \theta$ (approximate) | | Pure dead time | $K\,e^{-\theta s}$ | $\dfrac{1}{K(\lambda s + 1)}$ | $K_c = \dfrac{1}{K\lambda}$ | | Filter order rule | — | $F(s) = \dfrac{1}{(\lambda s + 1)^r}$, $r \geq$ (relative degree of $\tilde{G}_p^-$) | — | | Equivalent feedback controller | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{G_{c,IMC}(s)}{1 - G_{c,IMC}(s)\,\tilde{G}_p(s)}$ | — | — | | Closed-loop servo response (perfect model, no disturbance) | $Y(s) = G_{c,IMC}(s) G_p(s) F(s)\,Y_{sp}(s)$ | — | — | | Closed-loop disturbance response | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{D(s)} = \dfrac{G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)G_p(s)} = G_p(s)\left[1 - G_{c,IMC}(s)\tilde{G}_p(s)\right]$ | — | — | > [!NOTE] > **Filter Order Rule (KTU Favourite):** For a minimum-phase process with no time delay and relative degree $n$, choose $r = n$. For FOPDT with delay, the delay is treated as $\tilde{G}_p^+$ (not inverted) and $r = 1$. ## 2.4 Why the Filter is *Non-Negotiable* — The "Why" Behind Each Step | Step | Reason (the "Why") | |---|---| | Factor into $\tilde{G}_p^+$ and $\tilde{G}_p^-$ | Inverting time delays and RHP zeros is **impossible** in real hardware. The + part is left alone. | | Multiply inverse by filter $F(s)$ | The pure inverse $\left[\tilde{G}_p^-\right]^{-1}$ is often **improper** (more zeros than poles), giving infinite high-frequency gain — disastrous for noise. The filter makes it **proper**. | | Choose $r$ | Ensures the controller rolls off at high frequency, providing **measurement noise rejection**. | | Tune $\lambda$ | The single knob that trades **speed vs. robustness**; the only knob an operator touches. | ## 2.5 IMC for Setpoint Tracking vs. Disturbance Rejection > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction (frequent KTU question):** > - For **setpoint tracking** in the ideal case ($\tilde{G}_p = G_p$, $D = 0$): $Y = G_{c,IMC} \cdot G_p \cdot F \cdot Y_{sp}$. The closed-loop transfer function depends on $F(s)$ — so $\lambda$ directly shapes the response. > - For **disturbance rejection**: the closed-loop transfer function is $Y/G_p = 1 - G_{c,IMC}\tilde{G}_p = 1 - F(s)$. **Larger $\lambda$ gives slower disturbance rejection** — there is no free lunch. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | IMC Application | |---|---| | Petroleum refineries | Distillation column temperature/level control where time delays dominate | | Pharmaceutical batch reactors | pH and temperature control under changing feed conditions | | Power plants | Boiler steam-temperature control (long delays, multi-time-constant) | | HVAC systems | Building zone temperature control (large thermal mass) | | Chemical processes | Continuous stirred-tank reactor concentration control | > [!IMPORTANT] > IMC is the **theoretical foundation** of the most widely used industrial PID tuning methods: **IMC-PID**, **Cohen-Coon**, and the **Lambda tuning method** of Dahlin. Every KTU question on PID tuning is implicitly testing whether the student can derive the IMC origin of those rules. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation A — Derivation of the Equivalent Classical Feedback Controller The IMC block diagram is algebraically equivalent to a classical feedback loop. We derive the equivalent controller $G_c(s)$ to recover a standard PID form. **Given:** The IMC control law is $$ U(s) = G_{c,IMC}(s)\left[Y_{sp}(s) - \tilde{Y}(s)\right] $$ and the model output is $\tilde{Y}(s) = \tilde{G}_p(s)\,U(s)$. **Step 1.** Substitute the model output into the IMC control law: $$ U(s) = G_{c,IMC}(s)\left[Y_{sp}(s) - \tilde{G}_p(s)\,U(s)\right] $$ **Step 2.** Expand the right-hand side: $$ U(s) = G_{c,IMC}(s)\,Y_{sp}(s) - G_{c,IMC}(s)\,\tilde{G}_p(s)\,U(s) $$ **Step 3.** Collect all $U(s)$ terms on the left-hand side: $$ U(s) + G_{c,IMC}(s)\,\tilde{G}_p(s)\,U(s) = G_{c,IMC}(s)\,Y_{sp}(s) $$ **Step 4.** Factor out $U(s)$: $$ U(s)\left[1 + G_{c,IMC}(s)\,\tilde{G}_p(s)\right] = G_{c,IMC}(s)\,Y_{sp}(s) $$ **Step 5.** Solve for $U(s) / Y_{sp}(s)$ — this is the equivalent feedback controller $G_c(s)$: $$ G_c(s) = \frac{U(s)}{Y_{sp}(s)} = \frac{G_{c,IMC}(s)}{1 + G_{c,IMC}(s)\,\tilde{G}_p(s)} $$ > [!NOTE] > The textbook sign (with negative feedback $Y_{sp} - Y$) gives the conventional $G_c = G_{c,IMC}/(1 - G_{c,IMC}\tilde{G}_p)$ depending on the sign convention used in the block diagram. The KTU 2024 standard uses the convention above (signs cancel between the + and - summing junctions in the IMC diagram), giving the form shown in the formula table. ## 3.2 Derivation B — IMC Controller for an FOPDT Process **Given model:** $$ \tilde{G}_p(s) = \frac{K\,e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1} $$ **Step 1.** Factor into invertible and non-invertible parts: $$ \tilde{G}_p^+(s) = e^{-\theta s}, \qquad \tilde{G}_p^-(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} $$ The delay is not inverted because it would require a predictor (impossible in real time). **Step 2.** Invert the minimum-phase part: $$ [\tilde{G}_p^-(s)]^{-1} = \frac{\tau s + 1}{K} $$ This is **proper** (equal order numerator and denominator), so $r = 1$ suffices. **Step 3.** Append the first-order filter $F(s) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda s + 1}$: $$ G_{c,IMC}(s) = \frac{\tau s + 1}{K} \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda s + 1} = \frac{\tau s + 1}{K(\lambda s + 1)} $$ > [!NOTE] > This result is the cornerstone IMC formula for FOPDT processes and is **directly asked** in KTU Module 4 questions. ## 3.3 Derivation C — Conversion of the IMC Controller to a PI Controller **Step 1.** Write the equivalent feedback controller using the result of Derivation A: $$ G_c(s) = \frac{G_{c,IMC}(s)}{1 - G_{c,IMC}(s)\,\tilde{G}_p(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{\tau s + 1}{K(\lambda s + 1)}}{1 - \dfrac{\tau s + 1}{K(\lambda s + 1)} \cdot \dfrac{K\,e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}} $$ **Step 2.** Simplify the denominator. The product $G_{c,IMC} \tilde{G}_p$ gives: $$ G_{c,IMC}(s)\,\tilde{G}_p(s) = \frac{\tau s + 1}{K(\lambda s + 1)} \cdot \frac{K\,e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1} = \frac{e^{-\theta s}}{\lambda s + 1} $$ **Step 3.** Substitute into $G_c(s)$: $$ G_c(s) = \frac{\dfrac{\tau s + 1}{K(\lambda s + 1)}}{1 - \dfrac{e^{-\theta s}}{\lambda s + 1}} = \frac{\tau s + 1}{K(\lambda s + 1) - K\,e^{-\theta s}} $$ **Step 4.** Approximate the dead time using a **first-order Padé approximation** $e^{-\theta s} \approx \dfrac{1 - 0.5\theta s}{1 + 0.5\theta s}$ (this is the standard KTU approach): $$ G_c(s) = \frac{\tau s + 1}{K(\lambda s + 1) - K\,\dfrac{1 - 0.5\theta s}{1 + 0.5\theta s}} $$ **Step 5.** Combine the denominator over a common denominator $1 + 0.5\theta s$: $$ \text{Denominator} = \frac{K(\lambda s + 1)(1 + 0.5\theta s) - K(1 - 0.5\theta s)}{1 + 0.5\theta s} $$ **Step 6.** Expand $(\lambda s + 1)(1 + 0.5\theta s)$: $$ (\lambda s + 1)(1 + 0.5\theta s) = 0.5\lambda\theta s^2 + (\lambda + 0.5\theta)s + 1 $$ **Step 7.** Subtract $(1 - 0.5\theta s)$: $$ \left[0.5\lambda\theta s^2 + (\lambda + 0.5\theta)s + 1\right] - \left[1 - 0.5\theta s\right] = 0.5\lambda\theta s^2 + (\lambda + \theta)s $$ **Step 8.** Therefore the equivalent controller is: $$ G_c(s) = \frac{(\tau s + 1)(1 + 0.5\theta s)}{K\,s\left[0.5\lambda\theta s + (\lambda + \theta)\right]} $$ **Step 9.** This is a **PI controller** in series with a first-order lag. Matching the standard PI form $G_c(s) = K_c\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$ (combined with the first-order lag), the **IMC-PI tuning rules** are: $$ K_c = \frac{\tau}{K(\lambda + \theta)}, \qquad \tau_I = \tau $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examination Key Result:** > $K_c = \dfrac{\tau}{K(\lambda + \theta)}, \quad \tau_I = \tau, \quad \tau_D = 0$ — for FOPDT processes, IMC reduces to **PI control** with these explicit formulas. ## 3.4 Derivation D — IMC Controller for an SOPDT Process **Given model:** $$ \tilde{G}_p(s) = \frac{K\,e^{-\theta s}}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)} $$ **Step 1.** Factor as $\tilde{G}_p^+ = e^{-\theta s}$ and $\tilde{G}_p^- = \dfrac{K}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}$. **Step 2.** Invert the minimum-phase part: $$ [\tilde{G}_p^-]^{-1} = \frac{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}{K} $$ This inverse has **two zeros and zero poles** — it is **improper**. We need a second-order filter $F(s) = \dfrac{1}{(\lambda s + 1)^2}$ to make the controller strictly proper. **Step 3.** Construct the IMC controller: $$ G_{c,IMC}(s) = \frac{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}{K(\lambda s + 1)^2} $$ **Step 4.** For a **PID realization**, factor $(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1) = \tau_1\tau_2 s^2 + (\tau_1 + \tau_2)s + 1$ and group terms to obtain the **IMC-PID** settings (see formula sheet): $$ K_c = \frac{\tau_1 + \tau_2}{K(\lambda + \theta)}, \qquad \tau_I = \tau_1 + \tau_2, \qquad \tau_D = \frac{\tau_1\tau_2}{\tau_1 + \tau_2} $$ ## 3.5 Derivation E — Closed-Loop Disturbance Response **Starting point:** The closed-loop output is $$ Y(s) = G_p(s)\,U(s) + G_d(s)\,D(s) $$ and the IMC law is $U(s) = G_{c,IMC}(s)\left[Y_{sp}(s) - \tilde{G}_p(s)\,U(s)\right]$, giving $$ U(s) = \frac{G_{c,IMC}(s)}{1 + G_{c,IMC}(s)\tilde{G}_p(s)}Y_{sp}(s) $$ **Step 1.** Substitute into $Y(s)$: $$ Y(s) = \frac{G_p(s)\,G_{c,IMC}(s)}{1 + G_{c,IMC}(s)\tilde{G}_p(s)}Y_{sp}(s) + G_d(s)D(s) $$ **Step 2.** Using $G_{c,IMC} = \tilde{G}_p^{-1}F$, simplify $G_{c,IMC}\tilde{G}_p = F$: $$ Y(s) = \frac{G_p(s)}{\tilde{G}_p(s)}\cdot\frac{F(s)}{1 + F(s)}Y_{sp}(s) + G_d(s)D(s) $$ **Step 3.** For perfect model ($G_p = \tilde{G}_p$), the disturbance response simplifies by noting the IMC signal flow. Following the standard algebra (Garcia & Morari), the closed-loop response becomes: $$ Y(s) = G_p(s)\,G_{c,IMC}(s)\,F(s)\,Y_{sp}(s) + \left[1 - G_{c,IMC}(s)\tilde{G}_p(s)\right]G_d(s)\,D(s) $$ **Step 4.** Substitute $G_{c,IMC}\tilde{G}_p = F$: $$ Y(s) = G_p(s)\,F(s)\,Y_{sp}(s) + \left[1 - F(s)\right]G_d(s)\,D(s) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The setpoint response is filtered by $F(s)$ (smooth tracking), and the disturbance response is filtered by $[1 - F(s)]$ (smooth rejection). Both are governed by the single tuning knob $\lambda$. ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard) **Problem:** A temperature sensor in a mixing tank gives the FOPDT model $$ \tilde{G}_p(s) = \frac{2\,e^{-1.2s}}{3s + 1} $$ Design an IMC controller with $\lambda = 0.8$. Find the equivalent PI settings. **Step 1.** Identify parameters: $K = 2$, $\tau = 3$, $\theta = 1.2$, $\lambda = 0.8$. **Step 2.** Apply the IMC-PI formulas: $$ K_c = \frac{\tau}{K(\lambda + \theta)} = \frac{3}{2(0.8 + 1.2)} = \frac{3}{2 \times 2.0} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 $$ $$ \tau_I = \tau = 3 \text{ min}, \qquad \tau_D = 0 $$ **Step 3.** Express the controller in PID form: $$ G_c(s) = K_c\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s}\right) = 0.75\left(1 + \frac{1}{3s}\right) $$ **Step 4.** Sanity check: $\lambda + \theta = 2.0$. Compare with the open-loop response time of $\tau = 3$. The closed-loop speed is governed by $\lambda = 0.8$, which is **smaller than $\tau$** (aggressive tuning). > [!TIP] > **Quick rule of thumb for choosing $\lambda$:** > $\lambda \approx 0.8\,\tau$ for tight control, or $\lambda \approx \tau$ for moderate control, or $\lambda \approx 2\tau$ for very robust / slow control. A safe starting point is $\lambda = \theta$ (set the filter time constant equal to the dead time). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 IMC Block-Level Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR spNode["Ysp_s"] --> sumA(("+")) sumA --> diffNode["e_IMC_s"] diffNode --> imcCtrlNode["Gc_IMC_s"] imcCtrlNode --> uNode["U_s"] uNode --> procNode["Gp_s"] uNode --> modelNode["Gtilde_p_s"] procNode --> yNode["Y_s"] modelNode --> yTildeNode["Ytilde_s"] yTildeNode --> sumA yNode --> sumB(("+")) sumB --> dNode["D_s"] dNode --> gdNode["Gd_s"] gdNode --> sumB ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The setpoint $Y_{sp}$ is compared with the model output $\tilde{Y}$ at the first summing junction. The IMC controller $G_{c,IMC}$ generates $U$, which drives both the true process $G_p$ and the model $\tilde{G}_p$ in parallel. The load disturbance $D$ enters through $G_d$ at the process output. ## 4.2 IMC-to-Classical-Feedback Equivalence (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR spNode2["Ysp_s"] --> sumC(("+")) sumC --> errNode["E_s"] errNode --> fbCtrlNode["Gc_s"] fbCtrlNode --> uNode2["U_s"] uNode2 --> procNode2["Gp_s"] procNode2 --> yNode2["Y_s"] yNode2 --> sumC ``` > [!NOTE] > **Equivalence principle:** The IMC structure can be redrawn as a standard unity-feedback loop with controller $G_c(s) = \dfrac{G_{c,IMC}(s)}{1 - G_{c,IMC}(s)\tilde{G}_p(s)}$. This is the algebraic manipulation derived in Section 3.1. ## 4.3 Internal Model Controller Design Flow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode["Start: Given process model Gtilde_p_s"] --> factorNode["Step 1: Factor as Gtilde_plus x Gtilde_minus"] factorNode --> invNode["Step 2: Invert minimum-phase part"] invNode --> filterNode["Step 3: Choose filter F_s = 1 over lambda s + 1 to power r"] filterNode --> imcNode["Step 4: Form Gc_IMC = inv Gtilde_minus times F"] imcNode --> tuneNode["Step 5: Tune lambda for speed vs robustness"] tuneNode --> equivNode["Step 6: Convert to equivalent PID if required"] equivNode --> doneNode["End: Apply controller in closed loop"] ``` ## 4.4 Effect of $\lambda$ on Closed-Loop Step Response (Conceptual Sketch) ```mermaid flowchart LR smallLambda["lambda = 0.1 tau
Aggressive
Fast rise, high overshoot
Poor robustness"] medLambda["lambda = 0.8 tau
Balanced
Moderate speed
Good robustness"] largeLambda["lambda = 2 tau
Conservative
Slow rise, low overshoot
Excellent robustness"] smallLambda --> medLambda --> largeLambda ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Visual Takeaway:** As $\lambda$ increases from $0$ to $\infty$, the closed-loop response transitions from "almost inverse-based bang-bang" to "almost open-loop sluggish." The student must be able to sketch this trade-off curve qualitatively in the exam. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Differentiate between classical feedback control and Internal Model Control (IMC). Mention any two advantages of IMC.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Classical Feedback (PID) | Internal Model Control | |---|---|---| | Model usage | Implicit (used only for tuning) | Explicit (model is inside the loop) | | Reaction type | Reactive (acts on error) | Predictive + reactive | | Disturbance handling | Through feedback alone | Feedback corrected by model prediction | | Tuning | Empirical rules (Z-N, Cohen-Coon) | Analytical (based on $\lambda$) | | Robustness to model error | Depends on gain margin | Single parameter $\lambda$ trades speed vs. robustness | **Two advantages of IMC:** 1. **Analytical tuning:** Only one knob ($\lambda$) to adjust — easy to operate. 2. **Perfect control for perfect model:** In the ideal case ($\tilde{G}_p = G_p$, $D = 0$), the IMC loop becomes open-loop feed-forward, yielding ideal setpoint tracking. 3. *(Bonus point)* Decouples controller design into inversion + filtering, making it modular and easy to understand. --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is the role of the filter $F(s)$ in an IMC structure? Why is it necessary?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The filter $F(s) = \dfrac{1}{(\lambda s + 1)^r}$ performs three critical functions: 1. **Makes the controller physically realizable (proper):** The pure inverse $[\tilde{G}_p^-]^{-1}$ is often improper (more zeros than poles), giving infinite high-frequency gain. The filter rolls off this gain to a finite value. 2. **Ensures robustness to model-plant mismatch:** By attenuating high-frequency action, the filter prevents the controller from amplifying small model errors, which would otherwise cause instability. 3. **Provides a single tuning knob ($\lambda$):** A larger $\lambda$ gives a slower, smoother, more robust response; a smaller $\lambda$ gives a faster but more aggressive response. Without $F(s)$, the IMC controller would be unrealizable and the closed loop would be **highly sensitive to noise and modelling error**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Choice Q1] **(a)** Derive the IMC controller for a first-order plus dead time (FOPDT) process with transfer function $$ \tilde{G}_p(s) = \frac{K\,e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1} $$ Explain the role of each term in the final controller expression. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** For the FOPDT process with $K = 1.5$, $\tau = 5$ min, $\theta = 2$ min, design an IMC-based PI controller with $\lambda = 1.5$ min. Compute the closed-loop servo transfer function. Comment on the effect of increasing $\lambda$ by a factor of 2. **\[7 marks\]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1. Factor the model.** $$ \tilde{G}_p^+(s) = e^{-\theta s} \quad (\text{non-invertible, the dead time}) $$ $$ \tilde{G}_p^-(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} \quad (\text{minimum phase, invertible}) $$ **Step 2. Invert $\tilde{G}_p^-$:** $$ [\tilde{G}_p^-(s)]^{-1} = \frac{\tau s + 1}{K} $$ **Step 3. The result is already proper** (one zero, one pole), so a first-order filter $F(s) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda s + 1}$ is sufficient. **Step 4. IMC controller:** $$ \boxed{G_{c,IMC}(s) = \frac{\tau s + 1}{K(\lambda s + 1)}} $$ **Role of each term:** - $(\tau s + 1)$ — **process zero** that cancels the process pole $\dfrac{1}{\tau s + 1}$, achieving perfect model inversion. - $1/K$ — **steady-state gain inversion**, ensuring $G_{c,IMC}(0) \cdot K = 1$. - $1/(\lambda s + 1)$ — **filter** for realizability and robustness; introduces the closed-loop time constant $\lambda$. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1. Identify parameters:** $K = 1.5$, $\tau = 5$, $\theta = 2$, $\lambda = 1.5$. **Step 2. Compute PI settings using IMC-PI formulas:** $$ K_c = \frac{\tau}{K(\lambda + \theta)} = \frac{5}{1.5(1.5 + 2)} = \frac{5}{1.5 \times 3.5} = \frac{5}{5.25} = 0.952 $$ $$ \tau_I = \tau = 5 \text{ min} $$ **Step 3. Closed-loop servo transfer function (perfect model, no disturbance):** $$ \frac{Y(s)}{Y_{sp}(s)} = G_p(s)\,G_{c,IMC}(s)\,F(s) = \frac{K\,e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1} \cdot \frac{\tau s + 1}{K(\lambda s + 1)} \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda s + 1} $$ The $(\tau s + 1)$ and $K$ terms cancel, leaving: $$ \boxed{\frac{Y(s)}{Y_{sp}(s)} = \frac{e^{-\theta s}}{(\lambda s + 1)^2} = \frac{e^{-2s}}{(1.5 s + 1)^2}} $$ **Step 4. Effect of doubling $\lambda$ to $3.0$:** New closed-loop transfer function: $$ \frac{Y(s)}{Y_{sp}(s)} = \frac{e^{-2s}}{(3.0 s + 1)^2} $$ - **Slower response** — the rise time increases (approximately $\lambda$ scales the closed-loop time constant). - **Less aggressive** — overshoot and oscillation decrease. - **More robust** — improved rejection of model errors and measurement noise. --- ### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice Q2] **(a)** For a second-order overdamped process with model $$ \tilde{G}_p(s) = \frac{K\,e^{-\theta s}}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)} $$ derive the IMC controller and obtain the equivalent IMC-PID settings. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** A chemical reactor has the SOPDT model $K = 0.8$, $\tau_1 = 4$ min, $\tau_2 = 2$ min, $\theta = 1$ min. Design an IMC controller with $\lambda = 1.2$ min. Compute the controller gain $K_c$, integral time $\tau_I$, and derivative time $\tau_D$. **\[7 marks\]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1. Factor the model:** $$ \tilde{G}_p^+(s) = e^{-\theta s}, \qquad \tilde{G}_p^-(s) = \frac{K}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)} $$ **Step 2. Invert the minimum-phase part:** $$ [\tilde{G}_p^-(s)]^{-1} = \frac{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}{K} $$ **Step 3. The inverse is improper** (second-order numerator, no denominator). Use a second-order filter $F(s) = \dfrac{1}{(\lambda s + 1)^2}$. **Step 4. IMC controller:** $$ \boxed{G_{c,IMC}(s) = \frac{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}{K(\lambda s + 1)^2}} $$ **Step 5. Convert to PID form.** Expand the numerator: $$ (\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1) = \tau_1\tau_2 s^2 + (\tau_1 + \tau_2)s + 1 $$ Apply the Padé approximation to the delay, then rearrange to obtain the **IMC-PID** settings (using the formula sheet): $$ \boxed{K_c = \frac{\tau_1 + \tau_2}{K(\lambda + \theta)}, \quad \tau_I = \tau_1 + \tau_2, \quad \tau_D = \frac{\tau_1 \tau_2}{\tau_1 + \tau_2}} $$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1. Parameters:** $K = 0.8$, $\tau_1 = 4$, $\tau_2 = 2$, $\theta = 1$, $\lambda = 1.2$. **Step 2. Compute the IMC-PID settings:** $$ K_c = \frac{\tau_1 + \tau_2}{K(\lambda + \theta)} = \frac{4 + 2}{0.8(1.2 + 1)} = \frac{6}{0.8 \times 2.2} = \frac{6}{1.76} = 3.409 $$ $$ \tau_I = \tau_1 + \tau_2 = 4 + 2 = 6 \text{ min} $$ $$ \tau_D = \frac{\tau_1 \tau_2}{\tau_1 + \tau_2} = \frac{4 \times 2}{4 + 2} = \frac{8}{6} = 1.333 \text{ min} $$ **Step 3. Summary:** $$ \boxed{K_c \approx 3.41, \quad \tau_I = 6 \text{ min}, \quad \tau_D = 1.33 \text{ min}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the filter order:** Many students write $F(s) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda s + 1}$ for an SOPDT process, leaving the controller **improper** (2 zeros, 1 pole). The correct filter order is $r = 2$ for SOPDT — **this costs 2 marks**. > 2. **Inverting the dead time:** Students sometimes write $\dfrac{\tau_1 s + 1}{K\,\lambda s\,e^{\theta s}}$, attempting to invert the delay. This is **physically impossible** and loses all subsequent marks. > 3. **Wrong $\tau_D$ formula:** $\tau_D = \dfrac{\tau_1\tau_2}{\tau_1+\tau_2}$ is the **series-equivalent** of two first-order lags. Writing $\tau_D = \tau_1\tau_2$ (product) or $\tau_D = \tau_1 - \tau_2$ (difference) is a common error. > 4. **Forgetting units:** Always state minutes (or seconds) for time constants. Examiners deduct ½ mark for missing units. > 5. **Ignoring $\theta$ in the gain formula:** $K_c = \dfrac{\tau_1+\tau_2}{K\lambda}$ (without $\theta$) is the **no-delay** result. For processes with measurable dead time, the $\theta$ term **must** appear in the denominator. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for IMC (Module 4):** - **IMC structure:** Setpoint $\to$ (+) $\to$ $G_{c,IMC}$ $\to$ $U$ $\to$ both real process $G_p$ and model $\tilde{G}_p$ in parallel. Feedback signal = $Y - \tilde{Y}$. - **Core IMC formula:** $G_{c,IMC}(s) = [\tilde{G}_p^-(s)]^{-1} \cdot F(s)$. - **Filter:** $F(s) = \dfrac{1}{(\lambda s + 1)^r}$; order $r$ chosen so the controller is proper; $\lambda > 0$ is the single tuning parameter. - **Factorization rule:** Time delays and RHP zeros go into $\tilde{G}_p^+$; minimum-phase part into $\tilde{G}_p^-$. - **FOPDT IMC-PI:** $K_c = \dfrac{\tau}{K(\lambda + \theta)}$, $\tau_I = \tau$, $\tau_D = 0$. - **SOPDT IMC-PID:** $K_c = \dfrac{\tau_1+\tau_2}{K(\lambda+\theta)}$, $\tau_I = \tau_1+\tau_2$, $\tau_D = \dfrac{\tau_1\tau_2}{\tau_1+\tau_2}$. - **Equivalent feedback form:** $G_c(s) = \dfrac{G_{c,IMC}(s)}{1 - G_{c,IMC}(s)\tilde{G}_p(s)}$. - **Closed-loop servo response (perfect model):** $\dfrac{Y}{Y_{sp}} = G_p(s)\,F(s)$ — depends only on $F$. - **Closed-loop disturbance response:** $\dfrac{Y}{D} = G_d(s)[1 - F(s)]$ — depends on $1 - F$. - **Tuning guidance:** $\lambda \approx 0.8\tau$ (tight), $\lambda \approx \tau$ (balanced), $\lambda \approx 2\tau$ (robust). - **Padé approximation:** $e^{-\theta s} \approx \dfrac{1 - 0.5\theta s}{1 + 0.5\theta s}$ — used to convert the delay into a rational form for PID extraction. - **Practical limit:** $\lambda \geq 0.1\,\theta$ to avoid controller windup and excessive noise amplification; $\lambda \geq 0.2\,\tau$ is the conservative lower bound. - **Relationship to Ziegler-Nichols:** The IMC method is generally **less aggressive** than Z-N, providing smoother responses with better robustness — the standard "modern" alternative.

topics.title: Model Predictive Controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET502 - PROCESS DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Model Predictive Controller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** is an advanced multivariable control algorithm that uses an explicit dynamic process model to predict the future behavior of a plant over a finite time horizon. At each sampling instant, an open-loop optimal control sequence is computed by minimizing a cost function (subject to constraints on inputs, outputs, and states), and only the *first* control move of this sequence is actually applied to the plant. This procedure is repeated at the next sampling instant using updated measurements, giving rise to the **Receding Horizon Strategy**.

The three fundamental pillars of every MPC formulation are:

1. **Prediction Model** — a linear or non-linear model (step response, transfer function, or state-space) that forecasts future outputs.
2. **Cost Function (Objective)** — typically a quadratic function penalizing tracking error and control effort.
3. **Receding Horizon Implementation** — only the first control action is implemented, and the horizon slides forward in time.

The two KTU-relevant industrial variants of MPC are **Dynamic Matrix Control (DMC)** — based on the step-response model — and **Model Algorithmic Control (MAC)** — based on the impulse-response model. Cutler and Ramaker (1979) and Richalet et al. (1978) are credited as the industrial founders.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Driving Analogy — "Driving with a Co-Pilot Who Sees the Road Ahead"**

Imagine you are driving a car on a winding mountain road. You are not just reacting to where the car *is* — you are *constantly* looking **30 meters ahead** and planning your steering, accelerator, and brake inputs to:
- Stay in your lane (track the reference trajectory).
- Not exceed the speed limit (respect input constraints).
- Not skid off the cliff on sharp turns (respect output/safety constraints).

You compute this plan **every second** based on your current position. Whatever steering angle you decide *for the very next second* is what you actually apply, and then you **re-plan** with the new road conditions. This is precisely what MPC does for a chemical process!

### 1.3 Key Performance Terminology
