| Term | Symbol | Typical Range (KTU) | Physical Meaning |
|------|--------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Prediction Horizon** | $P$ | 10 – 50 samples | How far into the future the controller "looks" |
| **Control Horizon** | $M$ | 2 – 10 samples | How many future moves the controller is allowed to "plan" |
| **Sampling Time** | $T_s$ | 1 s – 5 min | Time between consecutive controller updates |
| **Move Suppression Factor** | $\lambda$ | 0.1 – 10 | Weight penalizing aggressive control moves |
| **Output Tracking Weight** | $Q$ | Identity matrix | Weight penalizing deviation from setpoint |
| **Constraint Limits** | $u_{min}, u_{max}$ | Process-specific | Actuator saturation bounds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of MPC:** $\boxed{M \le P}$. The control horizon can never exceed the prediction horizon, otherwise future outputs cannot be properly predicted.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Operational Phases of MPC

MPC operates in a strict cyclical sequence at every sampling instant $k$:

**Phase 1 — Measurement Acquisition**
- Read current process output $y(k)$ and estimate any unmeasured disturbances $d(k)$.

**Phase 2 — Future Prediction**
- Using the internal model, predict the free response $\hat{y}_{free}(k+j \vert k)$ assuming no further control changes.
- Superimpose the *forced response* due to the proposed future moves $\Delta u(k), \Delta u(k+1), \dots, \Delta u(k+M-1)$.
- Build the predicted trajectory over horizon $P$.

**Phase 3 — Optimization & Move Application**
- Minimize the cost function $J$ analytically/numerically over the next $M$ moves.
- Apply only $\Delta u(k)$ to the plant.
- Discard the rest of the optimized sequence (it is re-computed next instant).

### 2.2 Why MPC Dominates Modern Process Control

> [!NOTE]
> - **Multivariable by Design** — handles MIMO systems naturally.
> - **Constraint Handling** — constraints are part of the optimization, not patched on afterwards.
> - **Anticipatory Action** — it "sees" future setpoint changes and acts *before* the error develops.
> - **Tuning Intuition** — $P$, $M$, and $\lambda$ have direct engineering meaning.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form 80% of the numerical questions.**

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Meaning |
|---|----------|------------|---------|
| 1 | Free Response | $\hat{y}_{free}(k+j \vert k) = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} S_i \, \Delta u(k+j-i)$ | Output if no new moves are made |
| 2 | Forced Response | $\hat{y}_{forced}(k+j \vert k) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} S_{j-i+1} \, \Delta u(k+i-1)$ | Output due to future moves |
| 3 | Total Prediction | $\hat{y}(k+j \vert k) = \hat{y}_{free} + \hat{y}_{forced}$ | Sum of free and forced |
| 4 | Step Response Coeff. | $S_i = y_{step}(iT) - y_{step}((i-1)T)$ | Coefficients of DMC model |
| 5 | Cost Function | $J = \sum_{j=1}^{P} Q_j [r(k+j) - \hat{y}(k+j \vert k)]^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{M} \lambda_i [\Delta u(k+i-1)]^2$ | Quadratic objective |
| 6 | Control Law | $\Delta u(k) = K_{mpc} \, [r - \hat{y}_{free}]$ | Linear unconstrained MPC law |
| 7 | Receding Update | $u(k+1) = u(k) + \Delta u(k)$ | Apply first move, slide horizon |
| 8 | Settling Bound | $S_{co} = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} S_i$ | Steady-state step gain (for model validity) |
| 9 | Constraint Form | $u_{min} \le u(k) \le u_{max}$ | Input box constraints |
| 10 | Rate Constraint | $\vert \Delta u(k) \vert \le \Delta u_{max}$ | Slew-rate constraint on $\Delta u$ |

**Notation Conventions Used in KTU Papers:**
- $S_i$ — step response coefficient at the $i$-th sampling instant.
- $r(k+j)$ — reference/trajectory at future instant $k+j$.
- $\hat{y}(k+j \vert k)$ — predicted output at time $k+j$ computed at time $k$.
- $K_{mpc}$ — unconstrained MPC gain matrix (a $M \times P$ matrix).
- $Q, \lambda$ — positive semi-definite weighting matrices.

### 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

MPC is the *de facto* standard in **refineries, petrochemical plants, and pulp & paper mills** worldwide. Companies like **Honeywell (Profit Controller)**, **AspenTech (DMCplus)**, and **Shell (SMOC)** have deployed MPC on thousands of units globally. Its key value lies in:
- Tightening operating windows → more profit.
- Preventing constraint violations → fewer trips and shutdowns.
- Coordinating multiple interacting loops → fewer operator interventions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the DMC Prediction Equation

**Starting Point:** The unit step response of a stable SISO plant is sampled at intervals $T$ to yield the sequence $\{S_1, S_2, S_3, \dots, S_N\}$ where $N$ is the *model horizon* (or *settling time* in samples). After $N$ samples, the response is assumed to have reached steady state, so $S_i = S_N$ for $i > N$.

For a sequence of past input moves $\Delta u(k-1), \Delta u(k-2), \dots$, the output at time $k$ is given by the **superposition principle** (valid for linear systems):

$$y(k) = y_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} S_i \, \Delta u(k-i)$$

where $y_0$ is the initial steady-state output before any moves were applied.

**Step 1 — Write the predicted output at future instant $k+j$:**

$$\hat{y}(k+j \vert k) = y_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} S_i \, \Delta u(k+j-i)$$

**Step 2 — Split the sum into three physically meaningful parts:**

*Part A — Past moves (already happened, fully known):*

$$\hat{y}_{past}(k+j \vert k) = \sum_{i=j+1}^{\infty} S_i \, \Delta u(k+j-i)$$

*Part B — Current and future moves within the control horizon $M$:*

$$\hat{y}_{future}(k+j \vert k) = \sum_{i=\max(1, j-M+1)}^{j} S_{j-i+1} \, \Delta u(k+i-1)$$

*Part C — Assume no moves after $M$ (i.e., $\Delta u(k+M) = \Delta u(k+M+1) = \dots = 0$):*

$$\hat{y}_{after}(k+j \vert k) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Define the *free response* (no future moves) and *forced response* (due to future moves):**

$$\hat{y}_{free}(k+j \vert k) = y(k) + \sum_{i=1}^{j-1} S_{j-i} \, \Delta u(k-i) \quad \text{(measured output + effect of past moves)}$$

$$\hat{y}_{forced}(k+j \vert k) = \sum_{i=1}^{\min(M, j)} S_{j-i+1} \, \Delta u(k+i-1)$$

**Step 4 — Final Prediction Equation:**

$$\boxed{\hat{y}(k+j \vert k) = \hat{y}_{free}(k+j \vert k) + \sum_{i=1}^{\min(M, j)} S_{j-i+1} \, \Delta u(k+i-1)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Unconstrained Control Law (Linear Case)

**Step 1 — Write the cost function in vector-matrix form.** Define the prediction vector $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$, reference vector $\mathbf{r}$, free-response vector $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free}$, and move vector $\Delta \mathbf{u}$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free} + \mathbf{S}_M \, \Delta \mathbf{u}$$

where $\mathbf{S}_M$ is the $(P \times M)$ **dynamic matrix** of step response coefficients, and:

$$J = (\mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}})^T Q (\mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}) + \Delta \mathbf{u}^T \lambda \, \Delta \mathbf{u}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the prediction into $J$:**

$$J = (\mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free} - \mathbf{S}_M \Delta \mathbf{u})^T Q (\mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free} - \mathbf{S}_M \Delta \mathbf{u}) + \Delta \mathbf{u}^T \lambda \, \Delta \mathbf{u}$$

**Step 3 — Take the gradient with respect to $\Delta \mathbf{u}$ and set to zero** (first-order optimality condition):

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \Delta \mathbf{u}} = -2 \mathbf{S}_M^T Q (\mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free} - \mathbf{S}_M \Delta \mathbf{u}) + 2 \lambda \Delta \mathbf{u} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the linear system:**

$$\mathbf{S}_M^T Q (\mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free}) = (\mathbf{S}_M^T Q \mathbf{S}_M + \lambda) \Delta \mathbf{u}$$

**Step 5 — Invert the Hessian matrix:**

$$\Delta \mathbf{u} = (\mathbf{S}_M^T Q \mathbf{S}_M + \lambda)^{-1} \mathbf{S}_M^T Q (\mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free})$$

**Step 6 — Extract the first move.** Only the *first* element of $\Delta \mathbf{u}$ is applied; the rest are discarded. The first row of the inverse matrix defines the MPC gain:

$$\boxed{\Delta u(k) = \underbrace{[1, 0, \dots, 0] (\mathbf{S}_M^T Q \mathbf{S}_M + \lambda)^{-1} \mathbf{S}_M^T Q}_{\mathbf{K}_{mpc}} \, (\mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free})}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A first-order plus dead-time plant has the unit step response samples shown below. A DMC controller uses $P=3$ and $M=1$. The setpoint is $r = [10, 10, 10]^T$. Past output is $y(k) = 4$ and there have been no past moves. Compute the optimal $\Delta u(k)$ if $\lambda = 0$.

**Given Step Response Coefficients:**

$$S_1 = 2, \quad S_2 = 3.5, \quad S_3 = 4.5$$

**Step 1 — Compute the free response (no future moves).** Since no past moves exist:

$$\hat{y}_{free}(k+1 \vert k) = y(k) = 4$$
$$\hat{y}_{free}(k+2 \vert k) = y(k) + S_1 \Delta u(k-1) = 4 + 0 = 4$$
$$\hat{y}_{free}(k+3 \vert k) = y(k) + S_1 \Delta u(k-1) + S_2 \Delta u(k-2) = 4 + 0 + 0 = 4$$

Wait — we need past moves for the free response. Since $\Delta u(k-1) = \Delta u(k-2) = 0$, the free response is **constant** at 4 for all three future instants.

**Step 2 — Build the dynamic matrix $\mathbf{S}_M$ (size $P \times M = 3 \times 1$):**

For a single move at $k$ affecting predictions at $k+1, k+2, k+3$:

$$\mathbf{S}_M = \begin{bmatrix} S_1 \\ S_2 \\ S_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3.5 \\ 4.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Form the error vector $(\mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free})$:**

$$\mathbf{e} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 - 4 \\ 10 - 4 \\ 10 - 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 6 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the unconstrained control law with $Q = I$ and $\lambda = 0$:**

$$\Delta u(k) = \frac{\mathbf{S}_M^T \mathbf{e}}{\mathbf{S}_M^T \mathbf{S}_M} = \frac{2(6) + 3.5(6) + 4.5(6)}{2^2 + 3.5^2 + 4.5^2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute numerator and denominator separately:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 6 \times (2 + 3.5 + 4.5) = 6 \times 10 = 60$$
$$\text{Denominator} = 4 + 12.25 + 20.25 = 36.5$$

**Step 6 — Final answer:**

$$\Delta u(k) = \frac{60}{36.5} \approx 1.644$$

This single move is applied to the plant. The next sample is taken, the horizon slides forward, and the procedure repeats.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Numerical Insight:** A larger $\lambda$ (move suppression) would reduce $\Delta u(k)$ at the cost of slower setpoint tracking. This is the classic **performance vs. aggressiveness trade-off** that KTU examiners love to test.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Receding Horizon Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the closed-loop architecture of an MPC controller, showing the interaction between the process, the internal model, the optimizer, and the receding horizon logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    refNode["Reference r k plus j"] --> errComp
    measNode["Measured Output y k"] --> modEst
    modEst["Internal Process Model"] --> predBlock
    errComp["Error Computation r minus y hat free"] --> optBlock
    predBlock["Future Prediction over Horizon P"] --> optBlock
    optBlock["Quadratic Optimizer min J subject to constraints"] --> moveSel
    moveSel["Select First Move delta u k"] --> actuatorNode
    actuatorNode["Actuator with Limits u min u max"] --> plantNode
    plantNode["Real Plant with Disturbance d k"] --> measNode
    plantNode --> measNode
    optBlock --> constCheck
    constCheck["Constraint Validator delta u max bounds"] --> optBlock
    moveSel --> horizonShift
    horizonShift["Horizon Shifts Receding Horizon k becomes k plus 1"] --> predBlock
```

### 4.2 State-Space / Functional Architecture Flow

This second diagram provides a more functional view of how the optimizer interacts with the plant, the estimator, and the constraint-handling module.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Current State x k"] --> predA
    inputB["Reference Trajectory r"] --> objFn
    predA["Predictor uses State Space Model"] --> predOut
    predOut["Predicted Output y hat k plus j"] --> objFn
    objFn["Objective Function J Quadratic"] --> solver
    solver["Q P Solver Active Set or QP"] --> move
    move["First Control Move u k"] --> plantA
    plantA["Plant Dynamics"] --> sensorA
    sensorA["Sensor with Noise"] --> estNode
    estNode["State Estimator Kalman Filter"] --> inputA
    plantA --> distNode
    distNode["Measured plus Unmeasured Disturbance d k"] --> estNode
    solver --> conA
    conA["Constraint Set C u in U y in Y"] --> solver
```

### 4.3 Prediction Horizon Visualisation

This block-flow diagram clarifies the temporal structure of the receding horizon — showing how the prediction window slides forward at every sampling instant.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pastZone["Past Zone Known Inputs and Outputs"] --> currentInst
    currentInst["Current Instant k Measurement Taken"] --> predZone
    predZone["Prediction Horizon P Future Outputs y hat k plus 1 to y hat k plus P"]
    predZone --> controlZone
    controlZone["Control Horizon M Planned Moves delta u k to delta u k plus M minus 1"]
    controlZone --> applyMove
    applyMove["Apply Only First Move u k to Plant"] --> nextStep
    nextStep["Next Sampling Instant k plus 1 Horizon Slides Forward"] --> pastZone
    controlZone --> zeroZone
    zeroZone["Assumed Zero Moves After M"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Model Predictive Control. List its three essential components.*

> **Model Answer:**
> **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** is an advanced control strategy that uses an explicit dynamic model of the process to predict future outputs and compute optimal control moves by minimizing a cost function over a finite prediction horizon, with only the first move applied before the horizon recedes.
>
> **Three Essential Components:**
> 1. **Prediction Model** — mathematical model used to forecast future outputs over horizon $P$.
> 2. **Cost Function / Objective** — typically quadratic in tracking error and control effort, minimized subject to constraints.
> 3. **Receding Horizon Strategy** — apply only the first control move and slide the horizon forward at every sampling instant.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for naming components + 1 for brief explanation of receding horizon.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of the prediction horizon $P$ and control horizon $M$ in MPC? Why must $M \le P$?*

> **Model Answer:**
>
> - **Prediction Horizon $P$** determines how many future sampling instants the controller *looks ahead* to evaluate predicted errors. A larger $P$ improves performance but increases computational load.
> - **Control Horizon $M$** is the number of *future independent moves* the optimizer is allowed to compute. A smaller $M$ regularizes the controller and reduces computation.
> - **Why $M \le P$?** If $M > P$, then control moves beyond the prediction horizon would have *no effect* on any predicted output, making them mathematically unobservable in the cost function. Hence $M \le P$ is a structural requirement for a well-posed optimization.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 mark each for the three parts.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, Choice 1]**

**(a)** Derive the unconstrained DMC control law starting from the step-response prediction model. Clearly define the dynamic matrix $\mathbf{S}_M$ and show how the move suppression factor $\lambda$ enters the expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A second-order process has the following step response coefficients obtained by sampling a unit step test at $T_s = 1$ min: $S_1 = 0.5$, $S_2 = 1.2$, $S_3 = 1.7$, $S_4 = 1.95$, $S_5 = 2.0$. Design a DMC controller with $P = 3$ and $M = 1$. The current output is $y(k) = 0.8$ and the setpoint vector is $\mathbf{r} = [2, 2, 2]^T$. Past moves are all zero. Take $\lambda = 0$. Compute the optimal first move $\Delta u(k)$. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer to (a):**
>
> **Step 1 — Prediction Equation.** From Section 3.1 above, the predicted output is:
> $$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free} + \mathbf{S}_M \Delta \mathbf{u}$$
> where $\mathbf{S}_M$ is a $P \times M$ lower-triangular Toeplitz matrix of step response coefficients:
> $$\mathbf{S}_M = \begin{bmatrix} S_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ S_2 & S_1 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ S_P & S_{P-1} & \cdots & S_{P-M+1} \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Cost Function.** In matrix form (with $Q$ as a $P \times P$ diagonal matrix and $\lambda$ as a $M \times M$ diagonal matrix):
> $$J = (\mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free} - \mathbf{S}_M \Delta \mathbf{u})^T Q (\mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free} - \mathbf{S}_M \Delta \mathbf{u}) + \Delta \mathbf{u}^T \lambda \Delta \mathbf{u}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Optimality Condition.** Differentiating with respect to $\Delta \mathbf{u}$ and equating to zero:
> $$-2 \mathbf{S}_M^T Q (\mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free} - \mathbf{S}_M \Delta \mathbf{u}) + 2 \lambda \Delta \mathbf{u} = 0$$
>
> **Step 4 — Rearranging:**
> $$(\mathbf{S}_M^T Q \mathbf{S}_M + \lambda) \Delta \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{S}_M^T Q (\mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free})$$
>
> **Step 5 — Inverting:**
> $$\Delta \mathbf{u} = (\mathbf{S}_M^T Q \mathbf{S}_M + \lambda)^{-1} \mathbf{S}_M^T Q (\mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free})$$
>
> **Step 6 — Role of $\lambda$:** The move suppression factor $\lambda$ adds a positive-definite term to the Hessian $\mathbf{S}_M^T Q \mathbf{S}_M$, ensuring invertibility, regularizing the solution, and penalizing aggressive moves. As $\lambda \to 0$, the controller becomes more aggressive; as $\lambda \to \infty$, $\Delta \mathbf{u} \to 0$.
>
> **Step 7 — Final Control Law:** Extracting the first element of $\Delta \mathbf{u}$:
> $$\boxed{\Delta u(k) = \mathbf{K}_{mpc} (\mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free}) \quad \text{where} \quad \mathbf{K}_{mpc} = [1, 0, \dots, 0] (\mathbf{S}_M^T Q \mathbf{S}_M + \lambda)^{-1} \mathbf{S}_M^T Q}$$

> **Model Answer to (b):**
>
> **Step 1 — Identify Step Response Coefficients Used.** With $P=3$ and $M=1$, we need $S_1, S_2, S_3$:
> $$S_1 = 0.5, \quad S_2 = 1.2, \quad S_3 = 1.7$$
>
> **Step 2 — Construct the Dynamic Matrix.** Since $M=1$, it is a $3 \times 1$ column vector:
> $$\mathbf{S}_M = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 1.2 \\ 1.7 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Free Response.** With all past moves equal to zero and $y(k) = 0.8$:
> $$\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 \\ 0.8 \\ 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Error Vector.**
> $$\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 - 0.8 \\ 2 - 0.8 \\ 2 - 0.8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.2 \\ 1.2 \\ 1.2 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 5 — Numerator of the Control Law.**
> $$\mathbf{S}_M^T \mathbf{e} = (0.5)(1.2) + (1.2)(1.2) + (1.7)(1.2) = 1.2 \times (0.5 + 1.2 + 1.7) = 1.2 \times 3.4 = 4.08$$
>
> **Step 6 — Denominator of the Control Law.**
> $$\mathbf{S}_M^T \mathbf{S}_M = (0.5)^2 + (1.2)^2 + (1.7)^2 = 0.25 + 1.44 + 2.89 = 4.58$$
>
> **Step 7 — Compute the Optimal Move (with $\lambda = 0$).**
> $$\Delta u(k) = \frac{4.08}{4.58} \approx 0.891$$
>
> **Step 8 — Apply to Plant.** The control signal to be applied is:
> $$u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u(k) = u(k-1) + 0.891$$
>
> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{\Delta u(k) \approx 0.891}$

---

**Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Choice 2]**

**(a)** Explain the *Receding Horizon Strategy* in MPC with a clear diagram. Why is only the first move applied, and what happens to the rest of the optimized sequence? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare DMC and MAC. For the same plant with the unit step response $S_1 = 1, S_2 = 2, S_3 = 3, S_4 = 3.5, S_5 = 3.8$ and $S_i = S_5$ for $i \ge 5$, compute the *impulse response coefficients* $h_i$ such that MAC can be implemented. With $P = 3$, $M = 1$, $y(k) = 2$, $\mathbf{r} = [4, 4, 4]^T$, and $\lambda = 0$, compute $\Delta u(k)$. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer to (a):**
>
> **Step 1 — Concept.** In the Receding Horizon Strategy, at each sampling instant $k$, the MPC solves an open-loop optimal control problem over a *finite* future horizon of length $P$, yielding a sequence of $M$ moves: $\Delta u(k), \Delta u(k+1), \dots, \Delta u(k+M-1)$.
>
> **Step 2 — Application of Only the First Move.** After the optimization, the controller applies **only the first move** $\Delta u(k)$ to the actual plant. The remaining moves $u(k+1), \dots, u(k+M-1)$ are *discarded*.
>
> **Step 3 — Why Discard the Rest?** Three reasons:
> - The model is *imperfect*; future errors will accumulate and invalidate the predictions.
> - Disturbances may hit the plant at any time, invalidating the open-loop plan.
> - The optimizer re-solves the problem at the *next* instant $k+1$ with fresh measurements, producing a *new* better plan.
>
> **Step 4 — Horizon Sliding.** The prediction window shifts from $[k+1, k+P]$ to $[k+2, k+P+1]$. This "receding" motion gives the strategy its name.
>
> **Step 5 — Closed-Loop Effect.** Although the optimizer solves an *open-loop* problem, the *re-solving* at every instant creates a *closed-loop* controller with feedback properties.
>
> **Step 6 — Diagram (Textual).**
>
> ```
> Time:  k-2   k-1   k   k+1  k+2  k+3  k+4  k+5
>                  │═════════════════════════│  ← Horizon at instant k (P=5)
>                      │═════════════════════════│  ← Horizon at instant k+1 (slid)
>                  [Apply Δu(k)]  [Discard rest, recompute]
> ```
>
> **Step 7 — Engineering Insight.** This is what distinguishes MPC from open-loop optimal control — the closed-loop feedback re-injection at every sample.

> **Model Answer to (b):**
>
> **Step 1 — DMC vs. MAC Comparison Table.**
>
> | Aspect | DMC (Dynamic Matrix Control) | MAC (Model Algorithmic Control) |
> |--------|------------------------------|----------------------------------|
> | Internal Model | Step response coefficients $S_i$ | Impulse response coefficients $h_i$ |
> | Manipulated Variable | Moves $\Delta u(k)$ (incremental) | Absolute input $u(k)$ |
> | Industrial Origin | Cutler & Ramaker, Shell (1979) | Richalet et al., ADERSA (1978) |
> | Numerical Conditioning | Generally better | Can be ill-conditioned for slow plants |
>
> **Step 2 — Convert Step Response to Impulse Response.** The relationship is:
> $$h_i = S_i - S_{i-1}, \quad S_0 = 0$$
> Therefore:
> $$h_1 = S_1 - S_0 = 1 - 0 = 1$$
> $$h_2 = S_2 - S_1 = 2 - 1 = 1$$
> $$h_3 = S_3 - S_2 = 3 - 2 = 1$$
> $$h_4 = S_4 - S_3 = 3.5 - 3 = 0.5$$
> $$h_5 = S_5 - S_4 = 3.8 - 3.5 = 0.3$$
>
> **Step 3 — Construct the MAC Dynamic Matrix.** With $P=3$ and $M=1$, only the first column of the $P \times M$ dynamic matrix is needed, but for impulse response, the relationship between future output and current input is:
> $$\hat{y}(k+j \vert k) = \sum_{i=0}^{j-1} h_{i+1} u(k-i) + h_j \Delta u(k)$$
>
> For simplicity in this scalar-input case, the equivalent "step-response style" gain for MAC with $M=1$ and current move affecting predictions is captured by the cumulative step coefficients, but for direct MAC we use the impulse coefficients $h_1, h_2, h_3$ as the forcing gains:
> $$\mathbf{S}_{MAC} = \begin{bmatrix} h_1 \\ h_2 \\ h_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Free Response.** With $u(k-1) = 0$ and $y(k) = 2$:
> $$\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 5 — Error Vector.**
> $$\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 6 — Numerator.**
> $$\mathbf{S}_{MAC}^T \mathbf{e} = (1)(2) + (1)(2) + (1)(2) = 6$$
>
> **Step 7 — Denominator.**
> $$\mathbf{S}_{MAC}^T \mathbf{S}_{MAC} = 1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2 = 3$$
>
> **Step 8 — Optimal Move.**
> $$\Delta u(k) = \frac{6}{3} = 2.0$$
>
> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{\Delta u(k) = 2.0}$
>
> **Engineering Note:** In this simplified case, MAC and DMC give the same $\Delta u(k)$ because the impulse response $h_i = 1$ (constant) corresponds to an integrator-like response — yet the *interpretation* of the move differs (DMC adds to $u$, MAC directly applies $u$).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to slide the horizon** — Many students compute the entire $M$-move sequence and apply it. You will lose **3 marks** for this. Apply only $\Delta u(k)$.
> 2. **Confusing step response $S_i$ with impulse response $h_i$** — They are related by $h_i = S_i - S_{i-1}$, not equal. Examiners explicitly test this conversion.
> 3. **Neglecting the free response** — A common error is to assume $\hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free} = 0$ even when past moves exist. This breaks the prediction entirely.
> 4. **Writing $P = M$ always** — They are independent. Industrial practice often uses $M \ll P$.
> 5. **Dropping the $\lambda$ term in the Hessian** — When $\lambda > 0$ is given, omitting it from $(\mathbf{S}_M^T Q \mathbf{S}_M + \lambda)$ will lose **2–3 marks**.
> 6. **Forgetting the units** — Step response coefficients carry the units of the output per unit input change. Quote them when computing.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition** — MPC = Model-based + Prediction + Optimization + Receding Horizon.
- **Three Pillars** — Prediction Model, Cost Function $J$, Receding Horizon.
- **Key Variables** — $P$ (prediction horizon), $M$ (control horizon), $\lambda$ (move suppression), $Q$ (output weight).
- **Hard Constraint** — $M \le P$ (no exceptions).
- **Core Equation** — $\Delta u(k) = \mathbf{K}_{mpc}(\mathbf{r} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}_{free})$.
- **DMC vs. MAC** — DMC uses step response $S_i$; MAC uses impulse response $h_i = S_i - S_{i-1}$.
- **Prediction Equation** — $\hat{y} = \hat{y}_{free} + \hat{y}_{forced}$ (split the two contributions).
- **Hessian Form** — $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{S}_M^T Q \mathbf{S}_M + \lambda$ (must be positive definite).
- **Industrial Variants** — DMC (Shell), MAC (ADERSA), QDMC, IDCOM, HIECON.
- **Tuning Strategy** — Increase $P$ for smoother control; increase $M$ for faster but more aggressive control; increase $\lambda$ to slow down moves.
- **Constraint Categories** — Input bounds $u_{min} \le u \le u_{max}$, rate bounds $\vert \Delta u \vert \le \Delta u_{max}$, output bounds $y_{min} \le y \le y_{max}$.
- **Why MPC Wins** — Handles MIMO, constraints, and anticipates future setpoint changes inherently.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adaptive and Self-Tuning Controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET502 - PROCESS DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adaptive and Self-Tuning Controller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — ADAPTIVE AND SELF-TUNING CONTROLLER

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adaptive Controller:** A controller whose parameters are **automatically and continuously adjusted** in real-time to compensate for variations in process dynamics, environmental disturbances, or operating conditions, in order to maintain a desired closed-loop performance without manual re-tuning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-Tuning Controller (STC):** A specific class of adaptive controller that performs **on-line parameter identification** of the process model and uses the estimated parameters to **automatically re-design** the controller (e.g., PID, pole-placement, or minimum-variance) at every sampling instant.

The two terms are often used interchangeably in industry, but technically:
- **Adaptive Control** is the broader philosophy — the controller *adapts* to changing process behaviour.
- **Self-Tuning Control** is the most common *implementation* of adaptive control, based on the *identification → design → implementation* cycle.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Thermostat in a Mountain Cabin"

Imagine you live in a mountain cabin where the **outside temperature changes drastically** between day and night, and the **insulation properties** of the cabin vary as wood expands and contracts with humidity. A normal (fixed-gain) thermostat would either over-shoot in the morning or fail to reach the setpoint at night.

Now, an **adaptive controller** is like a *smart thermostat* that:
1. **Senses** how fast the room heats or cools (parameter estimation).
2. **Compares** this to a built-in "ideal response model" (reference model).
3. **Adjusts** its internal gain (heater power cycle) so the room temperature *always* follows the desired curve.

You never have to manually turn a knob — the controller **learns and re-tunes itself** continuously. This is the essence of adaptive control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Trigger for Adaptive Control:** Adaptive techniques are deployed when the process exhibits one or more of:
> - **Time-varying parameters** (catalyst deactivation, heat-exchanger fouling).
> - **Nonlinear dynamics** that are *linearisable* over the operating range.
> - **Large modelling uncertainty** at the design stage.
> - **Widely changing operating points** (e.g., aerospace, robotics, pH control).

### 1.3 Classification of Adaptive Controllers

Adaptive controllers broadly fall into two families:

| Family | Core Idea | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Gain Scheduling** | Pre-computed controller gains as a function of operating point | Aircraft flight control, HVAC |
| **Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)** | Drives process to follow a *reference model* | Robotics, servo systems |
| **Self-Tuning Regulator (STR)** | On-line identification + on-line controller design | Chemical processes, paper machines |

### 1.4 Why Conventional Fixed-Gain PID Fails

A standard PID controller:

$$u(t) = K_c\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt}\right]$$

has the limitation that $K_c, \tau_I, \tau_D$ are **constants**. When the process gain $K_p$ doubles, the closed-loop response becomes oscillatory, and the same $K_c$ is no longer optimal. Adaptive control solves this by **re-tuning $K_c, \tau_I, \tau_D$ on the fly**.

### 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics in Adaptive Control

- **Integral Square Error (ISE):** $\int_0^\infty e^2(t)dt$
- **Integral Absolute Error (IAE):** $\int_0^\infty \vert e(t)\vert dt$
- **Settling time (within 2% of setpoint)**
- **Maximum overshoot**
- **Adaptation gain $\gamma$** — controls speed of parameter update.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gain Scheduling — The Simplest Adaptive Strategy

Gain Scheduling is **not a feedback adaptive scheme** in the strict sense; it is an *open-loop compensation* based on a measured auxiliary variable $\rho$ (e.g., flow rate, temperature, speed).

### Operating Logic:
1. At the design stage, the process is linearised at **several operating points**.
2. A controller is tuned at each point (giving sets of $\{(K_c^{(i)}, \tau_I^{(i)}, \tau_D^{(i)})\}$).
3. During operation, the auxiliary variable $\rho$ is measured, and the controller gains are **interpolated** from a look-up table.

$$K_c(\rho) = f(\rho), \quad \tau_I(\rho) = g(\rho)$$

### Pros and Cons:
- ✅ **Fast response** — no learning time.
- ✅ Simple to implement.
- ❌ Requires a **good understanding** of the process to choose $\rho$.
- ❌ Cannot adapt to *unmeasured* disturbances or unmodelled dynamics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Gain scheduling is *not* a true closed-loop adaptive controller because it does not use the output error to update gains. KTU questions often ask to "distinguish gain scheduling from MRAC" — this is the key discriminator.

## 2.2 Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)

### 2.2.1 Architecture

The MRAC system has four parts:
1. **Process** with transfer function $G_p(s)$.
2. **Reference Model** $G_m(s)$ — embodies the *desired* closed-loop behaviour.
3. **Controller** with adjustable parameters $\theta$.
4. **Adaptation Mechanism** — adjusts $\theta$ to minimise the error $e_m = y_p - y_m$.

### 2.2.2 The MIT Rule (Classical MRAC)

The MIT rule is the **original** parameter update law developed at MIT in the 1960s. Define:

$$e_m(t) = y_p(t) - y_m(t)$$

The loss function to be minimised is:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2} e_m^2(t)$$

Gradient descent gives the update law:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = -\gamma \, e_m \frac{\partial e_m}{\partial \theta}$$

The **sensitivity derivative** $\partial e_m / \partial \theta$ is called the *sensitivity function* of the error with respect to the parameter. For example, if $\theta = K_c$ and the process is first-order:

$$\frac{\partial e_m}{\partial K_c} = \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial K_c} \approx \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial u} \cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial K_c}$$

This is approximated using the reference model as a surrogate for the true process.

### 2.2.3 Normalised MIT Rule

The unnormalised MIT rule can become unstable if $e_m$ is large. The **normalised** version divides by the square of a normalisation signal $N(t)$:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \frac{e_m \frac{\partial e_m}{\partial \theta}}{\alpha + \left(\frac{\partial e_m}{\partial \theta}\right)^2}$$

with $\alpha > 0$ preventing division by zero.

## 2.3 Self-Tuning Regulator (STR) — The Most Industrial Variant

The STR performs three tasks at every sampling instant $k$:

1. **Estimate** the parameters $\hat{\theta}$ of a discrete-time process model.
2. **Design** the controller based on $\hat{\theta}$.
3. **Implement** the new controller gains.

### 2.3.1 Discrete-Time Process Model (ARMAX / CARIMA)

The process is described by a **Controlled Auto-Regressive Moving Average with eXogenous input (ARMAX)** model:

$$A(z^{-1})y(k) = z^{-d}B(z^{-1})u(k) + C(z^{-1})\xi(k)$$

where
- $A(z^{-1}) = 1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \dots + a_{n_a} z^{-n_a}$
- $B(z^{-1}) = b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + \dots + b_{n_b} z^{-n_b}$
- $C(z^{-1}) = 1 + c_1 z^{-1} + \dots + c_{n_c} z^{-n_c}}$
- $\xi(k)$ is white noise, $d$ is the **time delay** in samples.

This is often re-cast in *regression form*:

$$y(k) = \phi^T(k)\,\theta(k) + \xi(k)$$

where
$$\phi^T(k) = [-y(k-1), -y(k-2), \dots, -y(k-n_a), u(k-d-1), \dots, u(k-d-1-n_b)]$$
$$\theta^T = [a_1, a_2, \dots, a_{n_a}, b_0, b_1, \dots, b_{n_b}]$$

### 2.3.2 Recursive Least Squares (RLS) — The Workhorse Identifier

The on-line estimate $\hat{\theta}(k)$ is updated using the **RLS algorithm**:

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)\,[y(k) - \phi^T(k)\hat{\theta}(k-1)]$$

$$K(k) = \frac{P(k-1)\phi(k)}{\lambda + \phi^T(k)P(k-1)\phi(k)}$$

$$P(k) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[P(k-1) - \frac{P(k-1)\phi(k)\phi^T(k)P(k-1)}{\lambda + \phi^T(k)P(k-1)\phi(k)}\right]$$

Here:
- $K(k)$ is the **Kalman gain vector** (n×1).
- $P(k)$ is the **covariance matrix** (n×n).
- $\lambda$ is the **forgetting factor**, typically $0.95 \le \lambda \le 0.99$. A value $< 1$ allows the algorithm to "forget" old data and track time-varying parameters.

> [!WARNING]
> If $\lambda$ is too small, $P(k)$ grows and the algorithm becomes sensitive to noise. If $\lambda = 1$, old data has equal weight, and parameter drift is uncompensated. KTU questions often test this trade-off.

### 2.3.3 Controller Design Options for STR

Once $\hat{\theta}$ is known, the controller can be designed by:
- **Minimum-variance control** (Åström, 1970) — minimises $E[y^2(k+d)]$.
- **Generalised minimum-variance** — adds a penalty on control effort.
- **Pole-placement self-tuning** — assigns closed-loop poles based on a user-specified polynomial.
- **PID auto-tuning** — converts the discrete model to a continuous equivalent and re-tunes $K_c, \tau_I, \tau_D$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Definition | Equation / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $e_m(t)$ | Model-reference error | $e_m = y_p - y_m$ |
| $J(\theta)$ | MIT loss function | $J = \tfrac{1}{2}e_m^2$ |
| MIT rule | Parameter update | $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e_m (\partial e_m / \partial \theta)$ |
| ARMAX | Discrete model | $A(z^{-1})y = z^{-d}B(z^{-1})u + C(z^{-1})\xi$ |
| Regression | Estimator form | $y(k) = \phi^T(k)\theta(k) + \xi(k)$ |
| RLS update | Parameter | $\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)\epsilon(k)$ |
| Kalman gain | RLS | $K(k) = P(k-1)\phi / (\lambda + \phi^T P \phi)$ |
| Covariance | RLS | $P(k) = \lambda^{-1}[P(k-1) - P\phi\phi^T P / (\lambda + \phi^T P \phi)]$ |
| Forgetting factor | Memory weight | $0.95 \le \lambda \le 0.99$ |
| Adaptation gain | MIT | $\gamma > 0$ |
| Settling time | 2% criterion | $t_s \approx 4 / \zeta \omega_n$ |

> [!NOTE]
> All absolute value bars above use the typeset rendering to remain table-safe. The KTU valuation key requires *both* the formula and the **physical meaning of each term** to earn full marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **pH control** in wastewater treatment — highly nonlinear titration curve.
- **Distillation column** control — gain changes with product composition.
- **Polymerisation reactors** — catalyst activity decays over weeks.
- **Aircraft autopilots** — aerodynamic coefficients depend on Mach number and altitude (gain-scheduled).
- **Robotic manipulators** — payload changes alter effective inertia (MRAC).
- **Paper machines** — basis weight control with STR.
- **Bioreactors** — biomass growth rate changes over fermentation cycles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The MIT Rule from First Principles

We begin with the loss function:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2} e_m^2(t)$$

Step 1 — Take the gradient with respect to $\theta$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = e_m(t) \frac{\partial e_m(t)}{\partial \theta}$$

Step 2 — Apply the **gradient descent** update:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta}$$

Step 3 — Substitute the gradient:

$$\boxed{\frac{d\theta}{dt} = -\gamma\, e_m(t)\,\frac{\partial e_m(t)}{\partial \theta}}$$

This is the **classical MIT rule**.

Step 4 — The sensitivity derivative $\partial e_m / \partial \theta$ is evaluated at each $t$ by treating $\theta$ as if it were constant (the "frozen parameter" assumption). For a first-order plant $G_p(s) = K_p/(\tau s + 1)$ with $K_p$ being the parameter, the sensitivity of the output is:

$$\frac{\partial y_p}{\partial K_p} = \frac{u(t)}{\tau s + 1}$$

which in the time domain is a convolution with the impulse response of the process.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sensitivity Derivative for a First-Order Plant

Consider the open-loop process:

$$\tau \frac{dy_p}{dt} + y_p = K_p\, u(t)$$

Step 1 — Differentiate both sides with respect to $K_p$:

$$\tau \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\frac{\partial y_p}{\partial K_p}\right) + \frac{\partial y_p}{\partial K_p} = u(t)$$

Step 2 — Recognise that this is *the same differential equation* as the original process, but driven by $u(t)$ instead of $K_p u(t)$. Hence the sensitivity is the **process output when the input is $u(t)$ divided by $K_p$**.

For the closed-loop MRAC system where the reference model is $G_m(s) = K_m/(\tau_m s + 1)$ and the controller is a single gain $K_c$, the sensitivity with respect to $K_c$ is:

$$\frac{\partial y_p}{\partial K_c} \approx \frac{G_m(s) \cdot G_p(s)}{1 + K_c G_p(s)} \cdot \frac{e_m}{K_c}$$

which simplifies the MIT update for the controller gain.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RLS Update Law (Matrix Form)

Start with the minimisation of the **cumulative least-squares criterion**:

$$V(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda^{k-i}\,[y(i) - \phi^T(i)\theta]^2$$

Step 1 — Differentiate $V$ with respect to $\theta$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda^{k-i}\phi(i)[y(i) - \phi^T(i)\theta] = 0$$

Step 2 — Define the weighted covariance matrix:

$$P^{-1}(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda^{k-i}\phi(i)\phi^T(i)$$

Step 3 — The optimal estimate is:

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = P(k)\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda^{k-i}\phi(i) y(i)$$

Step 4 — Separate the term at $i=k$:

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = P(k)\left[\lambda\sum_{i=1}^{k-1}\lambda^{k-1-i}\phi(i) y(i) + \phi(k) y(k)\right]$$

Step 5 — Recognise that $\hat{\theta}(k-1) = P(k-1)\sum_{i=1}^{k-1}\lambda^{k-1-i}\phi(i) y(i)$, hence:

$$\hat{\theta}(k) = P(k)\left[\lambda P^{-1}(k-1)\hat{\theta}(k-1) + \phi(k) y(k)\right]$$

Step 6 — Using the **matrix inversion lemma**:

$$P(k) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left[P(k-1) - \frac{P(k-1)\phi(k)\phi^T(k)P(k-1)}{\lambda + \phi^T(k)P(k-1)\phi(k)}\right]$$

Step 7 — Define the **a priori prediction error**:

$$\epsilon(k) = y(k) - \phi^T(k)\hat{\theta}(k-1)$$

Step 8 — Final compact RLS form:

$$\boxed{\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)\,\epsilon(k)}$$

with

$$K(k) = P(k-1)\phi(k)[\lambda + \phi^T(k)P(k-1)\phi(k)]^{-1}$$

This is the algorithm implemented in every commercial self-tuning controller.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Minimum-Variance Self-Tuning Law

For an ARMAX process:

$$A(z^{-1})y(k) = z^{-d}B(z^{-1})u(k) + C(z^{-1})\xi(k)$$

the d-step-ahead prediction is:

$$\hat{y}(k+d \vert k) = \frac{G(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})}y(k) + \frac{B(z^{-1})F(z^{-1})}{C(z^{-1})}u(k)$$

where $F$ and $G$ are obtained by the polynomial division:

$$C(z^{-1}) = A(z^{-1})F(z^{-1}) + z^{-d}G(z^{-1})$$

The **minimum-variance control law** sets the prediction to zero (assuming the setpoint is zero), giving:

$$u(k) = -\frac{G(z^{-1})}{B(z^{-1})F(z^{-1})}y(k)$$

This is implemented at every sample using the **latest $\hat{\theta}$** — hence the *self-tuning* nature.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Choices for a Self-Tuning Controller

| Design Aspect | MRAC | STR |
|---|---|---|
| Reference | Explicit reference model $G_m$ | Implicit, via pole-placement or MV cost |
| Estimation | Not always required | Always required (RLS) |
| Stability proof | Lyapounov-based | More complex (separation principle) |
| Computational load | Lower | Higher |
| Robustness | Sensitive to noise | More robust with $\lambda < 1$ and RLS |
| Industrial use | Aerospace, robotics | Chemical, petrochemical, pulp & paper |

> [!NOTE]
> The choice between MRAC and STR is not academic — it determines the **certification path** for safety-critical systems. KTU questions on "compare MRAC and STR" expect a tabular answer covering *at least*: estimation, stability, robustness, and complexity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Adaptive Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference rk] --> S[Summing Junction]
    S --> C[Adjustable Controller Ck]
    C --> U[Control Signal uk]
    U --> P[Process Gp]
    P --> Y[Process Output yk]
    Y --> F1[Feedback Path]
    F1 --> S
    Y --> A[Adaptation Mechanism]
    A --> C
```

This diagram shows the two essential features of any adaptive controller: the **forward control path** (R → S → C → P → Y) and the **adaptation feedback path** (Y → A → C).

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference rk] --> S1[Summing Junction 1]
    S1 --> CM[Reference Model Gm]
    CM --> YM[Model Output ym]
    R --> S2[Summing Junction 2]
    S2 --> CC[Adjustable Controller]
    CC --> U[Control uk]
    U --> PP[Process Gp]
    PP --> YP[Process Output yp]
    YP --> S2
    YP --> S3[Summing Junction 3]
    YM --> S3
    S3 --> EM[Error em = yp - ym]
    EM --> AM[Adaptation Mechanism MIT Rule]
    AM --> CC
```

The MRAC has **three loops**:
1. The conventional feedback loop (R → S2 → C → P → YP).
2. The model loop (R → S1 → Gm → YM).
3. The adaptation loop (EM → AM → C).

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Self-Tuning Regulator (STR) with RLS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference rk] --> CD[Controller Design Block]
    CD --> CK[Controller Gains theta]
    CK --> CO[Controller Output uk]
    CO --> PR[Process]
    PR --> YK[Output yk]
    YK --> UK1[uk-1, uk-2 etc]
    UK1 --> PHI[Regression Vector phi k]
    YK --> PHI
    PHI --> RLS[Recursive Least Squares]
    RLS --> TH[Estimated Parameters theta hat]
    TH --> CD
    NOI[Disturbance xi k] --> PR
```

The STR cycle is **estimate → design → implement → repeat**. Notice that unlike MRAC, there is no reference model — the *desired behaviour* is encoded in the **design algorithm** (e.g., pole-placement target polynomial).

## 4.4 State Diagram — STR Sampling Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ReadSensors
    ReadSensors --> Predict: form phi k
    Predict --> Estimate: RLS update theta hat
    Estimate --> Design: pole placement or MV
    Design --> Apply: compute uk
    Apply --> ReadSensors: wait sample time Ts
```

This state diagram represents the deterministic cycle executed by an industrial STR at every sampling instant $T_s$. A typical implementation in a DCS (Distributed Control System) uses a **periodic task** triggered by a real-time clock.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Gain Scheduling vs True Adaptive Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Gain Scheduling"
        OP1[Operating Point rho] --> TBL1[Look up table]
        TBL1 --> GC1[Fixed Gain Controller]
        GC1 --> P1[Process]
    end
    subgraph "True Adaptive (MRAC or STR)"
        OP2[Process Output yk] --> AD2[Adaptation Mechanism]
        AD2 --> GC2[Time varying Controller]
        GC2 --> P2[Process]
        P2 --> OP2
    end
```

The contrast is fundamental: **gain scheduling** uses an *external* operating variable, while **adaptive control** uses the *closed-loop output* to drive adaptation.

## 4.6 Summary Schematic — Decision Tree for Choosing Adaptive Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Process has time varying dynamics?]
    Q -- No --> F[Use Fixed Gain PID]
    Q -- Yes --> Q2[Operating point variable measurable?]
    Q2 -- Yes --> GS[Use Gain Scheduling]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3[Desired response model available?]
    Q3 -- Yes --> MR[Use MRAC]
    Q3 -- No --> ST[Use STR with RLS]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define an adaptive controller. List any two situations where adaptive control is preferred over a conventional fixed-gain PID controller.**

**Model Answer:**

An **adaptive controller** is a controller whose parameters are automatically adjusted on-line to maintain desired closed-loop performance when the process dynamics are time-varying or uncertain. (2 marks)

Adaptive control is preferred when:
1. The process gain or time constant changes significantly with operating conditions (e.g., pH neutralisation, aircraft dynamics).
2. The process exhibits long-term drift, e.g., catalyst deactivation in a chemical reactor or heat-exchanger fouling. (1 mark for both)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of the forgetting factor $\lambda$ in the Recursive Least Squares algorithm used in self-tuning controllers?**

**Model Answer:**

The forgetting factor $\lambda$, typically chosen in the range **$0.95 \le \lambda \le 0.99$**, gives exponentially less weight to older data samples. This allows the RLS algorithm to **track time-varying process parameters** by forgetting outdated information.

A value of $\lambda = 1$ corresponds to standard least squares (no forgetting), which is unable to track slow parameter drift. A value too close to 0 makes the algorithm sensitive to noise. (3 marks)

---

## 📝 PART B — Full-Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 scheme mandates an *internal choice* in Part B. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Self-Tuning Regulator with RLS

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Self-Tuning Regulator (STR)** based on the Recursive Least Squares (RLS) identification method. Discuss the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a first-order process described by the discrete-time ARX model:

$$y(k) = 0.8\,y(k-1) + 0.5\,u(k-1) + \xi(k)$$

where $\xi(k)$ is zero-mean white noise with variance $\sigma^2 = 0.01$. Implement **one full RLS update step** with $P(0) = 100I$, $\hat{\theta}(0) = [0, 0]^T$, $\lambda = 0.98$, and the current measurement $y(1) = 1.2$ given $y(0) = 1.0$ and $u(0) = 0.5$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The STR block diagram (refer Section 4.3) consists of:
1. **Process block** – the plant to be controlled.
2. **Controller block** – parameters $\theta_c$ updated each sample.
3. **Regression vector builder** – forms $\phi(k) = [-y(k-1), u(k-1)]^T$.
4. **RLS estimator** – produces $\hat{\theta}(k)$ on-line.
5. **Controller design block** – computes new $\theta_c$ from $\hat{\theta}(k)$.

At each sampling instant:
- Sensor measurements form $\phi(k)$ and the new $y(k)$ is read.
- RLS updates $\hat{\theta}(k)$ using the prediction error $\epsilon(k) = y(k) - \phi^T(k)\hat{\theta}(k-1)$.
- The design block solves for $\theta_c$ (e.g., via pole-placement or minimum-variance law).
- The new control $u(k)$ is applied and the cycle repeats. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Build the regression vector:**

$$\phi(1) = \begin{bmatrix} -y(0) \\ u(0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Kalman gain $K(1)$:**

$$P(0)\phi(1) = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 0 \\ 0 & 100 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} -1.0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -100 \\ 50 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\phi^T(1)P(0)\phi(1) = (-1.0)(-100) + (0.5)(50) = 100 + 25 = 125$$

$$K(1) = \frac{1}{0.98 + 125}\begin{bmatrix} -100 \\ 50 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{125.98}\begin{bmatrix} -100 \\ 50 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.7938 \\ 0.3969 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the prediction error:**

$$\hat{y}(1) = \phi^T(1)\hat{\theta}(0) = [-1.0, 0.5]\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

$$\epsilon(1) = y(1) - \hat{y}(1) = 1.2 - 0 = 1.2$$

**Step 4 — Update parameter estimate:**

$$\hat{\theta}(1) = \hat{\theta}(0) + K(1)\epsilon(1) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} -0.7938 \\ 0.3969 \end{bmatrix}(1.2)$$

$$\hat{\theta}(1) = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9526 \\ 0.4763 \end{bmatrix}$$

Since the true parameters are $[0.8, 0.5]^T$ (i.e., $a_1 = -0.8$, $b_0 = 0.5$), the estimates are still far from the true values — a large initial covariance $P(0) = 100I$ allows rapid learning, which will continue over subsequent samples.

**Step 5 — Update covariance matrix:**

$$P(1) = \frac{1}{0.98}\left[P(0) - K(1)\phi^T(1)P(0)\right]$$

$$K(1)\phi^T(1)P(0) = \begin{bmatrix} -0.7938 \\ 0.3969 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} -1.0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 100 & 0 \\ 0 & 100 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$= \begin{bmatrix} -0.7938 \\ 0.3969 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} -100 & 50 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 79.38 & -39.69 \\ -39.69 & 19.85 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$P(1) = \frac{1}{0.98}\left[\begin{bmatrix} 100 & 0 \\ 0 & 100 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 79.38 & -39.69 \\ -39.69 & 19.85 \end{bmatrix}\right]$$

$$P(1) = \frac{1}{0.98}\begin{bmatrix} 20.62 & 39.69 \\ 39.69 & 80.15 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 21.04 & 40.50 \\ 40.50 & 81.79 \end{bmatrix}$$

The reduced diagonal elements (21.04, 81.79) confirm that uncertainty has decreased after the first measurement. (7 marks)

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — MRAC with MIT Rule

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the **MIT rule** for parameter adaptation in a Model Reference Adaptive Control system. Discuss the role of the adaptation gain $\gamma$ and the normalised MIT rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A first-order plant has the transfer function $G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{5s + 1}$. The reference model is $G_m(s) = \frac{2}{3s + 1}$. A single-gain controller $K_c$ is adapted using the MIT rule. If the current model-reference error is $e_m = 0.4$, the sensitivity derivative $\partial e_m / \partial K_c = 0.6$, and the adaptation gain $\gamma = 0.5$, compute the change $\Delta K_c$ per sample assuming a sampling period of $T_s = 0.1$ s. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The MIT rule is derived as follows (refer Section 3.1):

Define the loss function $J = \tfrac{1}{2}e_m^2$.

Taking the gradient: $\partial J / \partial K_c = e_m (\partial e_m / \partial K_c)$.

Applying gradient descent: $\dot{K_c} = -\gamma e_m (\partial e_m / \partial K_c)$.

In discrete form: $\Delta K_c = -\gamma\, e_m (\partial e_m / \partial K_c)\, T_s$.

The **adaptation gain $\gamma$** controls the speed of adaptation:
- Small $\gamma$ → slow, stable convergence.
- Large $\gamma$ → fast but potentially unstable.

The **normalised MIT rule** divides by the squared sensitivity plus a small $\alpha > 0$:

$$\dot{K_c} = -\gamma \frac{e_m (\partial e_m / \partial K_c)}{\alpha + (\partial e_m / \partial K_c)^2}$$

This makes the adaptation speed independent of the magnitude of the sensitivity derivative, providing **robustness** to varying signal amplitudes. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Apply the discrete MIT update:**

$$\Delta K_c = -\gamma\, e_m\, \frac{\partial e_m}{\partial K_c}\, T_s$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\Delta K_c = -(0.5)(0.4)(0.6)(0.1) = -0.012$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** The negative sign means $K_c$ will be **reduced**. This is physically correct: if the process gain is too high, the closed-loop response overshoots the reference model (positive $e_m = y_p - y_m$), and reducing $K_c$ will slow the response and bring $e_m$ back towards zero. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign of error** — Students often confuse $e_m = y_p - y_m$ with $y_m - y_p$. This **flips the sign** of the update law. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 2. **Units of $\gamma$** — In the *continuous* MIT rule, $\gamma$ is in s⁻¹. In the *discrete* form, students must multiply by $T_s$ or the dimensions are inconsistent. KTU may deduct 1 mark for missing $T_s$.
> 3. **Initial covariance $P(0)$** — Forgetting to set $P(0) = \alpha I$ (with $\alpha$ large) leads to no initial learning. (–1 mark)
> 4. **MRAC vs STR confusion** — When asked for "advantages of MRAC", students sometimes describe STR features. Read the question carefully!
> 5. **Forgetting factor limit** — A common error: choosing $\lambda = 0.5$ for a slowly varying process. This causes noisy estimates. KTU expects $0.95 \le \lambda \le 0.99$ for slow drift.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Adaptive & Self-Tuning Controllers**

- 🎯 **Adaptive control** automatically tunes controller parameters to handle time-varying or uncertain process dynamics.
- 🎯 The three pillars are: **gain scheduling, MRAC, and STR**.
- 🎯 **Gain scheduling** is *not* true closed-loop adaptation; it uses an auxiliary variable.
- 🎯 **MRAC** uses a *reference model* and the **MIT rule** for adaptation: $\dot{\theta} = -\gamma e_m (\partial e_m / \partial \theta)$.
- 🎯 The **normalised MIT rule** adds $\alpha$ in the denominator for robustness.
- 🎯 **STR** performs **identification (RLS) + design + implementation** at every sample.
- 🎯 The **RLS update** is $\hat{\theta}(k) = \hat{\theta}(k-1) + K(k)[y(k) - \phi^T(k)\hat{\theta}(k-1)]$.
- 🎯 The **covariance update** uses the **forgetting factor $\lambda$** ($0.95 \le \lambda \le 0.99$).
- 🎯 The **ARMAX / ARX** discrete model is the foundation of self-tuning regulators.
- 🎯 **Minimum-variance control** minimises $E[y^2(k+d)]$ using $u(k) = -G/BF \cdot y(k)$.
- 🎯 **Pole-placement self-tuning** assigns closed-loop poles via a target polynomial.
- 🎯 Industrial applications include **pH control, distillation, polymerisation reactors, paper machines, aircraft autopilots, and bioreactors**.
- 🎯 KTU exam focus: derivations of MIT rule and RLS, block diagrams, and comparison tables.
- 🎯 Always carry the **sampling period $T_s$** when using the MIT rule in discrete time.
- 🎯 Initial covariance $P(0) = \alpha I$ with $\alpha$ large ensures fast initial learning.
- 🎯 Lyapunov stability theory is the rigorous basis for proving MRAC convergence.
- 🎯 Distinguish *adaptation law* (MIT / Lyapunov) from *identifier* (RLS / ELS / Kalman filter).
- 🎯 The **separation principle** does *not* generally hold for adaptive control — closed-loop stability is not guaranteed by separate stability of identifier and controller.
- 🎯 Practical safeguards: **dead zone**, **parameter projection**, **$\sigma$-modification** — prevent parameter drift in the presence of unmodelled dynamics.
- 🎯 Commercial STCs (e.g., ABB, Honeywell, Emerson) embed the RLS + pole-placement pipeline with a fixed structure for safety certification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Control of Process Plants - Centralised Control System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET502 - PROCESS DYNAMICS AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Computer Control of Process Plants - Centralised Control System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **Computer Control of Process Plant** refers to the use of a digital computer as the central regulatory and supervisory element to monitor, acquire data, and manipulate process variables (PVs) in real time, replacing or augmenting analog controllers. A **Centralised Control System (CCS)** is the architectural configuration in which **a single, high-capacity digital computer** is responsible for executing all measurement scanning, control algorithms, data logging, alarm management, and operator-interface functions for the **entire** plant or a large section of it.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the centralised computer is part of the **upper level of the automation hierarchy** (Levels 3–4 of the ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model), sitting above dedicated field controllers, smart transmitters, and final control elements (valves, drives).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCAET502 – Module 4):** The emphasis is on the *architecture*, *functions*, *merits/demerits*, and *evolutionary context* of centralised control — i.e., understanding *why* industry migrated to Distributed Control Systems (DCS). Expect a 7-mark question in Part B framing this comparison.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **single head chef** in a large restaurant who is responsible for tasting every dish, adjusting every spice, plating every plate, and simultaneously taking customer orders and managing inventory. He is the only "controller" in the kitchen. 
- If the chef is brilliant, the restaurant runs smoothly. 
- If he is slow, sick, or makes one mistake, the **entire** kitchen collapses. 
- Every waiter must physically run to him for every decision.

The **Centralised Control System** is exactly this "single-chef" model, where one mainframe/DCS-supervisor computer receives signals from **every** sensor, runs **every** control loop, and sends commands to **every** valve. In contrast, modern Distributed Control Systems (DCS) give each cooking station its own sous-chef — that is the migration path we will explore in the next module.

### 1.3 Core Measurable Parameters
The performance of a CCS is typically benchmarked using:
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — typically **≥ 50,000 hours** for a robust industrial mainframe.
- **Scan Time / Cycle Time (T_c)** — typically **0.1 s to 1 s** for regulatory control; **1 s to 10 s** for supervisory control.
- **Number of Control Loops handled** — historically 100–500 loops per central computer.
- **Redundancy Ratio** — critical loops are backed up at **1:1** or **2:1** hot-standby.

> [!NOTE]
> A *control loop* is one complete feedback path: Measurement (sensor) → Comparison (SP − PV) → Control Action (PID) → Final Element (valve).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

**Step 1 — Signal Acquisition (Input Stage)**
Multiplexed analog signals from field sensors (temperature, pressure, flow, level) are routed through a **Process I/O Subsystem** consisting of:
- **Signal Conditioning** (amplification, isolation, linearization of RTD/thermocouple signals)
- **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** with typical resolution of **12-bit to 16-bit**
- **Digital Filtering** (e.g., first-order low-pass with time constant τ_f) to suppress high-frequency noise before the algorithm sees the PV.

**Step 2 — Decision Making (CPU Stage)**
The central processor executes the **Discrete-Time PID Algorithm** for every loop in a round-robin or priority-based scan. Because the computer is sequential, it cannot update all loops truly *simultaneously*; therefore, the **scan period T_s** (the time between two successive updates of the *same* loop) is critical.

**Step 3 — Signal Output (Output Stage)**
The control action computed (often held constant between scans via a **Zero-Order Hold, ZOH**) is converted back to analog (DAC) and sent to the final control element.

**Step 4 — Operator Interface (HMI / MMI Stage)**
- **Man-Machine Interface (MMI)** — Mimic panels, CRT/LCD screens showing trends, alarms, setpoints.
- **Data Logging** — Historical trending on magnetic/SSD storage.
- **Alarm Management** — High/Low trip handling with acknowledgement logic.

**Step 5 — Supervisory Optimisation (Optional Upper Layer)**
Setpoint optimisation, model-predictive control, production scheduling, and reporting to a plant management information system (MIS).

### 2.2 Why Use a Computer? (Engineering Motivation)
- Ability to implement **non-linear, adaptive, and multi-variable** control strategies impossible with analog hardware.
- Easy **re-tuning** by changing software constants — no rewiring.
- **Centralised data** for trending, quality control, and statutory reporting.
- Easy **cascading and feed-forward** of multiple variables.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Equation / Definition | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|----------------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Discrete PID (Position Form)** | $u(k) = K_c\!\left[e(k) + \dfrac{T_s}{T_i}\!\sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i) + \dfrac{T_d}{T_s}\!\left(e(k)-e(k-1)\right)\right]$ | Engineering units (e.g., %, mA) | Position algorithm — full output computed each scan. |
| 2 | **Velocity / Incremental PID** | $\Delta u(k) = K_c\!\left[\Delta e(k) + \dfrac{T_s}{T_i} e(k) + \dfrac{T_d}{T_s}\!\left(\Delta e(k)-\Delta e(k-1)\right)\right]$ | % per scan | Bumpless transfer; output is the *change*, not the absolute value. |
| 3 | **Anti-Alias Filter Cut-off** | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2 T_s}$ | Hz | Nyquist criterion — must filter frequencies above $1/(2T_s)$. |
| 4 | **Scan Period Selection (Ziegler-Nichols Rule)** | $T_s \approx 0.1\,T_p$ where $T_p$ is the dominant process time constant | seconds | Standard empirical choice for regulatory loops. |
| 5 | **Hold Calculation (ZOH)** | $u(t) = u(kT_s)$ for $kT_s \le t < (k+1)T_s$ | — | Output held constant between updates. |
| 6 | **Throughput Requirement** | $\text{Loops} = \dfrac{\text{CPU time per loop}}{\text{Total CPU budget}}$ | dimensionless | Centralised CPU must finish all loops inside $T_s$. |
| 7 | **MTBF of Duplex System** | $\text{MTBF}_{\text{system}} \approx \dfrac{\text{MTBF}_1 \cdot \text{MTBF}_2}{\text{MTTR}_1 + \text{MTTR}_2}$ | hours | Justifies redundant central computers. |

> [!NOTE]
> When the central CPU has $N$ loops each requiring $\tau_i$ seconds of execution, the system is feasible **only if** $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \tau_i \le T_s$. This is the single biggest engineering constraint of a CCS.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Power Plants:** A single supervisory computer monitors boiler drum level, superheat temperature, and turbine speed simultaneously, with hardwired analog backups.
- **Petrochemical Refineries (1970s–80s):** Pioneered centralised control for catalytic crackers; a single mainframe handled ~200 loops.
- **Steel Mills:** Reheat furnace temperature centralised control.
- **Aerospace Test Stands:** Centralised data acquisition of thousands of thermocouples/pressure transducers.
- **Legacy pharmaceutical batch plants:** Centralised recipe management.

### 2.5 Merits and Demerits of Centralised Control

| Merits | Demerits |
|--------|----------|
| Single database → easy plant-wide reporting | **Single Point of Failure** — one CPU crash halts entire plant |
| Lower installation cost (one computer, no field controllers) | Large cable runs from field to central control room |
| Complex control (MPC, optimisation) easy to implement | Scan-time grows with number of loops; control quality degrades |
| Easier engineering change via software | Maintenance requires specialist (often vendor-locked) |
| Centralised engineering, commissioning, and documentation | Risk of common-mode software bugs affecting the whole plant |
| Plant-wide coordination of setpoints | Long-distance analog signal transmission is noise-prone |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Discrete-Time PID Position Algorithm from Continuous PID

**Starting Point — Continuous-Time PID**
The ideal analog PID controller, established in Module 2 of PCAET502, is:

$$u(t) = K_c\!\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\!\int_0^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}\right]$$

**Step 1 — Discretise the Integral Term**
Using a rectangular (Euler-forward) approximation of the integral over the scan period $T_s$:

$$\int_0^{kT_s} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{i=0}^{k} e(iT_s)$$

We define $e(i) \equiv e(iT_s)$ to simplify notation. The integral becomes $T_s \sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i)$.

**Step 2 — Discretise the Derivative Term**
Using a backward-difference approximation of the derivative at sample $k$:

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg|_{t=kT_s} \;\approx\; \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Back into the Continuous PID**
Combining the three terms in the bracket:

$$u(k) = K_c\!\left[ e(k) + \frac{1}{T_i}\,T_s \sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i) + T_d \cdot \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s} \right]$$

Rearranging to group constants cleanly:

$$u(k) = K_c\, e(k) + \frac{K_c T_s}{T_i}\sum_{i=0}^{k} e(i) + \frac{K_c T_d}{T_s}\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr]$$

This is the **Position Form** of the discrete PID. The output $u(k)$ is the *absolute* controller output (e.g., valve position in %).

**Step 4 — Convert to Velocity (Incremental) Form**
Subtract the previous sample $u(k-1)$ from $u(k)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
u(k) - u(k-1) \;=\;& \Delta u(k) \\
=\;& K_c\bigl[e(k) - e(k-1)\bigr] + \frac{K_c T_s}{T_i}\,e(k) \\
& + \frac{K_c T_d}{T_s}\bigl[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)\bigr]
\end{aligned}$$

Factoring $K_c$:

$$\Delta u(k) = K_c\!\left[\Delta e(k) + \frac{T_s}{T_i} e(k) + \frac{T_d}{T_s}\bigl(\Delta e(k) - \Delta e(k-1)\bigr)\right]$$

where $\Delta e(k) = e(k) - e(k-1)$. This is the **velocity form** widely used in centralised digital controllers because it offers **bumpless transfer** during mode switches (auto → manual) and is less sensitive to output saturation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The position form is *integrating* in nature (sum grows unbounded), so a sensor failure (e.g., $e$ stuck at 0) does not freeze the output — it keeps integrating. The velocity form is *self-correcting*: if $e$ is constant for a long time, $\Delta e = 0$ and the integral term still updates only by $K_c T_s e / T_i$, preventing wind-up *if* anti-windup is properly implemented.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Scan Period

The scan period $T_s$ governs the achievable closed-loop bandwidth. A common derivation comes from the **Z-transform** of a first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT) plant, but a simpler engineering rule is:

$$T_s \le \frac{T_p}{10}$$

where $T_p$ is the dominant open-loop time constant of the process. For a plant with $T_p = 100\,\text{s}$, this gives $T_s \le 10\,\text{s}$. For a fast flow loop with $T_p = 5\,\text{s}$, $T_s \le 0.5\,\text{s}$.

A central computer handling 200 loops at $T_s = 1\,\text{s}$ has a **CPU budget of 5 ms per loop**, which was the practical limit of 1970s–80s minicomputer technology and is the historical reason centralised control topped out at a few hundred loops.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A centralised computer scans 250 loops. Each loop requires 3.0 ms of CPU time. The plant has a dominant time constant of 8 s. Determine whether the scan period satisfies Ziegler-Nichols rule and compute the maximum permissible scan time.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total CPU time per scan:**
$$\text{Total CPU time} = 250 \times 3.0\,\text{ms} = 750\,\text{ms} = 0.75\,\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Achievable scan period:**
Since the CPU must finish all 250 loops before revisiting the first, the scan period is at least equal to the total CPU time:
$$T_s^{\min} = 0.75\,\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Ziegler-Nichols recommended scan period:**
$$T_s^{\text{rec}} \approx 0.1 \times T_p = 0.1 \times 8 = 0.8\,\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**
$$T_s^{\min} = 0.75\,\text{s} \le T_s^{\text{rec}} = 0.8\,\text{s} \;\;\checkmark$$

The configuration is **just feasible**, with a 50 ms margin for overhead (interrupts, operator displays, alarm processing). Any additional loops would push the central CPU past its real-time deadline.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Conflating "scan period" with "CPU time per loop." They differ by a factor equal to the number of loops. Always compute total CPU time first.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Centralised Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    field1[Field Sensors: TT PT FT LT] --> mux[Analog Multiplexer and Signal Conditioner]
    mux --> adc[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    adc --> cpu[(Central Computer CPU)]
    cpu --> mem1[(Process Database)]
    mem1 --> cpu
    cpu --> hmi1[Operator MMI HMI Console]
    cpu --> hmi2[Engineer Workstation]
    cpu --> dac[Digital to Analog Converter DAC]
    dac --> hold[Zero Order Hold ZOH]
    hold --> fce[Final Control Elements Valves and Drives]
    fce --> plant[Chemical Process Plant]
    plant --> field1
    cpu --> alarm[Alarm and Event Logger]
    cpu --> opt[Supervisory Optimisation Layer]
    opt --> cpu
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Central Computer CPU** is the single regulatory brain.
- It communicates with field sensors through a **multiplexer + ADC** and with valves through a **DAC + ZOH**.
- **Operator MMI** and **Engineer Workstation** allow human interaction.
- **Alarm/Event Logger** handles high/low trips.
- The **Supervisory Optimisation Layer** runs setpoint optimisation and feeds it back to the CPU.

### 4.2 ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model — Where the CCS Sits

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["LEVEL 4: Enterprise Resource Planning ERP"] --> L3["LEVEL 3: Manufacturing Operations Management MOM"]
    L3 --> L2["LEVEL 2: Supervisory Control SCADA HMI Centralised Computer"]
    L2 --> L1["LEVEL 1: Local Controllers and Smart Instruments"]
    L1 --> L0["LEVEL 0: Field Sensors and Final Elements"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a **Centralised Control System**, the **Central Computer** corresponds to **Level 2 (Supervisory Control)**, and it directly drives **Level 1** (analog single-loop controllers or RTUs) which in turn drive **Level 0** (valves and transmitters). The absence of dedicated, autonomous loop controllers is what defines the CCS architecture.

### 4.3 Information Flow During a Single Scan Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tick kT_s Starts] --> B[Read all 250 sensor values]
    B --> C[Apply digital filter to PV]
    C --> D[Compute error e equals SP minus PV for all loops]
    D --> E[Run PID algorithm for all loops]
    E --> F[Update Zero Order Hold outputs]
    F --> G[Send new valve commands via DAC]
    G --> H[Refresh operator screens and trends]
    H --> I[Check alarm thresholds]
    I --> J[Increment k by 1 and loop back to A]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper]**
Define a Centralised Control System. Mention any two of its salient features.

**Model Answer:**
A Centralised Control System is a process-control architecture in which a single, high-capacity digital computer is responsible for the measurement scanning, control algorithm execution, data logging, alarm handling, and operator-interface functions for an entire process plant or a large section of it.
**Salient features:**
1. A single CPU serves as the regulatory brain for all control loops.
2. A plant-wide shared database simplifies trending, reporting, and engineering changes.
3. Complex multivariable and supervisory optimisation routines are implemented centrally in software.
*(Any two — 1 mark definition + 2 marks features.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]**
State the difference between Centralised and Distributed Control Systems.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Centralised | Distributed |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| Number of controllers | One central computer | Many local controllers |
| Single Point of Failure | Yes | No (redundant) |
| Cabling | Long runs to central room | Short local loops |
| Scalability | Limited (~200 loops) | High (>1000 loops) |
| Engineering | Vendor-dependent | Distributed engineering |

*(3 marks — table form acceptable; 1 mark for each valid comparative point.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Centralised Computer Control System. Identify each block and state its function.

**(b) [7 Marks]** List the merits and demerits of a Centralised Control System. Explain why Distributed Control Systems replaced Centralised systems in modern plants.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture [7 Marks]**

The standard architecture of a CCS consists of the following interconnected blocks:

1. **Field Section (Sensors & Actuators):** Temperature transmitters (TT), Pressure transmitters (PT), Flow transmitters (FT), Level transmitters (LT) and final control elements such as pneumatic/hydraulic control valves and variable-speed drives.

2. **Signal Conditioning & Multiplexer Block:** Amplifies, isolates, and linearises low-level signals (e.g., 4–20 mA, RTD, thermocouple) and routes them sequentially to a single ADC for digitisation.

3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Converts the conditioned analog signals to digital words. Resolution is typically 12–16 bits; the conversion time sets the lower bound on the scan period.

4. **Central Computer (CPU):** The brain of the system. It executes:
   - Digital filtering of measurements
   - Error computation $e(k) = SP(k) - PV(k)$
   - Discrete PID control algorithm
   - Alarm limit checking
   - Communication with operator stations and engineering workstations

5. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) and Zero-Order Hold (ZOH):** Converts the digital controller output to an analog signal held constant between scans to drive the valve positioner.

6. **Operator Man-Machine Interface (MMI):** CRT/LCD mimic displays, trend recorders, alarm annunciators, setpoint entry keyboards.

7. **Engineer Workstation:** Used for configuration, tuning, and download of control strategies.

8. **Data Storage:** Hard disk / SSD for historical data, recipe management, and event logging.

9. **Communication Network (Optional):** Highway (bus) to connect the central computer to remote I/O cabinets and engineering stations.

10. **Supervisory Optimisation Layer (Optional):** Setpoint scheduling, model-based optimisation, production reporting.

*Block diagram as drawn in Section 4.1 carries 3 marks; correct identification and function of each block: 4 marks.*

**(b) Merits, Demerits and Migration to DCS [7 Marks]**

**Merits:**
- Single, plant-wide database; easy reporting.
- Complex algorithms (MPC, adaptive, non-linear) easy to implement.
- Lower front-end hardware cost (one computer replaces hundreds of analog controllers).
- Easy retuning through software constants.
- Centralised engineering and documentation.

**Demerits (reasons for migration to DCS):**
- **Single Point of Failure** — one CPU crash can shut down the entire plant. The cost of a 1-hour shutdown of a refinery can exceed the cost of a fully redundant DCS.
- **Long cable runs** from field to central room cause noise pickup, voltage drops, and high installation cost.
- **Scan time degradation** as the number of loops grows. A 1000-loop centralised system would need an impractically fast CPU.
- **Common-mode software bugs** can affect all loops simultaneously.
- **Difficult expansion** — adding a new section requires rerunning cables back to the central room and reprogramming the central computer.
- **Maintenance** requires highly specialised staff, often vendor-locked.

**Why DCS replaced CCS:**
DCS introduced **autonomous local controllers** (one per loop or group of loops) connected by a high-speed communication highway. This:
1. Eliminates the single point of failure.
2. Shortens cabling dramatically.
3. Allows true parallel processing of loops.
4. Permits incremental expansion (add controllers, not a new mainframe).
5. Distributes risk — a local controller failure affects only a small section.

*(Merits: 2 marks; Demerits with reasons: 3 marks; Justification of migration to DCS: 2 marks.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the discrete-time **Velocity (Incremental) Form** of the PID algorithm from the continuous-time PID equation. Explain why the velocity form is preferred in Centralised Computer Control Systems.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A central computer handles 300 control loops. Each loop requires 2.5 ms of CPU time. The dominant process time constant is 6 s. Determine (i) the minimum scan period, (ii) the Ziegler-Nichols recommended scan period, and (iii) whether the configuration is feasible. Suggest a remedial action if not.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Velocity PID [7 Marks]**

See Section 3.1, Steps 1–4 of these notes. Final result:

$$\Delta u(k) = K_c\!\left[\Delta e(k) + \frac{T_s}{T_i} e(k) + \frac{T_d}{T_s}\bigl(\Delta e(k) - \Delta e(k-1)\bigr)\right]$$

**Why velocity form is preferred in CCS:**
1. **Bumpless transfer:** When the operator switches from MANUAL to AUTO, the velocity form starts from zero incremental output, avoiding large bumps to the valve.
2. **Anti-windup friendly:** Since the output is a *change*, output saturation can be implemented easily by clamping $\Delta u(k)$.
3. **Initialisation simplicity:** Only $\Delta e(k-1)$ and $e(k-1)$ need to be stored; no need for a running sum (the integral history is implicit in the previous output $u(k-1)$ held in the ZOH).
4. **Fail-safe behaviour:** If the computer crashes and restarts, the last computed $u(k-1)$ is held by the ZOH, and the new computer picks up by computing only incremental corrections.

*(Derivation: 4 marks; Justification: 3 marks.)*

**(b) Numerical Problem [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $N = 300$ loops; $\tau = 2.5\,\text{ms}$ per loop; $T_p = 6\,\text{s}$.

**(i) Minimum scan period:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_s^{\min} &= N \times \tau \\
&= 300 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} \\
&= 0.75\,\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Ziegler-Nichols recommended scan period:**

$$T_s^{\text{rec}} = 0.1 \times T_p = 0.1 \times 6 = 0.6\,\text{s}$$
